Spring Boot applications commonly use @ControllerAdvice or @RestControllerAdvice to centralize exception handling and return standardized API error responses.

4. Interview Tips

  • Never leave catch blocks empty.
  • Prefer centralized exception handling.
  • Always log unexpected exceptions.

5. Follow-up Questions

  • What is @ControllerAdvice?
  • How do you handle exceptions globally in Spring Boot?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Good exception handling involves catching specific exceptions, using custom exceptions for business rules, logging meaningful information, and avoiding empty catch blocks. In Spring Boot, I prefer centralized exception handling using @RestControllerAdvice and try-with-resources for resource management.

subtopics.name: Exception Propagation
subtopics.content:
1. What is Exception Propagation?

Exception propagation is the process by which an exception moves up the method call stack until it is caught by a matching catch block.

2. Example

void method3() {
    int x = 10 / 0;
}

void method2() {
    method3();
}

void method1() {
    method2();
}

If none of these methods handle the exception, it propagates to the caller until it reaches the JVM.

3. Why is it useful?

  • Avoids duplicate handling.
  • Allows centralized exception management.
  • Improves code maintainability.

4. Real Project Usage

In Spring Boot, service-layer exceptions are often propagated to the controller layer, where a global exception handler converts them into appropriate HTTP responses.

5. Interview Tips

  • Unchecked exceptions propagate automatically.
  • Checked exceptions must be caught or declared using throws.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can checked exceptions propagate?
  • How does Spring Boot handle propagated exceptions?

Interview Answer (40 sec)

Exception propagation is the process of passing an exception from one method to its caller until it is handled. This enables centralized exception handling and reduces duplicate code. Checked exceptions must be declared or caught, while unchecked exceptions propagate automatically.

subtopics.name: Suppressed Exceptions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Suppressed Exceptions?

Suppressed exceptions occur when one exception is thrown in the try block and another exception occurs while closing a resource. Java preserves the secondary exception as a suppressed exception instead of discarding it.

2. Why were they introduced?

Before Java 7, exceptions thrown while closing resources could hide the original exception. Try-with-resources automatically preserves both exceptions.

3. Example

try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("file.txt"))) {
    throw new RuntimeException("Main Exception");
}

If closing the BufferedReader also throws an exception, it becomes a suppressed exception.

4. How to Access?

catch (Exception e) {
    Throwable[] suppressed = e.getSuppressed();
}

5. Real Project Usage

Suppressed exceptions are primarily encountered when using try-with-resources to manage files, database connections, sockets, and streams.

6. Interview Tips

  • Suppressed exceptions are mainly associated with try-with-resources.
  • Use getSuppressed() to retrieve them.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What is try-with-resources?
  • How does AutoCloseable work?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Suppressed exceptions occur when an exception is thrown while another exception is already being processed, typically during resource cleanup in try-with-resources. Java preserves these secondary exceptions instead of losing them, and they can be accessed using the getSuppressed() method.

subtopics.name: JVM, JDK, JRE
subtopics.content:
1. What is JDK?

JDK (Java Development Kit) is a complete development package used to develop, compile, debug, and run Java applications.

  • Contains JRE.
  • Contains development tools like javac, javadoc, jdb, and jar.

2. What is JRE?

JRE (Java Runtime Environment) provides the environment required to run Java applications.

  • Contains JVM.
  • Contains Java libraries and runtime files.

3. What is JVM?

JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the runtime engine responsible for loading, verifying, and executing Java bytecode. It also manages memory, garbage collection, and security.

4. Relationship

JDK
 ├── JRE
 │    ├── JVM

5. Responsibilities of JVM

  • Class Loading
  • Bytecode Verification
  • Memory Management
  • Garbage Collection
  • JIT Compilation

6. Real Project Usage

Developers use the JDK to build applications, while production servers require only the JRE (or the appropriate Java runtime). The JVM executes Spring Boot applications and manages memory automatically.

7. Interview Tips

  • JDK = Development.
  • JRE = Runtime.
  • JVM = Execution Engine.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What is JIT Compiler?
  • How does JVM achieve platform independence?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

JDK is the complete Java development kit containing tools to develop and compile Java applications. JRE provides the runtime environment required to execute Java programs, while JVM is the execution engine that loads, verifies, and runs bytecode. JVM also manages memory, performs garbage collection, and enables Java's platform independence.

subtopics.name: Heap vs Stack
subtopics.content:
1. Heap Memory

Heap memory stores objects and instance variables. It is shared among all threads and managed by the Garbage Collector.

2. Stack Memory

Each thread has its own Stack memory, which stores local variables, method calls, and references to objects.

3. Comparison

FeatureHeapStack
StoresObjectsLocal Variables & Method Frames
SharedYesNo
Managed ByGarbage CollectorJVM
LifetimeUntil unreachableMethod execution

4. Real Project Usage

Large object creation affects Heap memory, while deep recursion or excessive method calls may cause StackOverflowError.

5. Interview Tips

  • Objects live in Heap.
  • Method execution happens on Stack.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • What is StackOverflowError?
  • What is OutOfMemoryError?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Heap stores objects and is shared across all threads, while Stack stores local variables and method frames for each thread. Heap is managed by the Garbage Collector, whereas Stack memory is automatically released when a method finishes execution.

subtopics.name: Memory Leaks
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Memory Leak?

A memory leak occurs when objects are no longer needed by the application but are still referenced, preventing the Garbage Collector from reclaiming their memory.

2. Common Causes

  • Static collections holding references.
  • Unclosed resources.
  • Listener or callback registrations not removed.
  • Caching objects indefinitely.
  • ThreadLocal variables not cleared.

3. Symptoms

  • Increasing Heap usage.
  • Frequent Garbage Collection.
  • OutOfMemoryError.
  • Poor application performance.

4. Detection Tools

  • VisualVM
  • Java Flight Recorder (JFR)
  • JConsole
  • Eclipse MAT

5. Real Project Usage

Long-running Spring Boot services should avoid retaining unnecessary references in caches or static collections. Monitoring Heap usage helps detect memory leaks early.

6. Interview Tips

  • Java can still have memory leaks despite Garbage Collection.
  • Leaks occur due to live references, not because GC fails.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • How do you identify a memory leak?
  • Can Garbage Collection prevent all leaks?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

A memory leak occurs when objects remain reachable through references even though the application no longer needs them. Because the Garbage Collector only removes unreachable objects, these retained references cause increasing Heap usage and can eventually lead to OutOfMemoryError. Common causes include static collections, improper caching, and unclosed resources.

subtopics.name: Garbage Collection
subtopics.content:
1. What is Garbage Collection?

Garbage Collection (GC) is the JVM process of automatically identifying and removing objects that are no longer reachable, freeing Heap memory.

2. Advantages

  • Automatic memory management.
  • Prevents manual memory deallocation.
  • Reduces memory-related bugs.

3. Popular Garbage Collectors

  • Serial GC
  • Parallel GC
  • G1 GC (Default in modern JDKs)
  • ZGC
  • Shenandoah GC

4. How GC Works

  1. Marks reachable objects.
  2. Removes unreachable objects.
  3. Compacts memory (collector-dependent).

5. Real Project Usage

Spring Boot applications rely on the JVM's Garbage Collector to manage object lifecycles. GC logs and monitoring tools are used to identify memory pressure and optimize performance.

6. Interview Tips

  • GC works only on Heap memory.
  • GC is automatic but not guaranteed to run immediately after System.gc().

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What is G1 GC?
  • What is Stop-The-World?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Garbage Collection is the JVM's automatic memory management process that removes unreachable objects from Heap memory. It improves application reliability by eliminating manual memory management. Modern JVMs use collectors such as G1 GC, ZGC, and Shenandoah to balance throughput and pause times.

subtopics.name: Young vs Old Generation
subtopics.content:
1. JVM Heap Generations

The JVM divides Heap memory into generations to optimize Garbage Collection.

2. Young Generation

  • Stores newly created objects.
  • Contains Eden Space and Survivor Spaces (S0 & S1).
  • Minor GC occurs here frequently.

3. Old Generation

  • Stores long-lived objects promoted from the Young Generation.
  • Major GC or Full GC occurs here.

4. Lifecycle

New Object
     ↓
Eden
     ↓
Survivor (S0/S1)
     ↓
Old Generation

5. Why use Generations?

Most objects die young. Separating short-lived and long-lived objects improves GC efficiency and reduces pause times.

6. Real Project Usage

Applications creating many temporary objects, such as REST APIs and JSON processing, generate frequent Minor GCs. Monitoring promotion rates helps identify memory tuning opportunities.

7. Interview Tips

  • Minor GC = Young Generation.
  • Major/Full GC = Old Generation.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What is Eden Space?
  • What are Survivor Spaces?
  • What is object promotion?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

The JVM divides Heap memory into Young and Old Generations. New objects are created in the Young Generation, which consists of Eden and Survivor spaces. Objects that survive multiple garbage collection cycles are promoted to the Old Generation. This generational approach improves performance because most objects are short-lived and can be collected quickly using Minor GC.

subtopics.name: Metaspace
subtopics.content:
1. What is Metaspace?

Metaspace is the memory area introduced in Java 8 to store class metadata. It replaced the Permanent Generation (PermGen) used in earlier Java versions.

2. What does Metaspace store?

  • Class metadata
  • Method metadata
  • Runtime constant pool
  • Annotations
  • ClassLoader information

3. Why was PermGen replaced?

PermGen had a fixed size, which often caused OutOfMemoryError: PermGen Space. Metaspace uses native memory and can grow dynamically, reducing these issues.

4. Advantages

  • Uses native memory.
  • Dynamically grows when required.
  • Reduces OutOfMemoryError caused by PermGen.

5. Real Project Usage

Applications that dynamically load classes, such as Spring Boot applications using proxies or plugins, consume Metaspace. Excessive dynamic class loading without proper cleanup can still cause Metaspace-related memory issues.

6. Interview Tips

  • PermGen was removed in Java 8.
  • Metaspace uses native memory instead of Heap.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can Metaspace cause OutOfMemoryError?
  • What JVM options configure Metaspace?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Metaspace is the memory area introduced in Java 8 to store class metadata. It replaced PermGen and uses native memory instead of Heap, allowing it to grow dynamically. This reduces memory issues associated with the fixed-size PermGen while improving flexibility for applications that load many classes.

subtopics.name: ClassLoader
subtopics.content:
1. What is ClassLoader?

ClassLoader is a JVM component responsible for loading Java classes into memory at runtime. It loads .class files only when they are required.

2. Types of ClassLoaders

  • Bootstrap ClassLoader - Loads core Java classes (java.lang, java.util, etc.).
  • Platform (Extension) ClassLoader - Loads platform libraries.
  • Application ClassLoader - Loads classes from the application's classpath.

3. Parent Delegation Model

When a class needs to be loaded, the request is first delegated to the parent ClassLoader. Only if the parent cannot load the class does the child attempt to load it.

4. Why Parent Delegation?

  • Prevents duplicate class loading.
  • Improves security.
  • Ensures core Java classes cannot be overridden.

5. Real Project Usage

Spring Boot, application servers, and plugin-based applications use multiple ClassLoaders to isolate applications and dynamically load components.

6. Interview Tips

  • Remember the order: Bootstrap → Platform → Application.
  • Mention Parent Delegation Model.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can we create a custom ClassLoader?
  • Why can't we override java.lang.String?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

ClassLoader is responsible for loading Java classes into memory when required. Java uses three main ClassLoaders: Bootstrap, Platform, and Application ClassLoader. They follow the Parent Delegation Model, which improves security and prevents duplicate loading of core Java classes.

subtopics.name: Reference Types
subtopics.content:
1. Types of References in Java

Java provides four types of references that determine how the Garbage Collector treats objects.

2. Strong Reference

The default reference type. Objects with strong references are never garbage collected while the reference exists.

3. Weak Reference

Objects are eligible for Garbage Collection as soon as they become weakly reachable. Used by WeakHashMap.

4. Soft Reference

Objects are retained until the JVM requires memory. Useful for implementing caches.

5. Phantom Reference

Objects are already finalized and are about to be reclaimed. Mainly used for advanced resource cleanup and memory management.

6. Comparison

ReferenceGC Behavior
StrongNever collected while referenced
SoftCollected only when memory is low
WeakCollected during next GC
PhantomCollected after cleanup notification

7. Real Project Usage

Soft references are useful for memory-sensitive caches, while WeakHashMap uses weak references to automatically remove entries whose keys are no longer referenced.

8. Interview Tips

  • Strong is the default reference.
  • WeakHashMap uses Weak References.

9. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between SoftReference and WeakReference?
  • What is ReferenceQueue?

Interview Answer (55 sec)

Java supports Strong, Soft, Weak, and Phantom references. Strong references prevent objects from being garbage collected. Soft references are retained until memory becomes scarce, making them suitable for caches. Weak references are collected during the next garbage collection cycle and are used by WeakHashMap. Phantom references are used for advanced cleanup after an object becomes unreachable.

subtopics.name: Finalize Deprecation
subtopics.content:
1. What is finalize()?

The finalize() method was provided by the Object class to perform cleanup before an object was garbage collected.

2. Why was it deprecated?

  • Unpredictable execution.
  • No guarantee that it will run.
  • Poor performance.
  • Security concerns.
  • Could delay Garbage Collection.

3. Java Recommendation

Since Java 9, finalize() has been deprecated. Developers should use try-with-resources, AutoCloseable, or Cleaner API instead.

4. Alternatives

  • try-with-resources
  • AutoCloseable
  • Cleaner API

5. Real Project Usage

Modern Spring Boot applications avoid finalize(). Resources such as database connections, files, and sockets are managed using try-with-resources or framework-managed lifecycle methods.

6. Interview Tips

  • Do not recommend finalize().
  • Mention Cleaner API as the modern replacement.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What replaced finalize()?
  • Why is finalize() unreliable?

Interview Answer (40 sec)

The finalize() method was originally intended for cleanup before an object was garbage collected, but it has been deprecated because its execution is unpredictable and can negatively affect performance. Modern Java recommends using try-with-resources, AutoCloseable, or the Cleaner API instead.

subtopics.name: JVM Tuning Basics
subtopics.content:
1. What is JVM Tuning?

JVM tuning is the process of configuring JVM memory and Garbage Collection settings to improve application performance, reduce pause times, and optimize resource utilization.

2. Common JVM Parameters

OptionPurpose
-XmsInitial Heap Size
-XmxMaximum Heap Size
-XX:MaxMetaspaceSizeMaximum Metaspace Size
-XX:+UseG1GCEnable G1 Garbage Collector

3. Monitoring Tools

  • JVisualVM
  • Java Flight Recorder (JFR)
  • JConsole
  • GC Logs

4. Best Practices

  • Monitor Heap usage regularly.
  • Avoid excessive Full GC.
  • Choose an appropriate Garbage Collector.
  • Size Heap based on application workload.

5. Real Project Usage

In production Spring Boot applications, JVM tuning often involves adjusting Heap size, selecting G1 GC, enabling GC logs, and monitoring memory usage to reduce latency and improve throughput.

6. Interview Tips

  • Do not recommend tuning without monitoring.
  • Always mention GC logs and profiling tools.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • When would you increase -Xmx?
  • How do you identify GC issues?
  • Why is G1 GC the default?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

JVM tuning involves optimizing Heap size, Metaspace, and Garbage Collection settings to improve application performance. Common parameters include -Xms for initial Heap size, -Xmx for maximum Heap size, and G1 GC for efficient memory management. Before tuning, I always monitor the application using tools like VisualVM, Java Flight Recorder, and GC logs to identify actual bottlenecks.

subtopics.name: Lambda Expressions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Lambda Expressions?

Lambda Expressions were introduced in Java 8 to provide a concise way of implementing functional interfaces without creating anonymous inner classes.

2. Syntax

(parameters) -> expression

(parameters) -> {
    // multiple statements
}

3. Example

// Before Java 8
Runnable r = new Runnable() {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Hello");
    }
};

// Java 8
Runnable r = () -> System.out.println("Hello");

4. Advantages

  • Less boilerplate code.
  • Improves readability.
  • Works seamlessly with Streams API.
  • Encourages functional programming.

5. Real Project Usage

Lambda expressions are extensively used in Spring Boot applications with Streams, collection processing, sorting, asynchronous programming, and functional interfaces such as Predicate and Consumer.

6. Interview Tips

  • Lambda works only with Functional Interfaces.
  • It replaces anonymous inner classes in many scenarios.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can Lambda access local variables?
  • What is an effectively final variable?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Lambda expressions, introduced in Java 8, provide a concise way to implement functional interfaces. They reduce boilerplate code by replacing anonymous inner classes and are widely used with Streams, collection processing, and asynchronous programming. A lambda can only be used with a functional interface, which contains exactly one abstract method.

subtopics.name: Functional Interfaces
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Functional Interface?

A Functional Interface is an interface that contains exactly one abstract method. It can have multiple default and static methods.

2. @FunctionalInterface

The @FunctionalInterface annotation ensures that the interface contains only one abstract method.

3. Example

@FunctionalInterface
interface Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b);
}

4. Common Functional Interfaces

  • Predicate
  • Function
  • Consumer
  • Supplier
  • Runnable
  • Callable
  • Comparator

5. Real Project Usage

Functional interfaces are widely used with Lambda expressions, Streams API, asynchronous programming, event handling, and callback implementations.

6. Interview Tips

  • Exactly one abstract method.
  • Default and static methods are allowed.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can a functional interface have multiple default methods?
  • Is Runnable a functional interface?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

A Functional Interface contains exactly one abstract method and serves as the target type for lambda expressions. It may also contain any number of default and static methods. Common examples include Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier, Runnable, and Comparator.

subtopics.name: Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier
subtopics.content:
1. Predicate<T>

Represents a condition and returns a boolean.

Predicate<Integer> even = n -> n % 2 == 0;

2. Function<T,R>

Takes one input and returns one output.

Function<String,Integer> length = s -> s.length();

3. Consumer<T>

Consumes input and returns nothing.

Consumer<String> print = System.out::println;

4. Supplier<T>

Supplies data without taking any input.

Supplier<UUID> supplier = UUID::randomUUID;

5. Comparison

InterfaceInputOutput
PredicateYesBoolean
FunctionYesOne Result
ConsumerYesNone
SupplierNoOne Result

6. Real Project Usage

Predicate is commonly used for filtering, Function for object transformation, Consumer for logging or processing, and Supplier for lazy initialization and object creation.

7. Interview Tips

  • Predicate returns boolean.
  • Consumer returns void.
  • Supplier takes no input.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What are BiFunction and BiConsumer?
  • How are these used in Streams?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

Predicate evaluates a condition and returns a boolean. Function transforms one object into another. Consumer accepts an input and performs an operation without returning a value. Supplier provides data without taking any input. These interfaces are heavily used with Lambda expressions and the Streams API.

subtopics.name: Streams API
subtopics.content:
1. What is Streams API?

Streams API, introduced in Java 8, provides a functional approach to processing collections without modifying the original data.

2. Stream Pipeline

Source → Intermediate Operations → Terminal Operation

3. Common Intermediate Operations

  • filter()
  • map()
  • sorted()
  • distinct()
  • limit()

4. Common Terminal Operations

  • collect()
  • forEach()
  • count()
  • reduce()
  • findFirst()

5. Example

List<String> names = Arrays.asList("A","B","C");
List<String> result = names.stream()
    .filter(s -> !s.isEmpty())
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

6. Real Project Usage

Streams are widely used for filtering database results, DTO transformations, grouping records, aggregations, sorting, and processing REST API responses.

7. Interview Tips

  • Streams do not modify the original collection.
  • Intermediate operations are lazy.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What is lazy evaluation?
  • Difference between Collection and Stream?

Interview Answer (55 sec)

Streams API provides a functional and declarative way to process collections. A stream pipeline consists of a source, intermediate operations such as filter() and map(), and a terminal operation like collect() or reduce(). Streams improve code readability and are heavily used for filtering, transformation, sorting, and aggregation.

subtopics.name: map vs flatMap
subtopics.content:
1. map()

map() transforms each element into another object while maintaining a one-to-one relationship.

List<String> names = Arrays.asList("java", "spring");
List<String> upper = names.stream()
    .map(String::toUpperCase)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

2. flatMap()

flatMap() transforms each element into a Stream and then flattens all Streams into a single Stream.

List<List<String>> list = ...;
list.stream()
    .flatMap(Collection::stream)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

3. Comparison

Featuremap()flatMap()
TransformationOne-to-OneOne-to-Many
ReturnsObjectFlattened Stream
Nested CollectionsNoYes

4. Real Project Usage

map() is commonly used for DTO conversion, while flatMap() is used for flattening nested collections such as lists of orders containing lists of products.

5. Interview Tips

  • map() transforms.
  • flatMap() transforms and flattens.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can flatMap() replace map()?
  • What does Collection::stream do?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

map() performs a one-to-one transformation, converting each element into another object. flatMap() is used when each element itself contains multiple values, such as a collection or stream. It converts each element into a stream and then flattens all streams into a single stream. I use map() for DTO transformations and flatMap() for nested collections.

subtopics.name: filter vs map
subtopics.content:
1. filter()

filter() is used to select elements from a Stream based on a condition. It returns only the elements that satisfy the given Predicate.

List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1,2,3,4,5);
List<Integer> even = numbers.stream()
    .filter(n -> n % 2 == 0)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

2. map()

map() transforms each element into another object without changing the number of elements.

List<String> names = Arrays.asList("java", "spring");
List<String> upper = names.stream()
    .map(String::toUpperCase)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

3. Comparison

Featurefilter()map()
PurposeSelect ElementsTransform Elements
InputPredicateFunction
Output SizeMay DecreaseUsually Same

4. Real Project Usage

filter() is commonly used to select active users, valid records, or specific orders. map() is widely used for DTO conversion, formatting data, and transforming entities into API responses.

5. Interview Tips

  • filter() removes unwanted elements.
  • map() converts elements into another form.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can filter() be used after map()?
  • Difference between map() and flatMap()?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

filter() is used to select elements that satisfy a condition, while map() transforms each element into another object. filter() can reduce the number of elements in the stream, whereas map() generally preserves the number of elements by converting them into a different representation.

subtopics.name: reduce()
subtopics.content:
1. What is reduce()?

reduce() is a terminal Stream operation used to combine all elements into a single result, such as a sum, product, maximum, minimum, or concatenated string.

2. Example

List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1,2,3,4,5);
int sum = numbers.stream()
    .reduce(0, Integer::sum);

Output: 15

3. Syntax

stream.reduce(identity, accumulator)
  • Identity - Initial value.
  • Accumulator - Combines previous result with the next element.

4. Common Uses

  • Sum
  • Maximum
  • Minimum
  • String concatenation
  • Custom aggregations

5. Real Project Usage

reduce() is frequently used for calculating total order amounts, invoice totals, salaries, discounts, and other aggregated business values.

6. Interview Tips

  • reduce() returns a single value.
  • It is a terminal operation.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between reduce() and collect()?
  • Can reduce() return Optional?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

reduce() is a terminal Stream operation used to combine multiple elements into a single result. It is commonly used for operations like calculating sums, maximum values, minimum values, and custom aggregations. It takes an identity value and an accumulator function that combines the elements.

subtopics.name: Optional
subtopics.content:
1. What is Optional?

Optional is a container object introduced in Java 8 to represent the presence or absence of a value. It helps reduce NullPointerException by encouraging explicit handling of missing values.

2. Creating Optional

Optional.of(value)
Optional.ofNullable(value)
Optional.empty()

3. Common Methods

  • isPresent()
  • ifPresent()
  • orElse()
  • orElseGet()
  • orElseThrow()
  • map()
  • filter()

4. Example

Optional<String> name = Optional.ofNullable(user.getName());
String result = name.orElse("Unknown");

5. Real Project Usage

Optional is commonly used in Spring Data JPA repository methods such as findById() to indicate that a result may or may not exist. It is also useful when dealing with optional configuration values or API responses.

6. Interview Tips

  • Avoid calling get() without checking.
  • Prefer orElseThrow() for mandatory values.
  • Avoid using Optional for entity fields or method parameters.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between orElse() and orElseGet()?
  • Should Optional be used in DTOs?

Interview Answer (55 sec)

Optional is a container introduced in Java 8 to represent a value that may or may not be present. It helps avoid NullPointerException by forcing developers to explicitly handle missing values using methods like orElse(), orElseThrow(), and ifPresent(). In Spring Boot, Optional is commonly returned by repository methods such as findById().

subtopics.name: Method References
subtopics.content:
1. What are Method References?

Method References provide a shorter and cleaner way to refer to an existing method using the :: operator. They are a shorthand form of lambda expressions.

2. Types of Method References

  • Static Method Reference
  • Instance Method of a Particular Object
  • Instance Method of an Arbitrary Object
  • Constructor Reference

3. Examples

System.out::println
String::toUpperCase
Integer::parseInt
ArrayList::new

4. Advantages

  • Cleaner syntax.
  • Improves readability.
  • Avoids unnecessary lambda expressions.

5. Real Project Usage

Method references are frequently used with Streams API for printing, mapping, sorting, parsing values, collecting results, and object creation.

6. Interview Tips

  • Method references can only be used when a lambda simply calls an existing method.
  • Uses the :: operator.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between Lambda and Method Reference?
  • What is a Constructor Reference?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Method References are a shorthand form of lambda expressions that refer directly to existing methods using the :: operator. They improve readability and reduce boilerplate code. Common examples include System.out::println, String::toUpperCase, and ArrayList::new.

subtopics.name: Collectors
subtopics.content:
1. What are Collectors?

Collectors are utility methods provided by the Collectors class to collect Stream elements into various data structures or perform aggregations.

2. Common Collectors

  • toList()
  • toSet()
  • toMap()
  • joining()
  • groupingBy()
  • partitioningBy()
  • counting()
  • mapping()
  • summarizingInt()

3. Example

Map<String, List<Employee>> employeesByDept = employees.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Employee::getDepartment));

4. Common Use Cases

  • Grouping records
  • Creating Maps
  • Removing duplicates
  • Calculating statistics
  • Joining Strings

5. Real Project Usage

Collectors are extensively used in enterprise applications to group orders by customer, calculate sales reports, generate dashboards, convert lists into maps, and prepare API responses.

6. Interview Tips

  • groupingBy() is one of the most frequently asked collectors.
  • Know the difference between groupingBy() and partitioningBy().

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between groupingBy() and partitioningBy()?
  • When would you use toMap()?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

Collectors are utility methods used to collect Stream results into collections or perform aggregations. Common collectors include toList(), toSet(), toMap(), groupingBy(), partitioningBy(), and joining(). In enterprise applications, I frequently use groupingBy() for reporting, toMap() for lookups, and joining() for string aggregation.

subtopics.name: Process vs Thread
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Process?

A Process is an independent program in execution. Each process has its own memory space, resources, and execution environment.

2. What is a Thread?

A Thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads share the same process memory but execute independently.

3. Comparison

FeatureProcessThread
MemorySeparateShared
CommunicationInter-Process Communication (IPC)Shared Memory
Creation CostHighLow
Failure ImpactUsually isolatedMay affect entire process

4. Real Project Usage

A Spring Boot application typically runs as a single process with multiple threads handling incoming HTTP requests simultaneously.

5. Interview Tips

  • Threads share Heap memory.
  • Each thread has its own Stack.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can threads communicate faster than processes?
  • Why are threads considered lightweight?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

A process is an independent program with its own memory space and system resources. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads share the same Heap memory but have separate Stack memory. In Spring Boot, a single application process usually handles multiple client requests using different threads.

subtopics.name: Ways to Create Threads
subtopics.content:
1. Extending Thread Class

class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running...");
    }
}

2. Implementing Runnable

class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running...");
    }
}

3. Implementing Callable

Callable<Integer> task = () -> 100;
Future<Integer> future = executor.submit(task);

4. Which approach is preferred?

In modern Java, Runnable or Callable with ExecutorService is preferred because it supports thread pooling, better resource management, and result handling.

5. Real Project Usage

Spring Boot applications rarely create threads manually. Instead, they use ExecutorService, CompletableFuture, or Spring's @Async support.

6. Interview Tips

  • Avoid extending Thread unless necessary.
  • Prefer ExecutorService in production applications.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Why is ExecutorService preferred?
  • Difference between Runnable and Callable?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Threads can be created by extending the Thread class, implementing Runnable, or implementing Callable. In enterprise applications, Runnable or Callable together with ExecutorService is preferred because it supports thread pools, efficient resource management, and asynchronous execution.

subtopics.name: Runnable vs Callable
subtopics.content:
1. Runnable

Runnable represents a task that does not return a result and cannot throw checked exceptions.

Runnable task = () -> System.out.println("Hello");

2. Callable

Callable represents a task that returns a result and can throw checked exceptions.

Callable<Integer> task = () -> 100;

3. Comparison

FeatureRunnableCallable
Return ValueNoYes
Checked ExceptionsNoYes
Submit ToThread / ExecutorServiceExecutorService
ResultNoneFuture<T>

4. Real Project Usage

Runnable is suitable for background tasks such as logging, while Callable is used for asynchronous operations that return results, such as fetching data from multiple services.

5. Interview Tips

  • Callable returns Future.
  • Runnable cannot return values.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • What is Future?
  • Can Runnable throw RuntimeException?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Runnable is used for tasks that do not return a result and cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable can return a value and throw checked exceptions, with the result accessed through a Future. In enterprise applications, Callable is commonly used for asynchronous service calls and parallel processing.

subtopics.name: synchronized Keyword
subtopics.content:
1. What is synchronized?

The synchronized keyword ensures that only one thread can execute a synchronized block or method on the same object at a time.

2. Types

  • Synchronized Method
  • Synchronized Block
  • Static Synchronized Method (locks the Class object)

3. Example

public synchronized void increment() {
    count++;
}

4. Why use synchronized?

  • Prevents race conditions.
  • Ensures thread safety.
  • Protects shared resources.

5. Real Project Usage

Synchronization is useful when multiple threads modify shared data. However, excessive synchronization can reduce performance, so concurrent utilities like AtomicInteger or ConcurrentHashMap are often preferred.

6. Interview Tips

  • Synchronization provides mutual exclusion.
  • Every object has an intrinsic lock (monitor).

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between synchronized method and block?
  • What is an intrinsic lock?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

The synchronized keyword ensures that only one thread can execute a critical section at a time by acquiring the object's monitor lock. It is used to protect shared resources from race conditions. While synchronization ensures thread safety, it should be used carefully because excessive locking can reduce performance.

subtopics.name: volatile
subtopics.content:
1. What is volatile?

The volatile keyword ensures that changes made to a variable by one thread are immediately visible to all other threads.

2. What does volatile guarantee?

  • Visibility of changes across threads.
  • Prevents instruction reordering around the volatile variable.

3. What does volatile NOT guarantee?

  • Atomicity.
  • Mutual exclusion.

4. Example

private volatile boolean running = true;

If one thread changes running to false, all other threads immediately observe the updated value.

5. volatile vs synchronized

Featurevolatilesynchronized
VisibilityYesYes
AtomicityNoYes
LockingNoYes

6. Real Project Usage

volatile is commonly used for status flags, shutdown signals, and configuration values that are read by multiple threads but do not require compound operations.

7. Interview Tips

  • volatile is not a replacement for synchronized.
  • count++ is not thread-safe even if count is volatile.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • Why is count++ not atomic?
  • When should you choose AtomicInteger instead?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

The volatile keyword guarantees visibility of variable updates across threads and prevents certain instruction reorderings. However, it does not provide atomicity or locking. It is suitable for simple shared flags but not for compound operations such as incrementing a counter, where AtomicInteger or synchronized should be used.

subtopics.name: Race Condition
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Race Condition?

A race condition occurs when multiple threads access and modify shared data simultaneously, causing the final result to depend on the order of execution.

2. Example

count++;

If two threads execute this statement simultaneously, one update may be lost because increment consists of three operations: read, increment, and write.

3. Why does it happen?

  • Shared mutable data.
  • Lack of synchronization.
  • Non-atomic operations.

4. How to prevent it?

  • synchronized
  • AtomicInteger
  • Locks (ReentrantLock)
  • Concurrent Collections

5. Real Project Usage

Race conditions commonly occur when multiple threads update shared counters, caches, account balances, or inventory simultaneously.

6. Interview Tips

  • count++ is not atomic.
  • Visibility and atomicity are different concepts.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • How does synchronized prevent race conditions?
  • Why is AtomicInteger thread-safe?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

A race condition occurs when multiple threads access and modify shared data at the same time without proper synchronization. Since operations like count++ are not atomic, updates can be lost. Race conditions can be prevented using synchronized blocks, AtomicInteger, locks, or concurrent collections depending on the use case.

subtopics.name: Deadlock
subtopics.content:
1. What is Deadlock?

Deadlock is a situation where two or more threads wait indefinitely for each other to release resources, causing none of them to proceed.

2. Example

Thread 1 locks Object A and waits for Object B.
Thread 2 locks Object B and waits for Object A.

3. Four Necessary Conditions

  • Mutual Exclusion
  • Hold and Wait
  • No Preemption
  • Circular Wait

4. How to Prevent Deadlock?

  • Acquire locks in a fixed order.
  • Use lock timeouts.
  • Reduce nested synchronization.
  • Use concurrent utilities where possible.

5. Real Project Usage

Deadlocks can occur when multiple services or threads lock shared resources such as database records, files, or synchronized objects in different orders.

6. Interview Tips

  • Remember the four necessary conditions.
  • Lock ordering is the most common prevention technique.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • How do you detect deadlocks?
  • What tools can identify deadlocked threads?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

Deadlock occurs when two or more threads wait indefinitely for resources held by each other. It happens when the four necessary conditions—mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait—are satisfied. In production systems, deadlocks are commonly avoided by acquiring locks in a consistent order and minimizing nested synchronization.

subtopics.name: ExecutorService
subtopics.content:
1. What is ExecutorService?

ExecutorService is a framework introduced in Java 5 that manages a pool of worker threads and executes submitted tasks efficiently.

2. Why use ExecutorService?

  • Thread reuse.
  • Improved performance.
  • Better resource management.
  • Supports asynchronous execution.

3. Example

ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(5);
executor.submit(() -> System.out.println("Task"));
executor.shutdown();

4. Common Factory Methods

  • newFixedThreadPool()
  • newCachedThreadPool()
  • newSingleThreadExecutor()
  • newScheduledThreadPool()

5. Real Project Usage

ExecutorService is widely used in Spring Boot applications for asynchronous processing, parallel API calls, batch jobs, and background task execution.

6. Interview Tips

  • Always shutdown the ExecutorService.
  • Avoid creating threads manually in enterprise applications.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between execute() and submit()?
  • What happens if shutdown() is not called?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

ExecutorService is a thread pool framework that manages thread creation and reuse, improving performance and resource utilization. Instead of creating threads manually, tasks are submitted to the ExecutorService, which schedules and executes them efficiently. It is widely used for asynchronous processing and concurrent task execution in enterprise applications.

subtopics.name: Future vs CompletableFuture
subtopics.content:
1. What is Future?

Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation. It allows checking whether a task has completed and retrieving its result.

2. What is CompletableFuture?

CompletableFuture extends Future by supporting asynchronous task chaining, callbacks, exception handling, and combining multiple asynchronous operations.

3. Comparison

FeatureFutureCompletableFuture
Asynchronous ResultYesYes
CallbacksNoYes
Task ChainingNoYes
Exception HandlingLimitedRich API
Combining TasksNoYes

4. Real Project Usage

CompletableFuture is widely used to call multiple microservices in parallel, improving overall response time. Future is mainly used for simple asynchronous tasks.

5. Interview Tips

  • CompletableFuture is much more powerful than Future.
  • Know methods like thenApply(), thenCompose(), thenCombine(), and exceptionally().

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between thenApply() and thenCompose()?
  • How do you combine two CompletableFutures?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation but provides limited functionality. CompletableFuture extends Future by supporting callbacks, task chaining, exception handling, and combining multiple asynchronous operations. In modern Spring Boot applications, CompletableFuture is preferred for parallel API calls and asynchronous workflows.

subtopics.name: AtomicInteger
subtopics.content:
1. What is AtomicInteger?

AtomicInteger is a thread-safe class in java.util.concurrent.atomic that performs atomic operations without using synchronized blocks.

2. How does it work?

AtomicInteger uses low-level CPU instructions such as Compare-And-Swap (CAS) to update values atomically.

3. Common Methods

  • incrementAndGet()
  • decrementAndGet()
  • getAndIncrement()
  • compareAndSet()
  • get()
  • set()

4. Why use AtomicInteger?

  • Thread-safe.
  • No explicit locking.
  • Better performance than synchronized for simple counters.

5. Real Project Usage

AtomicInteger is commonly used for request counters, sequence generators, metrics collection, rate limiting, and tracking concurrent operations.

6. Interview Tips

  • AtomicInteger uses CAS.
  • Suitable for simple atomic operations, not complex business logic.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What is CAS?
  • AtomicInteger vs synchronized?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

AtomicInteger is a thread-safe class that performs atomic operations without locking by using the Compare-And-Swap (CAS) algorithm. It is commonly used for counters and sequence numbers because it offers better performance than synchronized for simple increment and update operations.

subtopics.name: Comparable vs Comparator
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Comparable defines the natural ordering of a class through the compareTo() method, implemented inside the class itself. Comparator defines an external, custom ordering through the compare() method, kept outside the class.

2. Comparison Table

ComparableComparator
Package: java.langPackage: java.util
Method: compareTo(T o)Method: compare(T a, T b)
Single, natural orderingMultiple, custom orderings
Modifies the classNo change to the class

3. Example

// Natural order
class Emp implements Comparable<Emp> {
    int id;
    public int compareTo(Emp e) { return Integer.compare(id, e.id); }
}

// Custom order
Comparator<Emp> byName = Comparator.comparing(e -> e.name);
list.sort(byName.reversed());

Use Comparable for a class's default order; use Comparator when you need several different sort orders.

subtopics.name: TreeSet Internals
subtopics.content:
1. What is a TreeSet?

TreeSet is a NavigableSet implementation that keeps elements in sorted order. Internally it is backed by a TreeMap, which is a Red-Black Tree (a self-balancing binary search tree).

2. How it works

  • Elements are stored as keys in the backing TreeMap with a constant dummy value.
  • Ordering is decided by the element's Comparable or a supplied Comparator.
  • The Red-Black tree guarantees O(log n) for add, remove, and contains.

3. Key Points

  • Does not allow null (comparison against null throws NPE).
  • Elements must be mutually comparable, otherwise ClassCastException.
  • Not thread-safe; wrap with Collections.synchronizedSortedSet() or use ConcurrentSkipListSet.
  • Provides navigation methods like first(), last(), ceiling(), floor().

subtopics.name: NavigableMap
subtopics.content:
1. What is NavigableMap?

NavigableMap extends SortedMap and adds navigation methods to find entries relative to a given key. TreeMap is the primary implementation.

2. Key Methods

  • lowerKey(k) — greatest key strictly less than k.
  • floorKey(k) — greatest key ≤ k.
  • ceilingKey(k) — smallest key ≥ k.
  • higherKey(k) — smallest key strictly greater than k.
  • firstEntry() / lastEntry() — extreme entries.
  • descendingMap() — reverse-ordered view.
  • headMap(), tailMap(), subMap() — range views.

3. Example

NavigableMap<Integer,String> map = new TreeMap<>();
map.put(10,"a"); map.put(20,"b"); map.put(30,"c");
map.floorKey(25);   // 20
map.ceilingKey(25); // 30

Useful for range queries, nearest-match lookups, and building things like rate limiters or leaderboards.

subtopics.name: NavigableSet
subtopics.content:
1. What is NavigableSet?

NavigableSet extends SortedSet and provides navigation methods to locate elements relative to a target value. TreeSet is the main implementation.

2. Key Methods

  • lower(e) — greatest element < e.
  • floor(e) — greatest element ≤ e.
  • ceiling(e) — smallest element ≥ e.
  • higher(e) — smallest element > e.
  • pollFirst() / pollLast() — retrieve and remove the extremes.
  • descendingSet() — reverse-ordered view.

3. Example

NavigableSet<Integer> set = new TreeSet<>(List.of(10,20,30));
set.ceiling(25); // 30
set.floor(25);   // 20
set.pollFirst(); // 10 (removed)

It is essentially the set analogue of NavigableMap, ideal for finding nearest neighbors in a sorted collection.

subtopics.name: LinkedHashMap Access Order
subtopics.content:
1. What is Access Order?

LinkedHashMap maintains a doubly-linked list across its entries. By default it uses insertion order, but it can be configured for access order — where the most recently accessed entry moves to the end.

2. Enabling Access Order

// initialCapacity, loadFactor, accessOrder=true
new LinkedHashMap<>(16, 0.75f, true);

With accessOrder = true, every get() or put() moves the entry to the tail, so the head always holds the least-recently-used entry.

3. Building an LRU Cache

class LRUCache<K,V> extends LinkedHashMap<K,V> {
    private final int capacity;
    LRUCache(int cap) { super(16, 0.75f, true); this.capacity = cap; }

    @Override
    protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> eldest) {
        return size() > capacity;
    }
}

This is the classic, idiomatic way to implement an LRU cache in core Java.

subtopics.name: PriorityQueue Internals
subtopics.content:
1. What is a PriorityQueue?

PriorityQueue is an unbounded queue where elements are ordered by priority rather than insertion order. The head is always the smallest element (or by a supplied Comparator).

2. Internal Structure

  • Backed by a binary min-heap stored in a resizable array.
  • For a node at index i: children are at 2i+1 and 2i+2.
  • offer() and poll() are O(log n); peek() is O(1).

3. Key Points

  • It is not sorted when iterated — only the head is guaranteed to be the min.
  • Does not permit null.
  • Not thread-safe; use PriorityBlockingQueue for concurrency.

4. Example

// Max-heap using reverse comparator
PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap =
    new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.reverseOrder());

Commonly used in Dijkstra's algorithm, task scheduling, and top-K problems.

subtopics.name: CopyOnWriteArraySet
subtopics.content:
1. What is CopyOnWriteArraySet?

CopyOnWriteArraySet is a thread-safe Set backed by a CopyOnWriteArrayList. On every mutation (add/remove) it creates a fresh copy of the underlying array.

2. How it works

  • Reads are lock-free and operate on a stable snapshot of the array.
  • Writes lock, copy the array, apply the change, then swap the reference.
  • Iterators are fail-safe — they never throw ConcurrentModificationException and reflect the snapshot at creation time.

3. When to Use

  • Best for read-heavy, write-rare scenarios (e.g., listener/observer lists).
  • Avoid for large sets with frequent writes — each write copies the whole array (O(n)).
  • Uniqueness is checked with equals() (linear scan), so lookups are O(n), unlike a HashSet.

subtopics.name: DelayQueue
subtopics.content:
1. What is DelayQueue?

DelayQueue is an unbounded, thread-safe blocking queue where each element can only be taken after its delay has expired. Elements must implement the Delayed interface.

2. How it works

  • Internally backed by a PriorityQueue ordered by the shortest remaining delay.
  • poll() returns null if the head's delay has not expired.
  • take() blocks until the head element becomes available.

3. Example

class Task implements Delayed {
    long startTime;
    public long getDelay(TimeUnit unit) {
        return unit.convert(startTime - System.currentTimeMillis(),
                            TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
    }
    public int compareTo(Delayed o) { ... }
}

Used for scheduled task execution, cache expiration, and retry-after-delay mechanisms.

subtopics.name: SynchronousQueue
subtopics.content:
1. What is SynchronousQueue?

SynchronousQueue is a blocking queue with zero capacity. Each put() must wait for a corresponding take() — it is a direct hand-off between a producer and a consumer thread.

2. Key Points

  • It holds no elements — size() is always 0 and peek() returns null.
  • A producer blocks until a consumer is ready, and vice versa.
  • Supports optional fairness (FIFO) ordering of waiting threads.

3. Where it's used

It is the default work queue for Executors.newCachedThreadPool(). Because tasks are handed off directly, the pool creates a new thread whenever no idle thread is available — ideal for many short-lived, bursty tasks.

subtopics.name: Spliterator
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Spliterator?

Spliterator ("splittable iterator") was introduced in Java 8 to support parallel traversal. It can traverse elements one by one and can also split itself so portions can be processed in parallel.

2. Core Methods

  • tryAdvance(action) — process the next element if present.
  • trySplit() — split off a chunk into another Spliterator for parallel work.
  • estimateSize() — estimated remaining elements.
  • characteristics() — flags like ORDERED, SORTED, SIZED, DISTINCT, IMMUTABLE.

3. Role in Streams

Every collection exposes a spliterator(). The Stream API uses it to drive both sequential and parallel streams — trySplit() is what enables the fork/join framework to divide the workload. Its characteristics allow the pipeline to apply optimizations (e.g., skipping a sort if already SORTED).

subtopics.name: Generic Class
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Generic Class?

A Generic Class is a class that is parameterized over one or more types. Instead of hardcoding a specific type, we define a type parameter (like T) that is supplied when the class is used. This provides compile-time type safety and eliminates the need for explicit casting.

2. Example

class Box<T> {
    private T value;
    public void set(T value) { this.value = value; }
    public T get() { return value; }
}

Box<String> box = new Box<>();
box.set("Hello");
String s = box.get(); // no cast needed

3. Benefits

  • Type safety: Errors are caught at compile time instead of runtime.
  • No casting: The compiler inserts casts automatically.
  • Code reuse: One class works with many types.

Common naming conventions for type parameters are T (Type), E (Element), K/V (Key/Value), and N (Number).

subtopics.name: Generic Method
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Generic Method?

A Generic Method declares its own type parameter, independent of the class. The type parameter is placed before the return type. This lets a single method operate on different types while remaining type-safe.

2. Example

public static <T> T firstElement(List<T> list) {
    return list.get(0);
}

String s = firstElement(List.of("a", "b"));
Integer i = firstElement(List.of(1, 2));

3. Key Points

  • The type parameter <T> is declared just before the return type.
  • The compiler infers the type from the arguments, so we rarely specify it explicitly.
  • A generic method can exist in a non-generic class, and can even define type parameters different from its enclosing generic class.

subtopics.name: Bounded Generics
subtopics.content:
1. What are Bounded Generics?

Bounded type parameters restrict the types that can be used as arguments by specifying an upper bound with the extends keyword. This allows us to call methods of the bound type inside the generic code.

2. Example

public static <T extends Number> double sum(List<T> list) {
    double total = 0;
    for (T n : list) {
        total += n.doubleValue(); // allowed because T is a Number
    }
    return total;
}

3. Multiple Bounds

A type parameter can have multiple bounds using &. If one bound is a class, it must come first, followed by interfaces. 

<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>

Bounded generics give us access to the bound type's API while keeping type safety.

subtopics.name: Wildcards (?, extends, super)
subtopics.content:
1. What are Wildcards?

A wildcard (?) represents an unknown type. It is used when we want flexibility in the types a method can accept, especially because generics are invariant (a List<Integer> is NOT a List<Number>).

2. Types of Wildcards

  • Unbounded (?): Unknown type. Example: List<?> — we can read as Object but cannot add elements (except null).
  • Upper Bounded (? extends T): Any type that is T or a subtype. Used for reading (producer).
  • Lower Bounded (? super T): Any type that is T or a supertype. Used for writing (consumer).

3. Example

// Upper bound: read numbers
double total(List<? extends Number> list) { ... }

// Lower bound: write integers
void addInts(List<? super Integer> list) {
    list.add(1);
}

Wildcards are governed by the PECS principle: Producer Extends, Consumer Super.

subtopics.name: PECS Principle
subtopics.content:
1. What is PECS?

PECS stands for Producer Extends, Consumer Super. It is a rule for choosing the correct wildcard bound.

2. The Rule

  • If a collection produces values that we read from it, use ? extends T.
  • If a collection consumes values that we write into it, use ? super T.

3. Example (Collections.copy)

public static <T> void copy(
        List<? super T> dest,      // consumer -> super
        List<? extends T> src) {    // producer -> extends
    for (T item : src) {
        dest.add(item);
    }
}

The source produces elements (we read from it → extends), and the destination consumes elements (we write to it → super). Following PECS gives APIs maximum flexibility.

subtopics.name: Type Erasure
subtopics.content:
1. What is Type Erasure?

Type Erasure is the process by which the Java compiler removes generic type information at compile time. Generics exist only for compile-time checks; at runtime, the type parameters are replaced with their bounds (or Object if unbounded). This is done to maintain backward compatibility with pre-generics code.

2. What Happens

  • List<String> and List<Integer> both become raw List at runtime — they share the same Class object.
  • The compiler inserts casts automatically where needed.
  • For bounded types, T extends Number is erased to Number.

3. Consequences

  • Cannot use instanceof with a parameterized type: obj instanceof List<String> is illegal.
  • Cannot create arrays of generic types: new T[10] is not allowed.
  • Cannot use primitives as type arguments.
  • Overloads that differ only by generic type (method(List<String>) vs method(List<Integer>)) clash after erasure.

subtopics.name: Why Generics don't support primitive types?
subtopics.content:
1. The Reason

Generics do not support primitive types because of type erasure. At runtime, type parameters are erased to Object (or their bound), and primitives like int or double are not subclasses of Object — only reference types are.

2. The Workaround

We use the wrapper classes (Integer, Double, Boolean, etc.) instead. Java's autoboxing converts between primitives and wrappers automatically. 

List<Integer> list = new ArrayList<>();
list.add(5);          // autoboxing int -> Integer
int x = list.get(0);  // unboxing Integer -> int

3. Trade-off

Autoboxing has a performance cost due to object creation and unboxing. For performance-critical numeric code, primitive-specialized types (like IntStream or arrays) are preferred. Project Valhalla aims to bring specialized generics over primitives in the future.

subtopics.name: equals() contract
subtopics.content:
1. What is the equals() contract?

The equals() method defines logical equality between objects. Any override must satisfy five properties:

  • Reflexive: x.equals(x) is always true.
  • Symmetric: x.equals(y) == y.equals(x).
  • Transitive: if x.equals(y) and y.equals(z), then x.equals(z).
  • Consistent: repeated calls return the same result if objects are unchanged.
  • Non-null: x.equals(null) must return false.

2. Example

@Override
public boolean equals(Object o) {
    if (this == o) return true;
    if (o == null || getClass() != o.getClass()) return false;
    Employee e = (Employee) o;
    return id == e.id && Objects.equals(name, e.name);
}

3. Golden Rule

Whenever you override equals(), you must also override hashCode(), otherwise the object will break in hash-based collections like HashMap and HashSet.

subtopics.name: hashCode() contract
subtopics.content:
1. What is the hashCode() contract?

The hashCode() method returns an integer used by hash-based collections. Its contract states:

  • If two objects are equal (via equals()), they must have the same hash code.
  • If two objects are unequal, they may have the same hash code (a collision) — but distinct hash codes improve performance.
  • hashCode() must be consistent — the same value across calls if the object is unchanged.

2. Example

@Override
public int hashCode() {
    return Objects.hash(id, name);
}

3. Why it matters

A HashMap first uses hashCode() to find the bucket, then equals() to find the exact key. If equals() is overridden but hashCode() is not, equal objects may land in different buckets, so lookups fail. This is why both must be overridden together and use the same fields.

subtopics.name: clone() method
subtopics.content:
1. What is the clone() method?

The clone() method, defined in Object, creates and returns a copy of an object. To use it, the class must implement the Cloneable marker interface, otherwise CloneNotSupportedException is thrown.

2. Example

class Employee implements Cloneable {
    @Override
    protected Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException {
        return super.clone(); // shallow copy
    }
}

3. Shallow vs Deep

By default, Object.clone() performs a shallow copy — primitive fields are copied, but reference fields point to the same nested objects. For a deep copy, we must clone nested objects manually.

4. Modern Preference

The Cloneable/clone() mechanism is considered flawed (broken contract, no constructor call, protected access). Modern code prefers copy constructors, static factory methods, or the Builder pattern instead.

subtopics.name: Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy
subtopics.content:
1. Definitions

A shallow copy creates a new object but copies only the references of nested objects — both copies share the same nested objects.

A deep copy creates a new object and recursively copies all nested objects, so the copy is completely independent.

2. Example

If an Employee contains an Address:

  • Shallow copy: both employees share the same Address; modifying it affects both.
  • Deep copy: each employee has its own Address; changes are isolated.

3. How to Deep Copy

  • Copy constructors that clone nested objects.
  • Serialization/deserialization (e.g., via Jackson or Java serialization).
  • Manual recursive cloning.

In enterprise applications I generally prefer deep copies where object independence and data integrity matter, to avoid accidental shared-state bugs.

subtopics.name: How to create Immutable Class
subtopics.content:
1. What is an Immutable Class?

An immutable class is one whose state cannot change after construction. String, Integer, and LocalDate are classic examples.

2. Rules to Make a Class Immutable

  • Declare the class final so it cannot be subclassed.
  • Make all fields private and final.
  • Do not provide setters.
  • Initialize all fields via the constructor.
  • For mutable reference fields, make defensive copies in the constructor and in getters.

3. Example

public final class Employee {
    private final String name;
    private final List<String> roles;

    public Employee(String name, List<String> roles) {
        this.name = name;
        this.roles = new ArrayList<>(roles); // defensive copy
    }

    public String getName() { return name; }

    public List<String> getRoles() {
        return new ArrayList<>(roles); // defensive copy
    }
}

In modern Java, a record gives shallow immutability out of the box.

subtopics.name: Benefits of Immutable Objects
subtopics.content:
Benefits of Immutable Objects

  • Thread safety: Immutable objects can be shared across threads without synchronization because their state never changes.
  • Safe caching & reuse: They can be freely cached; the JVM does this for String literals and small Integer values.
  • Valid hash keys: Their hash code never changes, making them ideal keys in HashMap/HashSet.
  • Predictability: No hidden state changes, which reduces bugs and simplifies reasoning.
  • Failure atomicity: If construction fails, no half-built object is exposed.

Trade-off

Every "modification" creates a new object, which can increase memory pressure and garbage collection. For high-frequency mutations, this cost must be weighed — but for most domain objects and DTOs, immutability is the safer default.

subtopics.name: Serialization
subtopics.content:
1. What is Serialization?

Serialization is the process of converting an object into a byte stream so it can be persisted to disk, sent over a network, or cached. Deserialization is the reverse — reconstructing the object from bytes.

2. How to Enable It

A class must implement the Serializable marker interface. 

class Employee implements Serializable {
    private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;
    private String name;
    private transient String password; // not serialized
}

3. Key Points

  • Fields marked transient or static are not serialized.
  • All non-transient fields must themselves be serializable, otherwise a NotSerializableException is thrown.
  • Serialization bypasses constructors during deserialization.
  • It is often considered a security risk; JSON (Jackson) or Protobuf are preferred for external data exchange.

subtopics.name: Externalizable
subtopics.content:
1. What is Externalizable?

Externalizable is a sub-interface of Serializable that gives the developer full control over the serialization format by implementing two methods: writeExternal() and readExternal().

2. Serializable vs Externalizable

SerializableExternalizable
Marker interface, no methodsHas writeExternal() / readExternal()
JVM handles the process (reflection)Developer controls exactly what is written
Does not call a constructorRequires a public no-arg constructor
Simpler but slowerMore code but faster and more compact

3. Example

public void writeExternal(ObjectOutput out) throws IOException {
    out.writeUTF(name);
    out.writeInt(age);
}
public void readExternal(ObjectInput in) throws IOException {
    name = in.readUTF();
    age = in.readInt();
}

Externalizable is used when we need custom, high-performance serialization control.

subtopics.name: transient keyword
subtopics.content:
1. What is the transient keyword?

The transient keyword marks a field to be excluded from serialization. When an object is serialized, transient fields are skipped, and on deserialization they are restored to their default value (null, 0, or false).

2. When to Use It

  • Sensitive data: passwords, tokens, or secret keys should not be persisted.
  • Derived/cached data: values that can be recomputed.
  • Non-serializable references: fields whose type does not implement Serializable.

3. Example

class User implements Serializable {
    private String username;
    private transient String password; // skipped during serialization
}

Note: static fields are also not serialized, because they belong to the class, not the instance.

subtopics.name: serialVersionUID
subtopics.content:
1. What is serialVersionUID?

serialVersionUID is a unique version identifier for a Serializable class. During deserialization, the JVM compares the UID in the byte stream with the UID of the loaded class. If they differ, an InvalidClassException is thrown.

2. Why Declare It Explicitly?

If we don't declare it, the JVM computes one automatically based on the class structure (fields, methods, etc.). Any small change to the class alters this generated value, breaking deserialization of previously stored objects. Declaring it explicitly gives us control over versioning compatibility.

3. Example

class Employee implements Serializable {
    private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;
}

Best practice: always declare serialVersionUID in serializable classes to ensure predictable, controlled version compatibility.

subtopics.name: Reflection API
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Reflection API?

Reflection allows a program to inspect and manipulate classes, methods, fields, and constructors at runtime, even without knowing their names at compile time.

2. Example

Class<?> clazz = Class.forName("com.app.Employee");
Object obj = clazz.getDeclaredConstructor().newInstance();

Method m = clazz.getMethod("getName");
String name = (String) m.invoke(obj);

Field f = clazz.getDeclaredField("salary");
f.setAccessible(true); // access private field
f.set(obj, 5000);

3. Use Cases

  • Frameworks like Spring and Hibernate use it for dependency injection and ORM mapping.
  • Testing frameworks (JUnit) discover and invoke test methods.
  • Serialization libraries read/write fields.

4. Drawbacks

  • Slower than direct calls (no JIT inlining).
  • Breaks encapsulation and compile-time safety.
  • Can fail at runtime and complicates security.

subtopics.name: How Spring uses Reflection
subtopics.content:
1. Where Spring uses Reflection

Spring relies heavily on reflection to power its IoC container and many framework features without the developer writing wiring code.

  • Bean instantiation: Spring calls constructors via reflection to create beans.
  • Dependency Injection: It reads @Autowired fields/setters/constructors and injects dependencies reflectively.
  • Annotation scanning: Component scanning detects @Component, @Service, @Controller, etc.
  • AOP proxies: Spring creates dynamic proxies (JDK or CGLIB) to weave cross-cutting concerns like transactions and security.
  • Request mapping: Spring MVC maps HTTP requests to controller methods by reflecting on @RequestMapping.

2. Trade-off

Reflection makes Spring flexible and declarative but adds startup cost. This is why newer frameworks (and Spring's AOT/native support with GraalVM) precompute some of this at build time to reduce reflection overhead.

subtopics.name: Static Nested Class
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Static Nested Class?

A static nested class is a class declared static inside another class. It does not hold a reference to an instance of the outer class, so it can be instantiated independently and can only directly access the outer class's static members.

2. Example

class Outer {
    static int count = 0;
    static class Nested {
        void show() {
            System.out.println("count = " + count);
        }
    }
}

Outer.Nested n = new Outer.Nested();
n.show();

3. Static Nested vs Inner Class

  • A static nested class has no implicit reference to the outer instance.
  • An inner (non-static) class holds a hidden reference to the enclosing instance and can access its instance members.

Static nested classes are commonly used for grouping helper classes (e.g., the Builder pattern) and avoid the memory leak risk of inner classes.

subtopics.name: Anonymous Inner Class
subtopics.content:
1. What is an Anonymous Inner Class?

An anonymous inner class is a class without a name, declared and instantiated in a single expression. It is used to provide a one-off implementation of an interface or abstract class, usually as an argument.

2. Example

Runnable r = new Runnable() {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running");
    }
};

3. Key Points

  • It can access final or effectively final local variables of the enclosing method.
  • It cannot declare a constructor (it has no name).
  • The compiler generates a class named like Outer$1.

4. Modern Alternative

Since Java 8, lambda expressions replace anonymous classes for functional interfaces, producing cleaner code: 

Runnable r = () -> System.out.println("Running");

subtopics.name: JVM Architecture Deep Dive
subtopics.content:
1. JVM Architecture Overview

The JVM has three main subsystems: the Class Loader, the Runtime Data Areas (memory), and the Execution Engine.

2. Class Loader Subsystem

Loads, links, and initializes .class files into memory using the parent-delegation model.

3. Runtime Data Areas

  • Method Area (Metaspace): class metadata, static variables — shared.
  • Heap: all objects and instance variables — shared, GC-managed.
  • Stack: one per thread; holds frames with local variables and partial results.
  • PC Register: per thread; address of the current instruction.
  • Native Method Stack: per thread; for native (JNI) calls.

4. Execution Engine

  • Interpreter: executes bytecode line by line.
  • JIT Compiler: compiles hot bytecode to native code for speed.
  • Garbage Collector: reclaims unused heap memory.

subtopics.name: Class Loading Process
subtopics.content:
1. The Three Phases

Class loading follows three phases: Loading, Linking, and Initialization.

2. Loading

The class loader reads the .class bytecode and creates a Class object in the method area.

3. Linking

  • Verification: ensures the bytecode is valid and safe.
  • Preparation: allocates memory for static fields and sets default values (0, null, false).
  • Resolution: replaces symbolic references with direct references.

4. Initialization

Static initializers and static field assignments run, giving statics their actual values. This happens lazily — on first active use of the class. 

Loading -> Verification -> Preparation -> Resolution -> Initialization

subtopics.name: Class Loader Hierarchy
subtopics.content:
1. The Hierarchy

Java uses a hierarchy of class loaders, each with a defined scope:

  • Bootstrap ClassLoader: loads core JDK classes (java.lang.*, rt.jar/modules). Written in native code; the root of the hierarchy.
  • Platform (Extension) ClassLoader: loads platform/extension classes.
  • Application (System) ClassLoader: loads classes from the application classpath.

2. Example

ClassLoader cl = MyClass.class.getClassLoader();
System.out.println(cl);              // AppClassLoader
System.out.println(cl.getParent()); // PlatformClassLoader
System.out.println(cl.getParent().getParent()); // null (Bootstrap)

3. Custom Class Loaders

Developers can extend ClassLoader to load classes from custom sources (network, encrypted files, plugins). Frameworks and app servers use custom loaders for isolation between deployed applications.

subtopics.name: Parent Delegation Model
subtopics.content:
1. What is Parent Delegation?

The parent delegation model means that before a class loader tries to load a class itself, it delegates the request to its parent. Only if the parent (and its ancestors) cannot find the class does the current loader attempt to load it.

2. The Flow

App ClassLoader -> Platform ClassLoader -> Bootstrap ClassLoader
(if not found, control returns downward to actually load)

3. Why It Matters

  • Security: core classes like java.lang.String can only be loaded by the bootstrap loader, so a malicious custom String cannot replace it.
  • Consistency: a class is loaded once by the right loader, avoiding duplicate definitions.

4. Exceptions

Some frameworks (web containers, OSGi, JDBC's SPI) intentionally break delegation for isolation or to load application-provided implementations.

subtopics.name: Program Counter Register
subtopics.content:
1. What is the PC Register?

The Program Counter (PC) Register is a small per-thread memory area that holds the address of the currently executing JVM instruction.

2. Key Points

  • Each thread has its own PC register, enabling independent execution and correct resumption after context switches.
  • For Java methods, it holds the address/offset of the current bytecode instruction.
  • For native methods, the PC register value is undefined (native code is outside JVM bytecode).
  • It is very small and never throws OutOfMemoryError.

3. Why It's Needed

Because the JVM is multithreaded and threads are frequently context-switched, each thread must remember where it left off. The PC register provides that per-thread bookmark so execution resumes correctly.

subtopics.name: Native Method Stack
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Native Method Stack?

The Native Method Stack is a per-thread memory area used when a Java program calls native (non-Java) methods through the Java Native Interface (JNI), such as C/C++ code.

2. Key Points

  • Each thread has its own native method stack, just like its Java stack.
  • It stores the state of native method invocations (parameters, local variables of native code).
  • When a native method runs, execution moves from the Java stack to the native stack.
  • It can throw StackOverflowError (too deep) or OutOfMemoryError (cannot expand).

3. Why It's Needed

Java relies on native code for OS-level operations (I/O, threading primitives). The native method stack manages these calls separately from pure Java frames, keeping the two execution models cleanly divided.

subtopics.name: Escape Analysis
subtopics.content:
1. What is Escape Analysis?

Escape analysis is a JIT optimization that determines whether an object escapes the scope in which it was created — i.e., whether it is accessible outside its method or thread.

2. Optimizations It Enables

  • Stack Allocation: if an object doesn't escape, it can be allocated on the stack instead of the heap, avoiding GC.
  • Scalar Replacement: the object's fields are broken into local variables, eliminating the object entirely.
  • Lock Elision: if a locked object doesn't escape a thread, the synchronization can be removed.

3. Example

void compute() {
    Point p = new Point(1, 2); // does not escape
    int sum = p.x + p.y;       // JIT may avoid heap allocation
}

Enabled by default under the C2 compiler, escape analysis reduces heap pressure and GC overhead, improving performance transparently.

subtopics.name: JIT Compiler
subtopics.content:
1. What is the JIT Compiler?

The Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler is part of the execution engine that compiles frequently executed ("hot") bytecode into native machine code at runtime, so it runs much faster than interpretation.

2. How it works

  • Code initially runs via the interpreter.
  • The JVM profiles execution and identifies hotspots (methods/loops run many times).
  • The JIT compiles those to native code and caches it in the code cache.
  • Subsequent calls execute the compiled native code directly.

3. Optimizations

  • Method inlining, loop unrolling, dead-code elimination.
  • Escape analysis, and speculative optimizations with deoptimization fallback.

The combination of interpreter + JIT is why the JVM is called an adaptive, HotSpot runtime — it optimizes based on actual runtime behavior.

subtopics.name: C1 vs C2 Compiler
subtopics.content:
1. Two JIT Compilers

HotSpot ships two JIT compilers that serve different goals:

2. C1 (Client Compiler)

  • Compiles quickly with lighter optimizations.
  • Optimizes for fast startup and low latency.
  • Ideal for short-running or GUI/client applications.

3. C2 (Server Compiler)

  • Compiles aggressively with deeper optimizations (inlining, escape analysis).
  • Higher compile cost but much faster steady-state performance.
  • Ideal for long-running server applications.

4. Tiered Compilation

Modern JVMs use tiered compilation, combining both: code starts interpreted, is quickly compiled by C1 for fast warm-up, and hot methods are later recompiled by C2 for peak performance — getting the best of both.

subtopics.name: OutOfMemoryError Types
subtopics.content:
