1. What is OutOfMemoryError?

OutOfMemoryError is thrown when the JVM cannot allocate memory and garbage collection cannot free enough. It is an Error, not an Exception.

2. Common Types

  • Java heap space: the heap is full of live objects (often a memory leak or undersized heap).
  • GC overhead limit exceeded: the GC runs constantly but reclaims very little.
  • Metaspace: too many classes loaded (class-loader leaks, dynamic proxies).
  • Unable to create new native thread: OS thread limit or native memory exhausted.
  • Direct buffer memory: off-heap NIO buffers exhausted.

3. How to Diagnose

  • Enable -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError to capture a heap dump.
  • Analyze with tools like Eclipse MAT or VisualVM.
  • Look for retained objects and class-loader leaks.

subtopics.name: StackOverflowError
subtopics.content:
1. What is StackOverflowError?

StackOverflowError is thrown when a thread's call stack exceeds its allocated size — typically from very deep or infinite recursion. Each method call pushes a frame; when there is no room for another, the error is thrown.

2. Common Causes

  • Recursion without a proper base case (infinite recursion).
  • Extremely deep but finite recursion on large inputs.
  • Cyclic method calls (A calls B calls A ...).

3. Example

void loop() {
    loop(); // no base case -> StackOverflowError
}

4. How to Fix / Tune

  • Add or correct the recursion base case.
  • Convert deep recursion to an iterative solution using an explicit stack.
  • Increase thread stack size with -Xss if the depth is legitimately large.

Unlike OutOfMemoryError (heap), this concerns the per-thread stack.

subtopics.name: G1 GC Internals
subtopics.content:
1. What is G1 GC?

The Garbage-First (G1) collector is the default GC since Java 9. It targets predictable pause times on large heaps while maintaining good throughput.

2. Region-Based Heap

G1 divides the heap into many equal-sized regions, each dynamically assigned as Eden, Survivor, Old, or Humongous (for very large objects). This replaces fixed contiguous young/old spaces.

3. How it works

  • Tracks each region's garbage and collects the most garbage-filled regions first (hence "Garbage-First").
  • Performs concurrent marking to find live objects with minimal pauses.
  • Uses evacuation — copying live objects to new regions, which also compacts and avoids fragmentation.

4. Tuning

-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis sets a soft pause-time goal, and G1 adjusts how many regions it collects per cycle to meet it. Ideal for heaps of several GB.

subtopics.name: ZGC
subtopics.content:
1. What is ZGC?

ZGC (Z Garbage Collector) is a scalable, low-latency collector designed for very large heaps (up to terabytes) with pause times consistently under a few milliseconds, independent of heap size.

2. Key Techniques

  • Concurrent everything: marking, relocation, and compaction happen mostly while the application runs.
  • Colored pointers: metadata is stored in unused bits of 64-bit references to track object state.
  • Load barriers: intercept references to relocate objects transparently and keep pointers valid.

3. Characteristics

  • Pause times do not grow with heap or live-set size.
  • Great for latency-sensitive services (trading, real-time APIs).
  • Enabled with -XX:+UseZGC; production-ready since Java 15 (generational ZGC in Java 21).

Trade-off: it favors ultra-low latency over raw throughput and uses more CPU/memory overhead.

subtopics.name: Shenandoah GC
subtopics.content:
1. What is Shenandoah GC?

Shenandoah is a low-pause-time garbage collector (developed by Red Hat) that performs concurrent compaction — moving objects while the application threads keep running — so pause times stay low regardless of heap size.

2. Key Technique: Brooks Pointers

Each object carries an extra forwarding pointer. During concurrent relocation, accesses are redirected through this pointer to the object's new location, allowing compaction without stopping all threads.

3. Shenandoah vs ZGC

  • Both aim for very low, heap-size-independent pauses.
  • Shenandoah uses Brooks forwarding pointers; ZGC uses colored pointers and load barriers.
  • Shenandoah works well on smaller-to-large heaps; ZGC scales to multi-terabyte heaps.

Enabled with -XX:+UseShenandoahGC. Ideal for applications needing consistent responsiveness.

subtopics.name: GC Tuning
subtopics.content:
1. What is GC Tuning?

GC tuning is the process of configuring the garbage collector and heap to balance three goals: throughput, latency (pause time), and memory footprint.

2. Key Steps

  • Choose the right collector: G1 (balanced), ZGC/Shenandoah (low latency), Parallel (max throughput).
  • Size the heap: set -Xms and -Xmx equal to avoid resizing pauses.
  • Set pause goals: e.g., -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 for G1.
  • Tune generations: adjust young-gen size to reduce promotion and minor GC frequency.

3. Methodology

  1. Enable GC logging (-Xlog:gc*).
  2. Measure pause times, frequency, and throughput.
  3. Change one parameter at a time and re-measure.

Golden rule: measure first. Most GC problems are actually memory leaks or an undersized heap, not GC configuration.

subtopics.name: JVM Parameters (-Xms, -Xmx)
subtopics.content:
1. What are -Xms and -Xmx?

  • -Xms — the initial heap size allocated at JVM startup.
  • -Xmx — the maximum heap size the JVM can grow to.

2. Example

java -Xms512m -Xmx2g -jar app.jar

This starts with 512 MB and allows the heap to grow up to 2 GB.

3. Best Practice

In production, set -Xms equal to -Xmx. This avoids costly heap-resizing pauses and makes memory behavior predictable, since the full heap is committed upfront.

4. Other Common Parameters

  • -Xss — thread stack size.
  • -XX:MetaspaceSize / -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize — class metadata memory.
  • -XX:+UseG1GC / -XX:+UseZGC — select the collector.
  • -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError — dump heap on OOM.

subtopics.name: JVM Monitoring Tools
subtopics.content:
1. Why Monitor the JVM?

Monitoring reveals memory usage, GC behavior, thread states, and CPU hotspots — essential for diagnosing leaks, deadlocks, and performance issues.

2. Command-Line Tools

  • jps — list running JVM processes.
  • jstat — live GC and heap statistics.
  • jmap — heap dumps and memory maps.
  • jstack — thread dumps (great for deadlock detection).
  • jcmd — versatile diagnostic command hub.

3. GUI & Profiling Tools

  • VisualVM — visual monitoring and profiling.
  • Java Mission Control (JMC) with Java Flight Recorder (JFR) — low-overhead production profiling.
  • Eclipse MAT — heap dump analysis for leak hunting.

4. Production Monitoring

Expose JVM metrics via JMX or Micrometer and visualize with Prometheus + Grafana for continuous observability.

subtopics.name: Heap Dump Analysis
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Heap Dump?

A heap dump is a snapshot of all objects in the JVM heap at a point in time, including their sizes, fields, and references. It is the primary artifact for diagnosing memory leaks and high memory usage.

2. How to Capture One

  • Automatically on OOM: -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/path.
  • On demand: jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=heap.hprof <pid> or via jcmd.

3. Analysis Tools

  • Eclipse MAT — finds leak suspects and dominator trees.
  • VisualVM / JMC — browse objects and references.

4. What to Look For

  • Retained size: memory that would be freed if an object were collected.
  • Dominator tree: which objects keep the most memory alive.
  • GC roots: the reference chain preventing collection — this pinpoints the leak.

Common culprits: static collections that grow unbounded, unclosed resources, and ThreadLocal leaks in pooled threads.

subtopics.name: Default Methods
subtopics.content:
1. What are Default Methods?

Default methods (Java 8) let an interface provide a concrete implementation using the default keyword. Implementing classes inherit this behavior automatically and can optionally override it.

2. Why they were introduced

Before Java 8, adding a method to an interface broke every implementing class. Default methods enabled backward-compatible interface evolution — for example, adding stream() and forEach() to the Collection hierarchy without breaking existing code.

3. Example

interface NotificationService {
    void send(String msg);
    default void log() {
        System.out.println("Logging notification");
    }
}

4. Diamond Problem

If a class inherits the same default method from two interfaces, it must override it and can call a specific one using InterfaceName.super.method().

subtopics.name: Static Interface Methods
subtopics.content:
1. What are Static Interface Methods?

Since Java 8, interfaces can declare static methods with a body. They belong to the interface itself, not to instances, and are called using the interface name.

2. Example

interface MathUtil {
    static int square(int x) { return x * x; }
}

int result = MathUtil.square(5); // 25

3. Key Points

  • They are not inherited by implementing classes and cannot be overridden.
  • They cannot be called on an instance or via this.
  • They are commonly used for utility/factory methods related to the interface — e.g., Comparator.comparing(), Stream.of(), Predicate.not().

They help keep helper methods grouped with the interface instead of a separate utility class.

subtopics.name: Predicate chaining
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Predicate?

Predicate<T> is a functional interface representing a boolean-valued condition via its test(T) method.

2. Chaining Methods

  • and(other) — logical AND (short-circuiting).
  • or(other) — logical OR.
  • negate() — logical NOT.
  • Predicate.not(p) — static negation (Java 11).
  • isEqual(obj) — tests equality with a reference object.

3. Example

Predicate<Integer> positive = n -> n > 0;
Predicate<Integer> even     = n -> n % 2 == 0;

Predicate<Integer> positiveAndEven = positive.and(even);
positiveAndEven.test(4); // true
positiveAndEven.test(-2); // false

Chaining lets us compose complex, reusable business rules from small predicates.

subtopics.name: Function chaining
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Function?

Function<T,R> represents a transformation from an input of type T to a result of type R via apply().

2. Chaining Methods

  • andThen(after) — applies this function first, then after.
  • compose(before) — applies before first, then this.
  • Function.identity() — returns the input unchanged.

3. Example

Function<Integer,Integer> times2 = x -> x * 2;
Function<Integer,Integer> plus3  = x -> x + 3;

times2.andThen(plus3).apply(5); // (5*2)+3 = 13
times2.compose(plus3).apply(5); // (5+3)*2 = 16

The key difference is order of execution: andThen runs the current function first, compose runs the argument first.

subtopics.name: BiFunction
subtopics.content:
1. What is BiFunction?

BiFunction<T,U,R> is a functional interface that takes two arguments of types T and U and returns a result of type R via apply(T, U).

2. Example

BiFunction<Integer,Integer,Integer> add = (a, b) -> a + b;
add.apply(3, 4); // 7

BiFunction<String,String,Integer> totalLen = (a, b) -> a.length() + b.length();

3. Chaining

It supports andThen(Function) to post-process the result: 

add.andThen(sum -> "Result: " + sum).apply(3, 4); // "Result: 7"

BiFunction is widely used in Map.merge(), Map.compute(), and stream reduce() operations that combine two values.

subtopics.name: BiConsumer
subtopics.content:
1. What is BiConsumer?

BiConsumer<T,U> takes two arguments and returns no result. It performs a side effect via accept(T, U).

2. Example

BiConsumer<String,Integer> print =
    (name, age) -> System.out.println(name + " = " + age);
print.accept("Alice", 30);

// Iterating a Map
Map<String,Integer> map = ...;
map.forEach((k, v) -> System.out.println(k + ":" + v));

3. Key Points

  • It is the two-argument version of Consumer.
  • Map.forEach() uses it directly to iterate key-value pairs.
  • Supports andThen() to chain multiple consumers on the same inputs.

subtopics.name: BinaryOperator
subtopics.content:
1. What is BinaryOperator?

BinaryOperator<T> is a special case of BiFunction where both operands and the result are the same type T. It represents an operation combining two values into one.

2. Example

BinaryOperator<Integer> sum = (a, b) -> a + b;
sum.apply(4, 6); // 10

// In stream reduction
int total = numbers.stream().reduce(0, Integer::sum);

3. Static Helpers

  • BinaryOperator.minBy(comparator) — returns the smaller of two values.
  • BinaryOperator.maxBy(comparator) — returns the larger of two values.

It is the natural type for the accumulator in Stream.reduce() and for Map.merge().

subtopics.name: UnaryOperator
subtopics.content:
1. What is UnaryOperator?

UnaryOperator<T> is a special case of Function where the input and output types are the same. It represents a transformation on a single value that returns the same type.

2. Example

UnaryOperator<String> toUpper = String::toUpperCase;
toUpper.apply("hello"); // "HELLO"

// Replacing all elements in place
List<Integer> nums = new ArrayList<>(List.of(1,2,3));
nums.replaceAll(n -> n * 10); // [10, 20, 30]

3. Key Points

  • It inherits andThen() and compose() from Function.
  • UnaryOperator.identity() returns the input unchanged.
  • List.replaceAll() and Stream.iterate() use it directly.

subtopics.name: Stream Pipeline Internals
subtopics.content:
1. Structure of a Stream Pipeline

A stream pipeline has three parts:

  • Source: a collection, array, or generator.
  • Intermediate operations: filter, map, sorted — they are lazy and return a new stream.
  • Terminal operation: collect, forEach, reduce — triggers execution and produces a result.

2. How it Executes

Intermediate operations are not run when declared; they build up a chain. When the terminal operation runs, elements are pushed through the pipeline one at a time (not stage-by-stage). This is called internal iteration and enables loop fusion — multiple operations on one element in a single pass.

3. Key Points

  • Streams do not store data; they process from a source.
  • A stream can be consumed only once.
  • Short-circuiting operations like findFirst and limit can stop early.

subtopics.name: Lazy Evaluation
subtopics.content:
1. What is Lazy Evaluation?

In streams, intermediate operations are lazy — they are not executed until a terminal operation is invoked. Until then, the pipeline just records what to do.

2. Example

Stream<Integer> s = Stream.of(1,2,3,4)
    .filter(n -> { System.out.println("filter " + n); return n % 2 == 0; });
// nothing printed yet — no terminal op

long count = s.count(); // now the pipeline runs

3. Benefits

  • Efficiency: avoids unnecessary work — combined with short-circuiting, findFirst() can stop after the first match.
  • Loop fusion: multiple operations apply to each element in a single pass.
  • Infinite streams: Stream.iterate()/generate() work because elements are produced only on demand.

subtopics.name: Parallel Stream
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Parallel Stream?

A parallel stream splits its data and processes chunks concurrently using the ForkJoinPool.commonPool(), potentially using all CPU cores. It is created via parallelStream() or stream().parallel().

2. How it works

The stream's Spliterator repeatedly calls trySplit() to divide the source, the fork/join framework processes the pieces in parallel, and the results are combined.

3. When to Use / Avoid

  • Good for: large datasets, CPU-bound work, and stateless, associative operations.
  • Avoid when: data is small, operations do I/O or blocking, or there is shared mutable state.
  • Reduction functions must be associative and side-effect-free for correct results.

4. Caution

All parallel streams share the common pool by default, so a blocking task can starve the whole application. Measure before assuming parallel is faster.

subtopics.name: groupingBy()
subtopics.content:
1. What is groupingBy()?

Collectors.groupingBy() groups stream elements into a Map where the key is produced by a classifier function and the value is a list (or a downstream-collected result) of the matching elements. It is the streams equivalent of SQL GROUP BY.

2. Example

// Group employees by department
Map<String,List<Emp>> byDept =
    emps.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Emp::getDept));

// With a downstream collector: count per department
Map<String,Long> countByDept =
    emps.stream().collect(
        Collectors.groupingBy(Emp::getDept, Collectors.counting()));

3. Variants

  • groupingBy(classifier)
  • groupingBy(classifier, downstream) — e.g. counting, summing, mapping.
  • groupingBy(classifier, mapFactory, downstream) — choose the map type (e.g. TreeMap).

subtopics.name: partitioningBy()
subtopics.content:
1. What is partitioningBy()?

Collectors.partitioningBy() splits elements into exactly two groups based on a Predicate, returning a Map<Boolean, List<T>> with keys true and false.

2. Example

Map<Boolean,List<Integer>> parts =
    numbers.stream()
           .collect(Collectors.partitioningBy(n -> n % 2 == 0));

parts.get(true);  // even numbers
parts.get(false); // odd numbers

3. partitioningBy vs groupingBy

  • partitioningBy always yields two entries (true/false), even if one is empty.
  • groupingBy yields one entry per distinct key.
  • Use partitioningBy for boolean splits; it also accepts a downstream collector.

subtopics.name: mapping()
subtopics.content:
1. What is mapping()?

Collectors.mapping() is a downstream collector that applies a transformation to each element before passing it to another collector. It is almost always used inside groupingBy or partitioningBy.

2. Example

// Group employees by department, collecting just their names
Map<String,List<String>> namesByDept =
    emps.stream().collect(
        Collectors.groupingBy(
            Emp::getDept,
            Collectors.mapping(Emp::getName, Collectors.toList())));

3. Key Point

Without mapping(), groupingBy would collect whole objects. mapping() lets us transform each grouped element and then collect the transformed results — combining a map and a collect step within grouping.

subtopics.name: collectingAndThen()
subtopics.content:
1. What is collectingAndThen()?

Collectors.collectingAndThen() runs a collector and then applies a finishing transformation to the result. It wraps a collector with a post-processing step.

2. Common Use: Immutable Result

List<String> immutable =
    stream.collect(Collectors.collectingAndThen(
        Collectors.toList(),
        Collections::unmodifiableList));

3. Another Example

// Group, then get the size of each group as an int result
Map<String,Integer> countByDept =
    emps.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
        Emp::getDept,
        Collectors.collectingAndThen(
            Collectors.toList(), List::size)));

It is ideal when you need to collect first and then adapt the collected result in one fluent step.

subtopics.name: toMap()
subtopics.content:
1. What is toMap()?

Collectors.toMap() collects stream elements into a Map using a key mapper and a value mapper.

2. Example

Map<Integer,String> byId =
    emps.stream().collect(
        Collectors.toMap(Emp::getId, Emp::getName));

3. Handling Duplicate Keys

If two elements produce the same key, toMap throws IllegalStateException. Supply a merge function to resolve conflicts: 

Collectors.toMap(
    Emp::getDept, Emp::getName,
    (existing, replacement) -> existing); // keep first

4. Choosing the Map Type

Collectors.toMap(k, v, merge, TreeMap::new);

A fourth argument supplies the map factory. Note the value mapper must not return null.

subtopics.name: joining()
subtopics.content:
1. What is joining()?

Collectors.joining() concatenates the elements of a stream of CharSequence into a single String.

2. Three Forms

  • joining() — simple concatenation.
  • joining(delimiter) — inserts a separator between elements.
  • joining(delimiter, prefix, suffix) — adds wrapping text.

3. Example

List<String> names = List.of("Ann", "Bob", "Cid");

names.stream().collect(Collectors.joining());
// "AnnBobCid"

names.stream().collect(Collectors.joining(", ", "[", "]"));
// "[Ann, Bob, Cid]"

Internally it uses a StringJoiner, making it efficient for building delimited strings such as CSV rows.

subtopics.name: summarizingInt()
subtopics.content:
1. What is summarizingInt()?

Collectors.summarizingInt() computes multiple statistics in a single pass and returns an IntSummaryStatistics object containing count, sum, min, max, and average.

2. Example

IntSummaryStatistics stats =
    emps.stream().collect(
        Collectors.summarizingInt(Emp::getAge));

stats.getCount();   // number of employees
stats.getSum();     // total age
stats.getMin();     // youngest
stats.getMax();     // oldest
stats.getAverage(); // average age

3. Key Point

  • Variants exist for other types: summarizingLong() and summarizingDouble().
  • It is more efficient than computing each statistic separately because it traverses the stream only once.

subtopics.name: findFirst vs findAny
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Both are short-circuiting terminal operations returning an Optional, but they differ in ordering guarantees:

  • findFirst() — returns the first element in encounter order.
  • findAny() — returns any element, giving the runtime freedom to pick whichever is convenient.

2. Sequential vs Parallel

  • In a sequential stream, both usually return the same (first) element.
  • In a parallel stream, findAny() is faster because it doesn't need to coordinate across threads to determine "first", while findFirst() must respect encounter order.

3. Rule of Thumb

Use findFirst() when order matters; use findAny() for better performance in parallel streams when any matching element is acceptable.

subtopics.name: Optional Best Practices
subtopics.content:
1. What is Optional?

Optional<T> is a container that may or may not hold a non-null value. It was introduced to model "absence" explicitly and reduce NullPointerExceptions.

2. Best Practices

  • Use it as a return type for methods that may not produce a value — not for fields or method parameters.
  • Prefer orElse(), orElseGet(), orElseThrow(), map(), and filter() over isPresent() + get().
  • Never call get() without checking presence — it can throw NoSuchElementException.
  • Use orElseGet() (lazy) instead of orElse() when the default is expensive to compute.
  • Do not wrap collections in Optional — return an empty collection instead.

3. Example

String name = findUser(id)
    .map(User::getName)
    .orElse("Unknown");

Used well, Optional makes the possibility of absence explicit and leads to cleaner, null-safe code.

subtopics.name: Try-with-Resources Deep Dive
subtopics.content:
1. What is Try-with-Resources?

Try-with-resources (Java 7) automatically closes resources that implement AutoCloseable at the end of the block, eliminating manual finally cleanup and resource leaks.

2. Example

try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("f.txt"));
     FileWriter fw = new FileWriter("out.txt")) {
    // use resources
} // both closed automatically, in reverse order

3. Key Details

  • Resources are closed in the reverse order of declaration.
  • They are closed before any catch/finally runs.
  • Java 9+ allows using an already-declared effectively-final variable inside the parentheses.

4. Suppressed Exceptions

If the body throws and close() also throws, the close exception is suppressed and attached to the primary exception, retrievable via getSuppressed(). This prevents the real error from being masked.

subtopics.name: AutoCloseable
subtopics.content:
1. What is AutoCloseable?

AutoCloseable is the interface (Java 7) that enables a class to be used in try-with-resources. It declares a single method: void close() throws Exception.

2. AutoCloseable vs Closeable

  • AutoCloseable — root interface; close() may throw any Exception.
  • Closeable (java.io) — extends AutoCloseable; close() throws only IOException and is idempotent.

3. Custom Example

class Connection implements AutoCloseable {
    public Connection() { System.out.println("opened"); }
    @Override
    public void close() {
        System.out.println("closed");
    }
}

try (Connection c = new Connection()) {
    // use c
} // close() called automatically

Best practice: make close() idempotent and avoid throwing from it where possible.

subtopics.name: Checked Exception Design
subtopics.content:
1. What are Checked Exceptions?

Checked exceptions extend Exception (but not RuntimeException) and must be either caught or declared with throws. They are checked at compile time.

2. When to Use

Use a checked exception for recoverable conditions the caller can reasonably be expected to handle — e.g., a missing file, a network timeout, or an unavailable service. It forces the caller to acknowledge the failure.

3. Design Guidelines

  • Throw checked exceptions only when the caller can take meaningful recovery action.
  • Avoid overusing them — they clutter signatures and can leak implementation details.
  • Do not use them for programming errors (those should be unchecked).
  • Wrap low-level checked exceptions into higher-level meaningful ones (exception translation).

Examples: IOException, SQLException, ParseException.

subtopics.name: Unchecked Exception Design
subtopics.content:
1. What are Unchecked Exceptions?

Unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException. They are not checked at compile time and need not be declared or caught.

2. When to Use

Use them for programming errors and unrecoverable conditions — violations of a method's contract that indicate a bug, such as passing null or an invalid argument.

3. Common Examples

  • NullPointerException
  • IllegalArgumentException / IllegalStateException
  • IndexOutOfBoundsException
  • ArithmeticException

4. Design Guidelines

  • Prefer unchecked exceptions for validation failures in modern APIs and frameworks (Spring uses them widely).
  • Fail fast — validate inputs and throw early with a clear message.
  • Don't catch broad RuntimeException to hide bugs.

Many modern designs favor unchecked exceptions to keep method signatures clean.

subtopics.name: Exception Wrapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is Exception Wrapping?

Exception wrapping means catching a low-level exception and rethrowing it inside a new, higher-level exception while preserving the original as the cause. This is done via the constructor or initCause().

2. Example

try {
    jdbcTemplate.query(...);
} catch (SQLException e) {
    throw new DataAccessException("Failed to load user", e); // wrap
}

3. Why Wrap?

  • Abstraction: callers deal with a meaningful domain exception instead of low-level details like SQLException.
  • Preserves the cause: the original stack trace is retained for debugging.
  • Decoupling: the caller isn't tied to the underlying technology.

Always pass the original exception as the cause — never swallow it — so root-cause analysis is possible.

subtopics.name: Chained Exceptions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Chained Exceptions?

Chained exceptions link one exception to another via the cause mechanism, forming a chain that traces how a low-level failure propagated up as a higher-level one.

2. How to Chain

catch (IOException e) {
    throw new ServiceException("Read failed", e); // e is the cause
}

// or explicitly
RuntimeException ex = new RuntimeException("failed");
ex.initCause(originalException);

3. Reading the Chain

  • getCause() returns the underlying exception.
  • Printed stack traces show Caused by: sections for each link.

Chaining preserves the full failure story — the top exception gives the business context while the cause chain reveals the technical root cause. This is the foundation of good root cause analysis.

subtopics.name: Root Cause Analysis
subtopics.content:
1. What is Root Cause Analysis?

Root cause analysis (RCA) in exception handling is the practice of tracing an exception chain down to the original, underlying cause rather than the surface-level symptom.

2. How to Find the Root Cause

Throwable cause = ex;
while (cause.getCause() != null) {
    cause = cause.getCause();
}
System.out.println("Root cause: " + cause);

Apache Commons Lang offers ExceptionUtils.getRootCause() for the same purpose.

3. Best Practices

  • Always preserve the cause when wrapping exceptions.
  • Read the bottom-most Caused by: in the stack trace — that is usually the true origin.
  • Log the full exception (with cause chain), not just the message.
  • Don't swallow exceptions with empty catch blocks — it destroys the trail.

Good RCA depends on properly chained exceptions and complete logging.

subtopics.name: StackTrace Analysis
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Stack Trace?

A stack trace is a snapshot of the method call stack at the moment an exception was thrown. It lists each frame — class, method, file, and line number — from the throw point back to the entry point.

2. How to Read It

  • The top line shows the exception type and message.
  • The first frame below it is where the exception was thrown.
  • Frames are listed most-recent first, going down toward the caller.
  • Caused by: sections reveal the chained root cause — read the deepest one.

3. Programmatic Access

StackTraceElement[] frames = ex.getStackTrace();
for (StackTraceElement f : frames) {
    System.out.println(f.getClassName() + "." +
        f.getMethodName() + ":" + f.getLineNumber());
}

Focus on frames in your own code first, then follow the cause chain. Filtering out framework noise speeds up debugging.

subtopics.name: Exception Translation Pattern
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Exception Translation Pattern?

The exception translation pattern converts a low-level exception into a higher-level one that is more meaningful to the caller, while preserving the original as the cause. It hides implementation details behind an abstraction.

2. Example

public User findUser(int id) {
    try {
        return dao.query(id);
    } catch (SQLException e) {
        // translate persistence detail into a domain exception
        throw new UserNotFoundException("User " + id + " not found", e);
    }
}

3. Why Use It?

  • Abstraction: callers depend on domain exceptions, not on JDBC/JPA specifics.
  • Layered design: each layer throws exceptions appropriate to its level.
  • Cause preservation: chaining keeps the root cause for debugging.

4. Real-World Use

Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy is a textbook example: it translates vendor-specific SQLExceptions into a consistent, unchecked exception hierarchy.

subtopics.name: Thread Lifecycle
subtopics.content:
1. Thread States

A Java thread moves through six states defined in Thread.State:

  • NEW: created but start() not yet called.
  • RUNNABLE: eligible to run; may be running or waiting for CPU.
  • BLOCKED: waiting to acquire a monitor lock.
  • WAITING: waiting indefinitely (wait(), join(), park()).
  • TIMED_WAITING: waiting for a bounded time (sleep(), wait(timeout)).
  • TERMINATED: finished execution.

2. Transitions

NEW --start()--> RUNNABLE
RUNNABLE --lock wait--> BLOCKED
RUNNABLE --wait()/join()--> WAITING
RUNNABLE --sleep(t)--> TIMED_WAITING
RUNNABLE --run() ends--> TERMINATED

Note that Java has no separate "running" state — running and ready-to-run are both RUNNABLE; the OS scheduler decides which runs.

subtopics.name: synchronized block vs method
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Both provide mutual exclusion, but they differ in lock scope:

  • A synchronized method locks the entire method — on this for instance methods, or on the Class object for static methods.
  • A synchronized block locks only a chosen section and lets you specify which object to lock on.

2. Example

// Method: locks whole method on 'this'
public synchronized void update() { ... }

// Block: locks only the critical section
public void update() {
    // non-critical work (concurrent)
    synchronized (lock) {
        // critical section only
    }
}

3. Which to Prefer

Synchronized blocks are generally preferred because they reduce the locked region, improving concurrency, and allow locking on a dedicated private lock object rather than exposing this.

subtopics.name: ReentrantLock
subtopics.content:
1. What is ReentrantLock?

ReentrantLock is an explicit lock from java.util.concurrent.locks that offers more flexibility than synchronized. "Reentrant" means the holding thread can acquire it multiple times.

2. Advantages over synchronized

  • tryLock(): attempt to acquire without blocking, optionally with a timeout.
  • Interruptible: lockInterruptibly() lets a waiting thread be interrupted.
  • Fairness: can be constructed fair (FIFO) to prevent starvation.
  • Condition objects: supports multiple wait-sets via newCondition().

3. Example

ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();
lock.lock();
try {
    // critical section
} finally {
    lock.unlock(); // always release in finally
}

The main caveat: you must manually unlock(), always in a finally block, or you risk a permanent lock leak.

subtopics.name: ReadWriteLock
subtopics.content:
1. What is ReadWriteLock?

ReadWriteLock maintains a pair of locks: a shared read lock and an exclusive write lock. Multiple readers can hold the read lock simultaneously, but the write lock is exclusive.

2. Rules

  • Many threads may read concurrently if no thread is writing.
  • Only one thread may write, and no reads are allowed during a write.

3. Example

ReadWriteLock rw = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

rw.readLock().lock();
try { /* read shared data */ } finally { rw.readLock().unlock(); }

rw.writeLock().lock();
try { /* modify shared data */ } finally { rw.writeLock().unlock(); }

4. When to Use

Ideal for read-heavy data structures like caches, where reads vastly outnumber writes, since it maximizes read concurrency.

subtopics.name: StampedLock
subtopics.content:
1. What is StampedLock?

StampedLock (Java 8) is a lock that returns a stamp (a long) on acquisition, used to release or validate the lock. It supports three modes: write, read, and optimistic read.

2. Optimistic Read

The key feature is optimistic reading: a thread reads without acquiring a lock, then validates the stamp. If a write occurred meanwhile, it falls back to a full read lock. This avoids lock contention when writes are rare. 

long stamp = lock.tryOptimisticRead();
int value = data; // read
if (!lock.validate(stamp)) {   // a write happened
    stamp = lock.readLock();
    try { value = data; } finally { lock.unlockRead(stamp); }
}

3. Caveats

  • Not reentrant — a thread can deadlock itself.
  • Does not support Conditions.
  • Usually outperforms ReentrantReadWriteLock in read-heavy workloads.

subtopics.name: CAS
subtopics.content:
1. What is CAS?

Compare-And-Swap (CAS) is an atomic hardware instruction that underlies lock-free concurrency. It takes three values: a memory location, an expected value, and a new value. It updates the location to the new value only if it currently equals the expected value.

2. How it works

if (memory == expected) {
    memory = newValue;   // done atomically by CPU
    return true;
}
return false; // retry with the fresh value

3. Where it's used

  • Atomic classes: AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference use CAS internally.
  • Concurrent collections and locks build on it.

4. ABA Problem

CAS can be fooled if a value changes from A → B → A; it looks unchanged. AtomicStampedReference solves this by attaching a version stamp. CAS enables high throughput but can waste CPU on retries under heavy contention.

subtopics.name: ThreadPoolExecutor
subtopics.content:
1. What is ThreadPoolExecutor?

ThreadPoolExecutor is the core implementation behind Java thread pools. It reuses a managed set of threads to execute submitted tasks, avoiding the cost of creating a thread per task.

2. Key Parameters

  • corePoolSize: threads kept alive even when idle.
  • maximumPoolSize: maximum threads allowed.
  • keepAliveTime: idle timeout for threads above the core size.
  • workQueue: queue holding pending tasks.
  • rejectedExecutionHandler: policy when the pool and queue are full.

3. Task Submission Flow

  1. If threads < core size, create a new thread.
  2. Else queue the task.
  3. If the queue is full and threads < max, create a new thread.
  4. Else apply the rejection policy (Abort, CallerRuns, Discard, DiscardOldest).

Creating pools explicitly (not via Executors) lets us bound the queue and avoid resource exhaustion.

subtopics.name: ScheduledExecutorService
subtopics.content:
1. What is ScheduledExecutorService?

ScheduledExecutorService extends ExecutorService to run tasks after a delay or periodically. It is the modern, robust replacement for Timer/TimerTask.

2. Key Methods

  • schedule(task, delay, unit) — run once after a delay.
  • scheduleAtFixedRate(task, initialDelay, period, unit) — runs at fixed intervals measured from each start time.
  • scheduleWithFixedDelay(task, initialDelay, delay, unit) — waits a fixed gap after each task completes.

3. fixedRate vs fixedDelay

  • fixedRate: period is between task start times; if a task overruns, the next may start immediately.
  • fixedDelay: gap is between task end and next start.

Unlike Timer, it uses a thread pool, so one failing task doesn't kill all scheduled tasks.

subtopics.name: thenApply()
subtopics.content:
1. What is thenApply()?

thenApply() is a CompletableFuture method that transforms the result of a completed stage using a Function, returning a new CompletableFuture with the transformed value.

2. Example

CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> 10)
    .thenApply(n -> n * 2)      // 20
    .thenApply(n -> "Value: " + n)
    .thenAccept(System.out::println);

3. thenApply vs thenCompose

  • thenApply — the function returns a plain value; result is CompletableFuture<R>.
  • thenCompose — the function itself returns a CompletableFuture; used to chain dependent async calls and avoid nesting (CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<R>>).

Think of thenApply as map and thenCompose as flatMap.

subtopics.name: thenCompose()
subtopics.content:
1. What is thenCompose()?

thenCompose() chains a stage whose function itself returns a CompletableFuture, flattening the result so you get CompletableFuture<R> instead of a nested future. It is the async equivalent of flatMap.

2. Example

CompletableFuture<User> userFuture = getUserAsync(id);

CompletableFuture<Account> accountFuture =
    userFuture.thenCompose(user -> getAccountAsync(user.getId()));

3. Why not thenApply here?

If we used thenApply(user -> getAccountAsync(...)), we would get CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<Account>> — a nested future that is awkward to unwrap. thenCompose flattens it into a single future.

Use it when the next step is another asynchronous operation that depends on the previous result.

subtopics.name: allOf()
subtopics.content:
1. What is allOf()?

CompletableFuture.allOf() returns a future that completes when all the given futures complete. It is used to wait for multiple parallel tasks to finish.

2. Example

CompletableFuture<String> f1 = fetchProfile();
CompletableFuture<String> f2 = fetchOrders();
CompletableFuture<String> f3 = fetchWishlist();

CompletableFuture<Void> all = CompletableFuture.allOf(f1, f2, f3);
all.join(); // wait for all

// Collect results after completion
String profile = f1.join();

3. Key Points

  • It returns CompletableFuture<Void> — you must read each future's result individually.
  • If any future completes exceptionally, allOf completes exceptionally too.
  • Great for fan-out patterns like aggregating results from several microservice calls concurrently.

subtopics.name: anyOf()
subtopics.content:
1. What is anyOf()?

CompletableFuture.anyOf() returns a future that completes as soon as any one of the given futures completes, carrying that first result.

2. Example

CompletableFuture<Object> fastest = CompletableFuture.anyOf(
    fetchFromCache(),
    fetchFromDb(),
    fetchFromApi());

System.out.println(fastest.join()); // whichever finished first

3. Key Points

  • Returns CompletableFuture<Object> since the winning type is not known in advance.
  • Useful for fastest-response patterns — querying redundant sources and taking whichever replies first.
  • If the first to complete does so exceptionally, anyOf completes exceptionally.

subtopics.name: CountDownLatch
subtopics.content:
1. What is CountDownLatch?

CountDownLatch lets one or more threads wait until a set of operations in other threads completes. It is initialized with a count; threads call countDown(), and waiters block on await() until the count reaches zero.

2. Example

CountDownLatch latch = new CountDownLatch(3);

for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    new Thread(() -> {
        doWork();
        latch.countDown();
    }).start();
}

latch.await(); // main thread waits for all 3
System.out.println("All done");

3. Key Point

A CountDownLatch is one-time use — once the count hits zero it cannot be reset. For a reusable barrier, use CyclicBarrier. It is ideal for "wait until N services are up" or "wait for all workers to finish" scenarios.

subtopics.name: CyclicBarrier
subtopics.content:
1. What is CyclicBarrier?

CyclicBarrier makes a fixed number of threads wait for each other at a common barrier point. When all parties call await(), they are all released together.

2. Example

CyclicBarrier barrier = new CyclicBarrier(3,
    () -> System.out.println("All threads reached barrier"));

Runnable task = () -> {
    doPhase();
    barrier.await(); // wait for the other two
};

3. CyclicBarrier vs CountDownLatch

  • Reusable: a CyclicBarrier resets automatically after tripping; a latch does not.
  • Symmetric waiting: all threads wait for each other, versus a latch where waiters differ from counters.
  • Supports an optional barrier action that runs once when the barrier trips.

Useful in iterative parallel algorithms where threads must sync at the end of each phase.

subtopics.name: Semaphore
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Semaphore?

A Semaphore maintains a set of permits to control how many threads can access a resource at once. Threads call acquire() to take a permit and release() to return it.

2. Example

Semaphore sem = new Semaphore(3); // allow 3 at a time

sem.acquire();
try {
    accessResource();
} finally {
    sem.release();
}

3. Key Points

  • A binary semaphore (1 permit) acts like a lock.
  • Can be fair (FIFO) to prevent starvation.
  • Unlike a lock, a permit released by one thread can be acquired by another — it isn't tied to ownership.

Commonly used for rate limiting, connection pools, and bounding concurrent access to a scarce resource.

subtopics.name: Phaser
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Phaser?

Phaser is a flexible, reusable synchronization barrier (Java 7) that supports a dynamic number of parties and multiple phases. It combines and generalizes CyclicBarrier and CountDownLatch.

2. Key Features

  • Parties can register and deregister dynamically at runtime.
  • Supports multi-phase execution — threads sync at the end of each phase.
  • arriveAndAwaitAdvance() waits for all parties; arriveAndDeregister() leaves the phaser.

3. Example

Phaser phaser = new Phaser(1); // register self
for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
    phaser.register();
    new Thread(() -> {
        doPhase();
        phaser.arriveAndAwaitAdvance();
    }).start();
}
phaser.arriveAndDeregister();

Use it when the number of participating threads is not fixed upfront or when you need repeated multi-phase coordination.

subtopics.name: ThreadLocal
subtopics.content:
1. What is ThreadLocal?

ThreadLocal provides each thread with its own independent copy of a variable. Threads cannot see each other's value, giving thread confinement without synchronization.

2. Example

private static final ThreadLocal<SimpleDateFormat> formatter =
    ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new SimpleDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd"));

String date = formatter.get().format(new Date());

3. Use Cases

  • Holding non-thread-safe objects like SimpleDateFormat.
  • Storing per-request context (user, transaction ID) in web apps.

4. Caution: Memory Leaks

In thread pools, threads are reused, so a ThreadLocal value can linger across requests and leak memory (or leak data between requests). Always call remove() in a finally block when done.

subtopics.name: ForkJoinPool
subtopics.content:
1. What is ForkJoinPool?

ForkJoinPool (Java 7) is designed for divide-and-conquer parallelism. It recursively splits a task into subtasks (fork), executes them in parallel, and combines the results (join).

2. Work Stealing

Each worker thread has its own deque of tasks. Idle threads steal tasks from the tail of busy threads' deques, keeping all cores busy and balancing load automatically.

3. Example

class SumTask extends RecursiveTask<Long> {
    protected Long compute() {
        if (small enough) return computeDirectly();
        SumTask left = new SumTask(...);
        left.fork();               // async
        SumTask right = new SumTask(...);
        long r = right.compute();
        return left.join() + r;
    }
}

Parallel streams use the shared ForkJoinPool.commonPool() under the hood. Tasks extend RecursiveTask (returns a value) or RecursiveAction (no result).

subtopics.name: Work Stealing Pool
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Work Stealing Pool?

A work-stealing pool is a thread pool where each worker thread keeps its own task queue (a deque). When a thread finishes its own tasks, it steals pending tasks from other threads' queues to stay busy.

2. How Stealing Works

  • A thread pushes and pops its own tasks from the head of its deque (LIFO, cache-friendly).
  • A stealing thread takes from the tail of another's deque, minimizing contention.

3. In Java

ExecutorService pool = Executors.newWorkStealingPool();

This factory returns a ForkJoinPool sized to the available processors. The design gives excellent load balancing and throughput for many small, independent (or recursively split) tasks, which is why it powers parallel streams.

subtopics.name: Starvation
subtopics.content:
1. What is Starvation?

Starvation occurs when a thread is perpetually denied access to resources (usually CPU time or a lock) and therefore cannot make progress, because other threads keep monopolizing them.

2. Common Causes

  • High-priority threads continuously preempting low-priority ones.
  • Unfair locks where the same threads keep re-acquiring a lock.
  • Long-held locks or a thread holding a monitor indefinitely.

3. How to Prevent It

  • Use fair locks (e.g., new ReentrantLock(true)) or fair semaphores that grant access in FIFO order.
  • Avoid holding locks longer than necessary.
  • Use bounded task execution and avoid priority extremes.

Starvation differs from deadlock: a starved thread could run but never gets the chance, whereas deadlocked threads are permanently blocked waiting on each other.

subtopics.name: Livelock
subtopics.content:
1. What is Livelock?

Livelock occurs when two or more threads are actively responding to each other and keep changing state in reaction, but none makes real progress. Unlike deadlock, the threads are not blocked — they are busy but stuck.

2. Analogy

Two people in a hallway step aside to let the other pass, both step the same way repeatedly, and neither ever gets through.

3. Common Cause

Deadlock-avoidance logic where threads repeatedly release and retry a lock in response to each other — for example, both back off and retry in lockstep forever.

4. How to Prevent It

  • Introduce randomized back-off so threads don't retry in sync.
  • Impose an ordering or priority to break the symmetry.
  • Cap retries and escalate to a different strategy.

Deadlock = stuck and idle; Livelock = stuck but busy; Starvation = one thread never gets a turn.

subtopics.name: What is Spring Framework?
subtopics.content:
1. What is Spring Framework?

Spring is a lightweight, open-source Java framework for building enterprise applications. Its core is an IoC (Inversion of Control) container that manages the lifecycle and wiring of application objects (beans).

2. Key Features

  • Dependency Injection for loose coupling.
  • AOP for cross-cutting concerns like logging and transactions.
  • Abstractions for data access, transactions, MVC web, security, and messaging.
  • Integration with countless third-party libraries.

3. Why it matters

Spring removes boilerplate, promotes testable and modular design, and is non-invasive — your business classes usually stay plain POJOs. It is the foundation on which Spring Boot and the wider Spring ecosystem are built.

subtopics.name: Why Spring?
subtopics.content:
Why do we use Spring?

  • Loose coupling: Dependency Injection wires objects through abstractions, making code flexible and testable.
  • Less boilerplate: Spring manages object creation, transactions, and resource handling for us.
  • Modularity: You use only the modules you need (Core, MVC, Data, Security, etc.).
  • AOP: Cross-cutting concerns are handled declaratively instead of scattered through code.
  • Testability: POJO-based beans and DI make unit testing straightforward with mocks.
  • Ecosystem: Spring Boot, Spring Data, Spring Security, and Spring Cloud cover almost every enterprise need.

In short, Spring lets developers focus on business logic while the framework handles infrastructure concerns.

subtopics.name: Spring Architecture
subtopics.content:
1. Layered Architecture

Spring is organized into modules grouped into layers, all built around the Core Container.

2. Main Layers/Modules

  • Core Container: Core, Beans, Context, and SpEL — provides IoC and DI.
  • Data Access/Integration: JDBC, ORM, Transactions, JMS.
  • Web: Spring MVC, WebFlux, WebSocket.
  • AOP: aspect-oriented programming support.
  • Test: testing support for Spring components.

3. Key Point

The BeanFactory/ApplicationContext at the center manages beans, while other modules layer functionality on top. This modular design lets applications include only what they need.

subtopics.name: IoC (Inversion of Control)
subtopics.content:
1. What is IoC?

Inversion of Control is a design principle where the control of creating and wiring objects is inverted from the application to a container. Instead of an object creating its own dependencies with new, the container provides them.

2. IoC vs DI

  • IoC is the broad principle — "don't call us, we'll call you."
  • Dependency Injection is the most common way Spring implements IoC.

3. The IoC Container

In Spring, the IoC container (ApplicationContext) reads configuration (annotations, Java config, or XML), instantiates beans, injects their dependencies, and manages their lifecycle.

Benefit: loose coupling, easier testing, and centralized configuration.

subtopics.name: Dependency Injection
subtopics.content:
1. What is Dependency Injection?

Dependency Injection (DI) is a technique where an object's dependencies are provided from the outside (by the container) rather than created by the object itself. It is Spring's implementation of IoC.

2. Types in Spring

  • Constructor Injection: dependencies passed via the constructor (preferred).
  • Setter Injection: dependencies set via setter methods (good for optional deps).
  • Field Injection: injected directly into fields with @Autowired (concise but hard to test).

3. Example

@Service
class OrderService {
    private final PaymentService payment;
    OrderService(PaymentService payment) { // constructor injection
        this.payment = payment;
    }
}

DI reduces coupling, improves testability (easy mocking), and follows the Dependency Inversion Principle.

subtopics.name: Constructor vs Setter Injection
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Constructor InjectionSetter Injection
Dependencies via constructorDependencies via setter methods
Best for mandatory dependenciesBest for optional dependencies
Allows final fields (immutable)Fields cannot be final
Object fully initialized at creationCan be changed after creation

2. Which to Prefer

Constructor injection is the recommended approach because it:

  • Guarantees required dependencies are present at construction.
  • Enables immutability with final fields.
  • Makes dependencies explicit and improves testability.
  • Helps detect circular dependencies early.

Setter injection is useful when a dependency is genuinely optional or must be reconfigurable.

subtopics.name: Bean Lifecycle
subtopics.content:
1. Spring Bean Lifecycle

The container manages a bean from instantiation to destruction through these stages:

  1. Instantiation — the bean object is created.
  2. Populate properties — dependencies are injected.
  3. Aware callbacks — e.g., BeanNameAware, ApplicationContextAware.
  4. BeanPostProcessor before-init — postProcessBeforeInitialization().
  5. Initialization — @PostConstruct, InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(), custom init method.
  6. BeanPostProcessor after-init — postProcessAfterInitialization() (where proxies are created).
  7. Bean ready for use.
  8. Destruction — @PreDestroy, DisposableBean.destroy(), custom destroy method.

2. Key Point

We hook into the lifecycle using @PostConstruct/@PreDestroy or the corresponding interfaces to run setup/cleanup logic.

subtopics.name: Bean Scopes
subtopics.content:
1. What are Bean Scopes?

A scope defines how many instances of a bean the container creates and how long they live.

2. Available Scopes

  • singleton (default): one shared instance per container.
  • prototype: a new instance every time the bean is requested.
  • request: one instance per HTTP request (web).
  • session: one instance per HTTP session (web).
  • application: one instance per ServletContext.
  • websocket: one instance per WebSocket session.

3. Example

@Component
@Scope("prototype")
class Task { }

Singleton is best for stateless services; prototype suits stateful, short-lived objects.

subtopics.name: Singleton Bean
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Singleton Bean?

The singleton scope is Spring's default. The container creates exactly one instance per ApplicationContext and returns that same shared instance for every injection or lookup.

2. Key Points

  • Created eagerly at container startup (by default).
  • Shared across the whole application, so it should ideally be stateless.
  • Spring's singleton is per-container, not the classic GoF JVM-wide singleton.

3. Thread Safety Caution

Because one instance is shared across threads, mutable instance state can cause race conditions. Keep singletons stateless, or synchronize/inject request-scoped state where needed.

subtopics.name: Prototype Bean
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Prototype Bean?

A prototype-scoped bean produces a new instance every time it is requested from the container or injected.

2. Key Points

  • Suited for stateful or short-lived objects.
  • Spring does not manage the full lifecycle — it creates and configures the bean but does not call destruction callbacks (@PreDestroy is not invoked).

3. Singleton Injecting Prototype Problem

If a singleton injects a prototype, only one prototype instance is created (at singleton creation) and reused — defeating the purpose. Solutions:

  • ObjectProvider / ObjectFactory.
  • @Lookup method injection.
  • Scoped proxies.

subtopics.name: @Component vs @Bean
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

@Component@Bean
Class-level annotationMethod-level annotation
Auto-detected via component scanningDeclared explicitly in a @Configuration class
Spring instantiates the classYou control instantiation in the method body
For your own classesIdeal for third-party classes you can't annotate

2. Examples

@Component
class MyService { }

@Configuration
class AppConfig {
    @Bean
    ObjectMapper objectMapper() {
        return new ObjectMapper(); // third-party class
    }
}

3. When to Use

Use @Component (and its stereotypes @Service, @Repository, @Controller) for your own classes; use @Bean when you need custom construction logic or must register a library class.

subtopics.name: @ComponentScan
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ComponentScan?

@ComponentScan tells Spring which packages to scan for classes annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller, so they can be registered as beans automatically.

2. Example

@Configuration
@ComponentScan(basePackages = "com.app.services")
class AppConfig { }

3. Key Points

  • By default it scans the package of the annotated class and all sub-packages.
  • @SpringBootApplication includes @ComponentScan, which is why Boot auto-detects beans in and below the main class's package.
  • Filters (includeFilters/excludeFilters) can fine-tune what is scanned.

Placing the main application class in a root package ensures all components are discovered.

subtopics.name: @Autowired
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Autowired?

@Autowired tells Spring to inject a dependency automatically by type. It can be applied to constructors, setters, or fields.

2. How Resolution Works

  • Spring matches by type first.
  • If multiple candidates exist, it narrows by @Qualifier or @Primary, then by bean name.
  • If none is found and required=true (default), it throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException.

3. Example

@Service
class OrderService {
    private final PaymentService payment;
    @Autowired // optional on a single constructor since Spring 4.3
    OrderService(PaymentService payment) {
        this.payment = payment;
    }
}

For a class with a single constructor, @Autowired is optional. Constructor injection is preferred over field injection.

subtopics.name: @Qualifier
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Qualifier?

@Qualifier resolves ambiguity when multiple beans of the same type exist. It tells Spring exactly which bean to inject by name.

2. Example

@Component("emailService")
class EmailService implements Notifier { }

@Component("smsService")
class SmsService implements Notifier { }

@Service
class AlertService {
    AlertService(@Qualifier("smsService") Notifier notifier) { ... }
}

3. @Qualifier vs @Primary

  • @Primary sets a default bean when several match.
  • @Qualifier is explicit at the injection point and overrides @Primary.

Use @Primary for the common default and @Qualifier to pick a specific alternative.

subtopics.name: @Primary
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Primary?

@Primary marks one bean as the default choice when multiple candidates of the same type are available for injection.

2. Example

@Component
@Primary
class EmailService implements Notifier { }

@Component
class SmsService implements Notifier { }

@Service
class AlertService {
    // Injects EmailService because it is @Primary
    AlertService(Notifier notifier) { ... }
}

3. Key Points

  • Applied at the bean definition, not the injection point.
  • A more specific @Qualifier at the injection point overrides @Primary.
  • Only one bean per type should be marked @Primary.

subtopics.name: Circular Dependency
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Circular Dependency?

A circular dependency occurs when two or more beans depend on each other — e.g., bean A needs B and B needs A. Spring cannot fully construct either without the other.

2. Behavior by Injection Type

  • Constructor injection: fails at startup with BeanCurrentlyInCreationException — neither bean can be built first.
  • Setter/field injection: Spring can resolve it using an early bean reference (exposing a partially-created singleton).

3. How to Fix

  • Redesign to remove the cycle (best solution — often a sign of poor separation).
  • Use @Lazy on one dependency to defer its creation.
  • Switch one side to setter injection.
  • Introduce an intermediate service or use events.

Since Spring Boot 2.6, circular references are prohibited by default and must be explicitly allowed — pushing developers to fix the design.

subtopics.name: Lazy Initialization
subtopics.content:
1. What is Lazy Initialization?

With lazy initialization, a bean is created only when it is first requested, rather than at container startup. It is enabled with @Lazy.

2. Example

@Component
@Lazy
class HeavyService { }

// Globally in Spring Boot:
// spring.main.lazy-initialization=true

3. Pros and Cons

  • Pros: faster startup, saves memory for rarely-used beans.
  • Cons: first request is slower; configuration errors surface at runtime instead of startup.

Use it for expensive, seldom-used beans, but keep critical beans eager so startup validates the wiring.

subtopics.name: Eager Initialization
subtopics.content:
1. What is Eager Initialization?

Eager initialization is Spring's default for singleton beans — all singletons are created and wired at container startup, before the application begins serving requests.

2. Benefits

  • Fail fast: misconfigurations and missing dependencies are detected at startup, not at runtime.
  • Fast first request: beans are already ready when needed.

3. Eager vs Lazy

  • Eager (default): created at startup — safer, slower boot.
  • Lazy (@Lazy): created on first use — faster boot, deferred error detection.

Eager loading is generally preferred so the application validates its full configuration during startup.

subtopics.name: ApplicationContext vs BeanFactory
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Both are Spring IoC containers, but ApplicationContext is a superset of BeanFactory with enterprise features.

2. Comparison

BeanFactoryApplicationContext
Basic containerAdvanced container
Lazy bean creationEager (pre-instantiates singletons)
No built-in i18n/eventsSupports i18n, events, annotations
Manual BeanPostProcessor registrationAuto-registers post-processors

3. Which to Use

ApplicationContext is used in almost all real applications (Spring Boot uses it). BeanFactory is a low-level interface, useful only in very memory-constrained scenarios.

subtopics.name: Spring Modules
subtopics.content:
Core Spring Modules

  • Core & Beans: the IoC container and DI foundation.
  • Context: ApplicationContext, events, i18n.
  • SpEL: Spring Expression Language.
  • AOP: aspect-oriented programming.
  • JDBC & ORM: data access and Hibernate/JPA integration.
  • Transactions: declarative and programmatic transaction management.
  • Web MVC / WebFlux: servlet-based and reactive web frameworks.
  • Test: integration and unit testing support.

Broader Ecosystem

  • Spring Boot — auto-configuration and rapid setup.
  • Spring Data — repository abstractions.
  • Spring Security — authentication and authorization.
  • Spring Cloud — microservices infrastructure.

The modular design lets applications include only what they need.

subtopics.name: What is Spring Boot?
subtopics.content:
1. What is Spring Boot?

Spring Boot is a framework built on top of Spring that simplifies application setup through auto-configuration, starter dependencies, and embedded servers. It lets you create production-ready, standalone applications with minimal configuration.

2. Key Features

  • Auto-configuration: configures beans based on classpath and properties.
  • Starters: curated dependency bundles.
  • Embedded servers: Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow — no external deployment.
  • Actuator: production monitoring endpoints.
  • No XML: convention over configuration.

3. Why it matters

It removes boilerplate and lets developers focus on business logic, packaging apps as runnable fat JARs — ideal for microservices.

subtopics.name: Advantages of Spring Boot
subtopics.content:
Advantages of Spring Boot

  • Rapid development: auto-configuration and starters remove boilerplate setup.
  • Standalone apps: embedded servers mean no separate WAR deployment.
  • Opinionated defaults: sensible configuration out of the box, overridable when needed.
  • Production-ready: Actuator provides health, metrics, and monitoring.
  • Easy dependency management: starters manage compatible versions.
  • Microservice-friendly: fat JARs and externalized config suit cloud deployments.
  • Strong ecosystem: integrates seamlessly with Spring Data, Security, Cloud.

In short, Spring Boot maximizes developer productivity while producing deployable, observable applications.

subtopics.name: Spring Boot Architecture
subtopics.content:
1. Layered Architecture

A typical Spring Boot application is organized into layers:

  • Controller (Presentation): handles HTTP requests/responses.
  • Service (Business): contains business logic.
  • Repository (Persistence): data access via Spring Data JPA.
  • Model/Entity: domain objects.

2. Core Building Blocks

  • Starters: dependency bundles.
  • Auto-configuration: conditional bean setup.
  • Embedded server: serves the app.
  • Spring Boot CLI/Actuator: tooling and monitoring.

3. Flow

A request hits the embedded server → DispatcherServlet → Controller → Service → Repository → Database, and the response travels back up. Auto-configuration wires the beans behind the scenes.

subtopics.name: Auto Configuration
subtopics.content:
1. What is Auto-Configuration?

Auto-configuration automatically configures Spring beans based on the classpath dependencies, existing beans, and property settings — so you don't have to configure everything manually.

2. How it works

  • Triggered by @EnableAutoConfiguration (included in @SpringBootApplication).
  • Spring reads auto-configuration classes listed in META-INF/spring/...AutoConfiguration.imports.
  • Each uses @Conditional annotations (e.g., @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean) to decide whether to apply.

3. Example

If spring-boot-starter-data-jpa and an H2 driver are on the classpath, Boot auto-configures a DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, and TransactionManager — unless you define your own.

Use --debug to see the auto-configuration report showing what matched and why.

subtopics.name: @SpringBootApplication
subtopics.content:
1. What is @SpringBootApplication?

@SpringBootApplication is a convenience annotation placed on the main class. It is a meta-annotation combining three:

  • @SpringBootConfiguration — marks it as a configuration class.
  • @EnableAutoConfiguration — enables auto-configuration.
  • @ComponentScan — scans the package and sub-packages for components.

2. Example

@SpringBootApplication
public class App {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(App.class, args);
    }
}

3. Key Point

Place the main class in a root package so component scanning discovers all beans. You can customize scanning or exclude auto-configs via its attributes (e.g., exclude, scanBasePackages).

subtopics.name: Spring Boot Starters
subtopics.content:
1. What are Starters?

Starters are curated dependency descriptors that bundle commonly-used libraries for a feature, with compatible versions managed for you.

2. Common Starters

  • spring-boot-starter-web — MVC, REST, embedded Tomcat.
  • spring-boot-starter-data-jpa — JPA + Hibernate.
  • spring-boot-starter-security — Spring Security.
  • spring-boot-starter-test — JUnit, Mockito, etc.
  • spring-boot-starter-actuator — monitoring endpoints.

3. Benefit

Instead of manually adding many dependencies and matching versions, you add one starter. This eliminates dependency conflicts and simplifies the build. You can also create custom starters for reusable internal modules.

subtopics.name: application.properties vs application.yml
subtopics.content:
1. Both Configure the App

Both files externalize configuration; they differ only in format.

2. Comparison

application.propertiesapplication.yml
Key=value flat formatHierarchical YAML format
Repetitive prefixesNested, less repetition
Simple, no indentation issuesCleaner for complex config, indentation-sensitive

3. Example

# properties
spring.datasource.url=jdbc:mysql://localhost/db
spring.datasource.username=root

# yml
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:mysql://localhost/db
    username: root

YAML is preferred for large, nested configurations; properties for simple ones. Both support profiles.

subtopics.name: Profiles
subtopics.content:
1. What are Profiles?

Profiles let you define environment-specific configurations and beans (e.g., dev, test, prod) and activate the right set at runtime.

2. Profile-Specific Files

application-dev.yml
application-prod.yml
# activate:
spring.profiles.active=prod

3. Profile-Specific Beans

@Bean
@Profile("dev")
DataSource devDataSource() { ... }

@Bean
@Profile("prod")
DataSource prodDataSource() { ... }

4. Activation Options

  • Property: spring.profiles.active=prod.
  • Env variable: SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod.
  • Command line: --spring.profiles.active=prod.

Profiles make the same build deployable across environments without code changes.

subtopics.name: External Configuration
subtopics.content:
1. What is External Configuration?

Spring Boot lets you configure an app from sources outside the code, so the same artifact runs in different environments. Values are read from many sources with a defined precedence.

2. Precedence (highest first, simplified)

  1. Command-line arguments.
  2. OS environment variables / JVM system properties.
  3. application-{profile}.properties/yml.
  4. application.properties/yml.
  5. Default values in @Value/@ConfigurationProperties.

3. Why it matters

  • Follows the 12-Factor App principle of config in the environment.
  • Secrets (DB passwords, API keys) stay out of the codebase.
  • Enables one build, many environments.

Bind values with @Value for single properties or @ConfigurationProperties for grouped, type-safe config.

subtopics.name: CommandLineRunner
subtopics.content:
1. What is CommandLineRunner?

CommandLineRunner is a functional interface whose run(String... args) method executes once after the application context is fully initialized, just before startup completes.

2. Example

@Component
class StartupTask implements CommandLineRunner {
    @Override
    public void run(String... args) {
        System.out.println("App started, running init logic");
    }
}

3. Use Cases

  • Seeding initial data.
  • Warming up caches.
  • Running one-time startup logic or diagnostics.

It receives raw string arguments. If you need parsed options, use ApplicationRunner instead. Multiple runners can be ordered with @Order.

subtopics.name: ApplicationRunner
subtopics.content:
1. What is ApplicationRunner?

ApplicationRunner is like CommandLineRunner — it runs once after startup — but its run method receives an ApplicationArguments object that provides parsed access to command-line arguments.

2. Example

@Component
class Runner implements ApplicationRunner {
    @Override
    public void run(ApplicationArguments args) {
        boolean debug = args.containsOption("debug");
        List<String> files = args.getOptionValues("file");
    }
}

3. CommandLineRunner vs ApplicationRunner

  • CommandLineRunner: raw String... arguments.
  • ApplicationRunner: structured ApplicationArguments with option parsing.

Use ApplicationRunner when you need to interpret named options like --file=x --debug.

subtopics.name: Spring Boot DevTools
subtopics.content:
1. What is DevTools?

spring-boot-devtools is a development-time module that speeds up the dev loop with automatic restarts and other conveniences.

2. Key Features

  • Automatic restart: restarts the app when classpath files change.
  • LiveReload: auto-refreshes the browser on resource changes.
  • Sensible dev defaults: disables template/resource caching.
  • Remote debugging support.

3. Key Point

DevTools uses two classloaders — a base loader for unchanging library classes and a restart loader for your code — so restarts are much faster than a full JVM boot. It is automatically disabled in production (e.g., when running from a fully packaged JAR), so it never affects deployed apps.

subtopics.name: Spring Boot Actuator
subtopics.content:
1. What is Actuator?

Spring Boot Actuator adds production-ready endpoints to monitor and manage a running application — health, metrics, environment, and more — with minimal configuration.

2. Common Endpoints

  • /actuator/health — application health status.
  • /actuator/metrics — JVM, HTTP, and custom metrics.
  • /actuator/info — build/app info.
  • /actuator/env — configuration properties.
  • /actuator/loggers — view/change log levels at runtime.

3. Configuration

management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics

By default only health is exposed over HTTP. Actuator integrates with Micrometer to publish metrics to Prometheus, Grafana, etc. Secure the endpoints in production.

subtopics.name: Health Checks
subtopics.content:
1. What are Health Checks?

The Actuator /actuator/health endpoint reports whether the application and its dependencies (database, disk, message brokers) are healthy. It aggregates results from multiple HealthIndicators.

2. Status Values

  • UP, DOWN, OUT_OF_SERVICE, UNKNOWN.
  • The overall status is the worst of all indicators.

3. Custom Health Indicator

@Component
class MyHealth implements HealthIndicator {
    public Health health() {
        return check() ? Health.up().build()
                       : Health.down().withDetail("error", "x").build();
    }
}

4. Kubernetes Probes

Boot exposes liveness and readiness groups (/actuator/health/liveness, /readiness) that map directly to Kubernetes probes for orchestrated deployments.

subtopics.name: Custom Actuator Endpoint
subtopics.content:
1. Creating a Custom Endpoint

You can expose your own management endpoint using @Endpoint and operation annotations.

2. Example

@Component
@Endpoint(id = "features")
class FeatureEndpoint {

    @ReadOperation
    public Map<String,Boolean> features() {
        return Map.of("beta", true, "newUI", false);
    }

    @WriteOperation
    public void toggle(String name, boolean enabled) { ... }
}

3. Operation Annotations

  • @ReadOperation → HTTP GET.
  • @WriteOperation → HTTP POST.
  • @DeleteOperation → HTTP DELETE.

Expose it via management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=features. It becomes accessible at /actuator/features. Custom endpoints are useful for domain-specific operational controls.

subtopics.name: Embedded Tomcat
subtopics.content:
1. What is Embedded Tomcat?

Spring Boot ships with an embedded servlet container (Tomcat by default) bundled inside the application, so the app runs as a standalone JAR without deploying a WAR to an external server.

2. Benefits

  • Run with a simple java -jar app.jar.
  • No external server installation or WAR deployment.
  • Consistent behavior across environments — the server is part of the artifact.
  • Ideal for microservices and containerized deployments.

3. Switching / Configuring

# change port
server.port=9090

# to use Jetty/Undertow instead of Tomcat, exclude tomcat
# starter and add the alternative starter

Provided by spring-boot-starter-web. You can still build a traditional WAR if an external server is required.

subtopics.name: Fat Jar
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Fat JAR?

A fat JAR (or uber JAR) is a single executable JAR that packages your application code plus all its dependencies and the embedded server, so it can run standalone.

2. Structure

Spring Boot uses a special nested layout: your classes under BOOT-INF/classes, dependency JARs under BOOT-INF/lib, and a custom JarLauncher that loads nested JARs. It is built by the spring-boot-maven-plugin/Gradle plugin.

3. Running It

java -jar myapp.jar

4. Benefit

One self-contained artifact means simple deployment and consistent runtime — perfect for containers and cloud. The trade-off is a larger file; layered JARs and Docker layer caching mitigate this.

subtopics.name: Banner Customization
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Banner?

The banner is the ASCII art printed at application startup. Spring Boot lets you customize or disable it.

2. Custom Banner

Create src/main/resources/banner.txt. It supports placeholders like ${application.version} and ${spring-boot.version}, plus ANSI colors. 

My App ${application.version}
Powered by Spring Boot ${spring-boot.version}

3. Disabling / Configuring

# disable
spring.main.banner-mode=off

# programmatically
new SpringApplicationBuilder(App.class)
    .bannerMode(Banner.Mode.OFF)
    .run(args);

Banner modes are CONSOLE, LOG, and OFF. It is a minor but common branding customization.

subtopics.name: Logging in Spring Boot
subtopics.content:
1. Default Logging

Spring Boot uses SLF4J as the logging facade with Logback as the default implementation, configured automatically.

2. Configuring Log Levels

logging.level.root=INFO
logging.level.com.app=DEBUG
logging.file.name=app.log
logging.pattern.console=%d %-5level %logger - %msg%n

3. Custom Configuration

  • Add logback-spring.xml for full Logback control (supports profiles).
  • Switch to Log4j2 by excluding the default logging starter and adding spring-boot-starter-log4j2.

4. Best Practices

  • Use SLF4J API (private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(...)).
  • Use parameterized logging (log.info("user {}", id)) to avoid string concatenation.
  • Never log sensitive data.

subtopics.name: Environment Variables
subtopics.content:
1. Using Environment Variables

Spring Boot automatically maps OS environment variables to configuration properties, which is essential for containerized and cloud deployments.

2. Relaxed Binding

A property like spring.datasource.url can be supplied as the environment variable SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL — dots become underscores and case is uppercased.

3. Accessing Values

@Value("${DB_PASSWORD}")
private String dbPassword;

// or via the Environment abstraction
@Autowired Environment env;
String v = env.getProperty("DB_PASSWORD");

4. Why it matters

Environment variables keep secrets and environment-specific values out of the codebase and let the same build run anywhere — a core 12-Factor principle. They also take precedence over property files.

subtopics.name: ConfigurationProperties
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ConfigurationProperties?

@ConfigurationProperties binds a group of related properties to a strongly-typed Java object, giving type-safe configuration.

2. Example

@Component
@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "app.mail")
class MailProps {
    private String host;
    private int port;
    // getters and setters
}

# application.yml
app:
  mail:
    host: smtp.example.com
    port: 587

3. @ConfigurationProperties vs @Value

  • @ConfigurationProperties: binds many properties, supports nesting, validation (@Validated), and relaxed binding.
  • @Value: injects a single property, supports SpEL.

Use @ConfigurationProperties for grouped configuration; it is cleaner and validatable.

subtopics.name: @Value
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Value?

@Value injects a single property value into a field, constructor parameter, or method, using property placeholders or SpEL.

2. Examples

@Value("${server.port}")
private int port;

@Value("${app.name:DefaultApp}") // with default
private String name;

@Value("#{2 * 60}") // SpEL expression
private int timeout;

3. Key Points

  • ${...} resolves property placeholders; #{...} evaluates SpEL.
  • A default after : avoids failures when the property is missing.
  • Best for one-off values; for grouped config prefer @ConfigurationProperties.

subtopics.name: Spring Boot Lifecycle
subtopics.content:
1. Application Startup Lifecycle

When SpringApplication.run() executes, Boot goes through an ordered startup sequence:

  1. Create the SpringApplication and determine the application type.
  2. Fire ApplicationStartingEvent and prepare the Environment.
  3. Print the banner.
  4. Create and configure the ApplicationContext.
  5. Run ApplicationContextInitializers and load bean definitions.
  6. Refresh the context — instantiate singletons, apply auto-configuration.
  7. Start the embedded server.
  8. Run CommandLineRunner/ApplicationRunner beans.
