  9. Fire ApplicationReadyEvent.

2. Key Events

You can hook into ApplicationStartingEvent, ApplicationEnvironmentPreparedEvent, ApplicationReadyEvent, and ApplicationFailedEvent via listeners to run custom logic at each phase.

subtopics.name: Graceful Shutdown
subtopics.content:
1. What is Graceful Shutdown?

Graceful shutdown lets the application finish in-flight requests before stopping, instead of terminating abruptly and dropping active connections.

2. Enabling It

server.shutdown=graceful
spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=30s

On shutdown, the server stops accepting new requests and waits (up to the timeout) for existing ones to complete.

3. Cleanup Hooks

  • @PreDestroy methods run during shutdown.
  • DisposableBean.destroy() is invoked.
  • Register a JVM shutdown hook via the context if needed.

Graceful shutdown is important in Kubernetes and load-balanced environments to avoid failed requests during rolling deployments.

subtopics.name: Spring Boot Best Practices
subtopics.content:
Spring Boot Best Practices

  • Layered structure: keep controller, service, and repository responsibilities separate.
  • Constructor injection: prefer it for immutability and testability.
  • Externalize config: use profiles and environment variables; keep secrets out of code.
  • Type-safe config: use @ConfigurationProperties over scattered @Value.
  • Global exception handling: use @ControllerAdvice for consistent error responses.
  • Use DTOs: don't expose JPA entities directly in APIs.
  • Validation: validate inputs with @Valid.
  • Observability: enable Actuator, structured logging, and metrics.
  • Secure endpoints: lock down Actuator and APIs in production.
  • Write tests: leverage spring-boot-starter-test and slice tests (@WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest).

Following these keeps the application maintainable, secure, and production-ready.

subtopics.name: Bean Creation Process
subtopics.content:
1. Bean Creation Steps

The container creates a bean through a well-defined sequence:

  1. Load definition: read bean metadata from annotations/config.
  2. Instantiate: call the constructor (resolving constructor DI).
  3. Populate properties: inject setter/field dependencies.
  4. Aware callbacks: BeanNameAware, ApplicationContextAware, etc.
  5. BeanPostProcessor before-init.
  6. Initialization: @PostConstruct, afterPropertiesSet(), custom init.
  7. BeanPostProcessor after-init: proxies (AOP) applied here.
  8. Ready for use.

2. Key Point

Understanding this order explains where AOP proxies are created (after initialization) and where you can hook custom logic (post-processors and init callbacks).

subtopics.name: BeanPostProcessor
subtopics.content:
1. What is BeanPostProcessor?

BeanPostProcessor is an extension point that lets you intercept and modify every bean after instantiation but around its initialization. It has two callbacks:

  • postProcessBeforeInitialization() — runs before init callbacks.
  • postProcessAfterInitialization() — runs after; can return a proxy.

2. Example

@Component
class LoggingBPP implements BeanPostProcessor {
    public Object postProcessAfterInitialization(Object bean, String name) {
        System.out.println("Initialized: " + name);
        return bean;
    }
}

3. Where it's used

Spring uses BeanPostProcessor internally to implement @Autowired, @PostConstruct, and AOP proxying. It operates on bean instances (unlike BeanFactoryPostProcessor, which operates on definitions).

subtopics.name: InitializingBean
subtopics.content:
1. What is InitializingBean?

InitializingBean is an interface with a single method, afterPropertiesSet(), which Spring calls after all dependencies are injected, allowing custom initialization logic.

2. Example

@Component
class CacheService implements InitializingBean {
    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() {
        loadCache(); // runs after DI is complete
    }
}

3. Preferred Alternative

Using @PostConstruct is generally preferred over implementing InitializingBean because it avoids coupling your class to a Spring interface. The order is: @PostConstruct → afterPropertiesSet() → custom init-method.

subtopics.name: DisposableBean
subtopics.content:
1. What is DisposableBean?

DisposableBean is an interface with a single method, destroy(), called by the container when the bean is being destroyed (typically at context shutdown) to release resources.

2. Example

@Component
class ConnectionPool implements DisposableBean {
    @Override
    public void destroy() {
        closeAllConnections();
    }
}

3. Preferred Alternative

@PreDestroy is preferred over implementing DisposableBean to avoid Spring coupling. Destruction order: @PreDestroy → destroy() → custom destroy-method.

Note: destruction callbacks are not called for prototype-scoped beans — the container doesn't manage their full lifecycle.

subtopics.name: @PostConstruct
subtopics.content:
1. What is @PostConstruct?

@PostConstruct marks a method to run once, after dependency injection completes, for initialization logic. It is a Jakarta/Java standard annotation, not Spring-specific.

2. Example

@Component
class ReportService {
    @Autowired private DataSource ds;

    @PostConstruct
    void init() {
        // ds is injected and ready here
        warmUpCache();
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • The method must be void and take no arguments.
  • It runs before the bean is available to others.
  • Preferred over InitializingBean because it keeps the class framework-agnostic.
  • Since Java 11 it needs the jakarta.annotation (or javax.annotation) dependency.

subtopics.name: @PreDestroy
subtopics.content:
1. What is @PreDestroy?

@PreDestroy marks a method to be executed just before the bean is destroyed, during container shutdown, for cleanup such as closing connections or flushing buffers.

2. Example

@Component
class FileService {
    @PreDestroy
    void cleanup() {
        closeOpenFiles();
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • Runs only for container-managed singletons — not for prototype beans.
  • Preferred over DisposableBean to avoid Spring coupling.
  • For graceful shutdown to trigger it, the context must close normally (Boot registers a shutdown hook automatically).

subtopics.name: BeanFactoryPostProcessor
subtopics.content:
1. What is BeanFactoryPostProcessor?

BeanFactoryPostProcessor lets you modify bean definitions (metadata) before any beans are instantiated. It operates on the configuration, not on bean instances.

2. Example

@Component
class MyBFPP implements BeanFactoryPostProcessor {
    public void postProcessBeanFactory(ConfigurableListableBeanFactory bf) {
        BeanDefinition bd = bf.getBeanDefinition("myBean");
        bd.setScope("prototype"); // modify definition
    }
}

3. BeanFactoryPostProcessor vs BeanPostProcessor

  • BeanFactoryPostProcessor: modifies bean definitions before instantiation.
  • BeanPostProcessor: modifies bean instances during initialization.

The classic built-in example is PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer, which resolves ${...} placeholders in definitions.

subtopics.name: FactoryBean
subtopics.content:
1. What is a FactoryBean?

FactoryBean<T> is a special bean that acts as a factory for creating other objects. When you request the bean by name, Spring returns the object produced by its getObject(), not the factory itself.

2. Example

@Component("connection")
class ConnectionFactoryBean implements FactoryBean<Connection> {
    public Connection getObject() { return createConnection(); }
    public Class<?> getObjectType() { return Connection.class; }
    public boolean isSingleton() { return true; }
}

3. Key Points

  • context.getBean("connection") returns the Connection.
  • To get the factory itself, prefix with &: getBean("&connection").
  • Used for complex creation logic — e.g., Spring's SqlSessionFactoryBean for MyBatis.

subtopics.name: Bean Naming
subtopics.content:
1. Default Bean Names

By default, a bean's name is the class name with the first letter lowercased. For example, OrderService becomes orderService.

2. Custom Names

@Component("customName")
class MyService { }

@Bean(name = "dataSourceBean")
DataSource ds() { ... }

3. Key Points

  • For @Bean methods, the default name is the method name.
  • Bean names must be unique within a container.
  • Names matter for @Qualifier and getBean(name) lookups.
  • You can assign multiple names/aliases in the annotation.

subtopics.name: Bean Alias
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Bean Alias?

A bean alias is an alternative name for an existing bean. The same bean can then be referenced by multiple names.

2. Ways to Define Aliases

// Multiple names in the annotation
@Bean(name = {"primaryDs", "mainDataSource"})
DataSource ds() { ... }

// XML
<alias name="realBean" alias="aliasName"/>

3. Use Cases

  • Provide a friendlier or environment-specific name.
  • Maintain backward compatibility when renaming a bean.
  • Let different modules refer to the same bean by their own conventional names.

All aliases point to the same singleton instance.

subtopics.name: Injecting Collections
subtopics.content:
1. Injecting Collections of Beans

Spring can inject all beans of a given type as a List, Set, or array automatically.

2. Example

interface Notifier { }
@Component class EmailNotifier implements Notifier { }
@Component class SmsNotifier implements Notifier { }

@Service
class AlertService {
    private final List<Notifier> notifiers;
    AlertService(List<Notifier> notifiers) { // gets both
        this.notifiers = notifiers;
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • All matching beans are collected into the collection.
  • Order can be controlled with @Order or by implementing Ordered.
  • Great for the Strategy pattern — plug in multiple implementations and iterate.

subtopics.name: Injecting Maps
subtopics.content:
1. Injecting Beans as a Map

Spring can inject all beans of a type into a Map<String, T> where the key is the bean name and the value is the bean instance.

2. Example

@Component("email") class EmailNotifier implements Notifier { }
@Component("sms")   class SmsNotifier   implements Notifier { }

@Service
class AlertService {
    private final Map<String, Notifier> notifiers;
    AlertService(Map<String, Notifier> notifiers) {
        this.notifiers = notifiers; // {"email":..., "sms":...}
    }

    void send(String channel) {
        notifiers.get(channel).notifyUser();
    }
}

3. Use Case

This is a clean way to select an implementation by key at runtime, avoiding long if/switch chains — a common Strategy/Factory pattern in Spring.

subtopics.name: Injecting Optional Beans
subtopics.content:
1. Handling Optional Dependencies

Sometimes a bean may not exist. Spring offers several ways to inject a dependency only if it is available, without failing startup.

2. Options

// 1. required = false
@Autowired(required = false)
private AuditService audit; // null if absent

// 2. Java Optional
@Autowired
private Optional<AuditService> audit;

// 3. ObjectProvider (lazy, safe)
@Autowired
private ObjectProvider<AuditService> auditProvider;

3. Recommendation

  • Optional<T> makes the optionality explicit and avoids null checks.
  • ObjectProvider is the most flexible — supports getIfAvailable() and lazy retrieval.
  • Avoid required=false field injection where possible.

subtopics.name: @Lookup
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Lookup?

@Lookup is method injection. Spring overrides the annotated method to return a fresh bean from the container each time it is called. Its main purpose is to inject a prototype bean into a singleton correctly.

2. The Problem It Solves

If a singleton injects a prototype normally, only one prototype instance is created and reused. @Lookup ensures a new instance on every call.

3. Example

@Component
abstract class TaskProcessor {
    void process() {
        Task task = createTask(); // new instance each call
        task.run();
    }

    @Lookup
    protected abstract Task createTask(); // prototype bean
}

Spring dynamically implements the abstract method to fetch a new prototype from the context each time.

subtopics.name: ObjectProvider
subtopics.content:
1. What is ObjectProvider?

ObjectProvider<T> is a flexible way to obtain a dependency on demand, gracefully handling cases where the bean is absent or where there are multiple candidates.

2. Example

@Service
class ReportService {
    private final ObjectProvider<PdfExporter> exporterProvider;

    ReportService(ObjectProvider<PdfExporter> p) { this.exporterProvider = p; }

    void export() {
        PdfExporter e = exporterProvider.getIfAvailable(); // null if none
        exporterProvider.ifAvailable(PdfExporter::export);
    }
}

3. Key Methods

  • getObject() — get the bean (throws if none/ambiguous).
  • getIfAvailable() — returns null if absent.
  • getIfUnique() — returns null if multiple exist.
  • stream() — stream all matching beans.

It also resolves the prototype-in-singleton problem by fetching a fresh instance on each getObject() call, and defers creation (lazy).

subtopics.name: What is AOP?
subtopics.content:
1. What is AOP?

Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) is a paradigm that separates cross-cutting concerns — logic that spans many modules, like logging, security, and transactions — from the core business logic.

2. The Problem It Solves

Without AOP, concerns like logging or transaction handling get scattered and duplicated across many classes (code tangling and scattering). AOP centralizes them into aspects.

3. Core Concepts

  • Aspect: the module holding cross-cutting logic.
  • Advice: the action taken (before/after/around).
  • Join Point: a point in execution (a method call).
  • Pointcut: an expression selecting join points.

Spring implements AOP using proxies, applying advice around bean methods.

subtopics.name: Why AOP?
subtopics.content:
Why use AOP?

  • Separation of concerns: keeps business logic clean by extracting cross-cutting code.
  • No duplication: logging, security, and transaction code live in one aspect instead of being copied everywhere.
  • Maintainability: a change to a concern is made in one place.
  • Declarative: features like @Transactional are applied via annotations, not manual code.
  • Cleaner code: methods focus on what they do, not on infrastructure.

Common Use Cases

  • Transaction management (@Transactional).
  • Security checks.
  • Logging and auditing.
  • Performance monitoring.
  • Caching and retry logic.

subtopics.name: Advice Types
subtopics.content:
1. What is Advice?

Advice is the action an aspect takes at a matched join point. Spring provides five types.

2. The Five Types

  • @Before: runs before the method executes.
  • @After: runs after the method (finally — success or exception).
  • @AfterReturning: runs after successful return; can access the return value.
  • @AfterThrowing: runs if the method throws an exception.
  • @Around: wraps the method — full control before and after; can modify args/result or prevent execution.

3. Example

@Aspect @Component
class LogAspect {
    @Before("execution(* com.app.service.*.*(..))")
    void logBefore(JoinPoint jp) {
        System.out.println("Calling: " + jp.getSignature());
    }
}

@Around is the most powerful, receiving a ProceedingJoinPoint to call proceed().

subtopics.name: Join Point
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Join Point?

A join point is a specific point during program execution where an aspect can be applied. In Spring AOP, a join point is always a method execution on a Spring-managed bean.

2. Accessing Join Point Info

@Before("execution(* com.app..*(..))")
void advice(JoinPoint jp) {
    jp.getSignature();   // method signature
    jp.getArgs();        // method arguments
    jp.getTarget();      // target object
}

3. Key Point

While full AspectJ supports many join point types (field access, constructor calls, etc.), Spring AOP is limited to method-execution join points because it is proxy-based. A pointcut is an expression that selects which join points advice runs on.

subtopics.name: Pointcut
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Pointcut?

A pointcut is an expression that selects which join points (methods) an advice should apply to. It defines the where.

2. Common Designators

  • execution(...) — match method execution (most common).
  • within(...) — match types in a package/class.
  • @annotation(...) — match methods with a given annotation.
  • args(...) — match by argument types.

3. Example

// Reusable named pointcut
@Pointcut("execution(* com.app.service.*.*(..))")
void serviceMethods() {}

@Before("serviceMethods()")
void log() { ... }

The execution expression format is: execution(modifiers? returnType package.Class.method(args) ). Named pointcuts promote reuse.

subtopics.name: Aspect
subtopics.content:
1. What is an Aspect?

An aspect is a module that encapsulates a cross-cutting concern. It combines pointcuts (where) and advice (what) into one reusable unit.

2. Example

@Aspect
@Component
class PerformanceAspect {

    @Pointcut("execution(* com.app.service.*.*(..))")
    void serviceLayer() {}

    @Around("serviceLayer()")
    Object measure(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.nanoTime();
        Object result = pjp.proceed();
        System.out.println(pjp.getSignature() + " took " +
            (System.nanoTime() - start) + "ns");
        return result;
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • Annotate the class with @Aspect and register it as a bean (@Component).
  • Enable AOP with @EnableAspectJAutoProxy (auto-enabled in Spring Boot).

subtopics.name: Weaving
subtopics.content:
1. What is Weaving?

Weaving is the process of linking aspects with the target objects to create the advised (proxied) object. It's when the cross-cutting code is actually applied.

2. Types of Weaving

  • Compile-time weaving: aspects woven during compilation (AspectJ compiler).
  • Load-time weaving (LTW): woven when classes are loaded into the JVM.
  • Runtime weaving: done via proxies at runtime — this is what Spring AOP uses.

3. Spring's Approach

Spring AOP uses runtime proxy-based weaving: it creates a JDK dynamic proxy or CGLIB proxy around the bean and routes method calls through the advice. Full AspectJ is needed for compile-time or load-time weaving and for non-method join points.

subtopics.name: Proxy Pattern
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Proxy Pattern?

The Proxy pattern provides a surrogate object that controls access to a real object. The proxy can add behavior (logging, security, transactions) before/after delegating to the real target.

2. How Spring Uses It

Spring AOP is built on proxies. When a bean has aspects (or @Transactional), Spring wraps it in a proxy. Calls go to the proxy, which applies advice, then delegates to the actual bean. 

Caller -> Proxy (applies advice) -> Target Bean

3. Two Proxy Mechanisms

  • JDK Dynamic Proxy: used when the bean implements an interface.
  • CGLIB Proxy: used when there is no interface — subclasses the class.

A key limitation: self-invocation (a method calling another method in the same bean) bypasses the proxy, so advice won't apply.

subtopics.name: JDK Dynamic Proxy
subtopics.content:
1. What is a JDK Dynamic Proxy?

A JDK dynamic proxy is a proxy created at runtime by the JDK's java.lang.reflect.Proxy. It works only for classes that implement an interface, and the proxy implements that same interface.

2. How it works

  • The proxy implements the target's interface(s).
  • All calls are routed through an InvocationHandler.invoke(), where advice is applied.

3. Key Points

  • Requires at least one interface — this is Spring's default when interfaces exist.
  • You must inject/reference the bean by its interface type, not the concrete class.
  • Lightweight, no extra library.

If a class has no interface, Spring falls back to CGLIB.

subtopics.name: CGLIB Proxy
subtopics.content:
1. What is a CGLIB Proxy?

CGLIB (Code Generation Library) creates a proxy by subclassing the target class at runtime and overriding its methods to apply advice. It is used when the bean does not implement an interface.

2. Key Points

  • Works on concrete classes (no interface required).
  • The target class and methods must not be final (final can't be overridden).
  • Since Spring Boot 2.x, CGLIB is the default proxy mechanism even for interfaces (proxyTargetClass=true).

3. JDK vs CGLIB

JDK Dynamic ProxyCGLIB Proxy
Requires an interfaceSubclasses the target class
Uses reflectionGenerates bytecode
Cannot proxy final classes/methods (n/a)Cannot proxy final classes/methods

subtopics.name: Around Advice
subtopics.content:
1. What is Around Advice?

Around advice is the most powerful advice type. It wraps the target method, giving full control to run code before and after, modify arguments and return values, or even skip execution entirely.

2. Example

@Around("execution(* com.app.service.*.*(..))")
Object around(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
    System.out.println("Before");
    try {
        Object result = pjp.proceed(); // call target
        System.out.println("After success");
        return result;
    } catch (Exception e) {
        System.out.println("After failure");
        throw e;
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • It receives a ProceedingJoinPoint; you must call proceed() to run the target (unless intentionally short-circuiting).
  • Return type should be Object and it should declare throws Throwable.
  • Ideal for timing, caching, retries, and transaction-like wrapping.

subtopics.name: Before vs After Advice
subtopics.content:
1. Before Advice

@Before runs before the target method. It cannot change the method's execution or return value, but can throw to prevent it. Good for validation, logging entry, and security checks.

2. After Advice Variants

  • @After: runs after the method regardless of outcome (like finally).
  • @AfterReturning: runs only on successful return; can read the returned value.
  • @AfterThrowing: runs only if an exception is thrown; can read the exception.

3. Example

@AfterReturning(pointcut = "serviceMethods()", returning = "result")
void logResult(Object result) {
    System.out.println("Returned: " + result);
}

Before = pre-processing; After = post-processing/cleanup. For full control before and after, use @Around.

subtopics.name: Transaction AOP
subtopics.content:
1. Transactions via AOP

Spring's @Transactional is implemented using AOP. When you annotate a method or class, Spring wraps the bean in a proxy that begins a transaction before the method and commits or rolls back after.

2. How it works

Proxy:
  begin transaction
  try {
     invoke target method
     commit
  } catch (RuntimeException e) {
     rollback
  }

This is essentially an around advice managed by the TransactionInterceptor and a PlatformTransactionManager.

3. Key Implications

  • Because it's proxy-based, self-invocation (calling @Transactional methods within the same class) bypasses the transaction.
  • By default it rolls back on unchecked exceptions, not checked ones.
  • @Transactional only works on public methods of Spring beans.

subtopics.name: Logging Aspect
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Logging Aspect?

A logging aspect centralizes logging of method calls, arguments, results, and execution time across the application, removing repetitive log statements from business code.

2. Example

@Aspect @Component
class LoggingAspect {

    private static final Logger log =
        LoggerFactory.getLogger(LoggingAspect.class);

    @Around("execution(* com.app.service.*.*(..))")
    Object logExecution(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
        log.info("Enter {} args={}", pjp.getSignature(), pjp.getArgs());
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
        Object result = pjp.proceed();
        log.info("Exit {} in {}ms", pjp.getSignature(),
                 System.currentTimeMillis() - start);
        return result;
    }
}

3. Benefit

One aspect logs every service method consistently — no duplication, easy to enable/disable, and business methods stay clean.

subtopics.name: Exception Handling Aspect
subtopics.content:
1. What is an Exception Handling Aspect?

An exception handling aspect centralizes how exceptions from methods are logged, transformed, or reported, using @AfterThrowing or @Around advice.

2. Example

@Aspect @Component
class ExceptionAspect {

    @AfterThrowing(
        pointcut = "execution(* com.app.service.*.*(..))",
        throwing = "ex")
    void handle(JoinPoint jp, Throwable ex) {
        log.error("Exception in {}: {}",
            jp.getSignature(), ex.getMessage(), ex);
    }
}

3. AOP vs @ControllerAdvice

  • Exception aspect: good for cross-layer logging/auditing of exceptions.
  • @ControllerAdvice + @ExceptionHandler: the standard way to convert exceptions into HTTP responses at the web layer.

Often used together — the aspect logs, and @ControllerAdvice shapes the client response.

subtopics.name: Spring MVC Architecture
subtopics.content:
1. What is Spring MVC?

Spring MVC is a web framework built on the Model-View-Controller pattern, using a front controller (the DispatcherServlet) to route all requests.

2. Components

  • DispatcherServlet: front controller receiving all requests.
  • HandlerMapping: maps a request to a controller method.
  • Controller: processes the request, returns a model/view.
  • ViewResolver: resolves logical view names to actual views.
  • Model: data passed to the view.

3. Flow

Request -> DispatcherServlet -> HandlerMapping
        -> Controller -> Service -> Model
        -> ViewResolver -> View -> Response

For REST APIs, controllers return data (JSON) directly instead of views.

subtopics.name: DispatcherServlet
subtopics.content:
1. What is DispatcherServlet?

The DispatcherServlet is the front controller of Spring MVC. Every HTTP request passes through it, and it orchestrates the entire request-handling workflow.

2. Responsibilities

  • Receives all incoming requests (mapped to / by default).
  • Consults HandlerMapping to find the right controller.
  • Invokes the controller via a HandlerAdapter.
  • Uses message converters or a ViewResolver to build the response.
  • Delegates exceptions to HandlerExceptionResolvers.

3. Key Point

In Spring Boot it is auto-configured — no web.xml needed. It centralizes cross-cutting request concerns (routing, conversion, exception handling), embodying the Front Controller pattern.

subtopics.name: Request Lifecycle
subtopics.content:
Spring MVC Request Lifecycle

  1. Request arrives at the DispatcherServlet.
  2. HandlerMapping determines the matching controller method.
  3. HandlerInterceptors run their preHandle().
  4. HandlerAdapter invokes the controller; arguments are resolved (@RequestBody, @PathVariable, etc.).
  5. The controller executes business logic and returns data or a view name.
  6. For REST, a message converter serializes the result to JSON; for MVC, a ViewResolver renders the view.
  7. Interceptors run postHandle() and afterCompletion().
  8. The response is sent back to the client.

Exception Path

If any step throws, the DispatcherServlet routes it to a HandlerExceptionResolver (e.g., @ControllerAdvice) to produce an error response.

subtopics.name: @Controller vs @RestController
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

@Controller@RestController
Returns view names (MVC)Returns data (REST)
Needs @ResponseBody for data@ResponseBody is built-in
For server-side rendered pagesFor JSON/XML APIs

2. Key Point

@RestController is a convenience annotation that combines @Controller + @ResponseBody, so every method's return value is automatically serialized (typically to JSON) into the response body.

3. Example

@RestController
class UserApi {
    @GetMapping("/users/{id}")
    User get(@PathVariable int id) { return service.find(id); }
}

@Controller
class PageController {
    @GetMapping("/home")
    String home() { return "home"; } // view name
}

subtopics.name: @RequestMapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is @RequestMapping?

@RequestMapping maps HTTP requests to controller classes and methods. It can specify the URL path, HTTP method, headers, params, and content types.

2. Example

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/users")
class UserController {

    @RequestMapping(method = RequestMethod.GET)
    List<User> all() { ... }

    @RequestMapping(value = "/{id}", method = RequestMethod.GET)
    User byId(@PathVariable int id) { ... }
}

3. Key Points

  • At class level it sets a base path; at method level it appends to it.
  • Attributes: value/path, method, params, headers, consumes, produces.
  • Shortcut annotations — @GetMapping, @PostMapping, etc. — are preferred for readability.

subtopics.name: @GetMapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is @GetMapping?

@GetMapping is a shortcut for @RequestMapping(method = RequestMethod.GET). It maps HTTP GET requests, used to retrieve resources.

2. Example

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/users")
class UserController {

    @GetMapping                 // GET /api/users
    List<User> all() { ... }

    @GetMapping("/{id}")        // GET /api/users/5
    User byId(@PathVariable int id) { ... }

    @GetMapping(params = "active") // GET /api/users?active=true
    List<User> active() { ... }
}

3. Key Points

  • GET is safe and idempotent — it should not modify state.
  • Parameters come from the path (@PathVariable) or query string (@RequestParam).
  • Responses are cacheable.

subtopics.name: @PostMapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is @PostMapping?

@PostMapping maps HTTP POST requests, used to create new resources. It is shorthand for @RequestMapping(method = POST).

2. Example

@PostMapping("/api/users")
ResponseEntity<User> create(@Valid @RequestBody User user) {
    User saved = service.save(user);
    return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(saved);
}

3. Key Points

  • The request body is bound with @RequestBody and can be validated with @Valid.
  • POST is not idempotent — repeated calls create multiple resources.
  • Conventionally returns 201 Created with the new resource (and optionally a Location header).

subtopics.name: @PutMapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is @PutMapping?

@PutMapping maps HTTP PUT requests, used to fully update/replace an existing resource.

2. Example

@PutMapping("/api/users/{id}")
User update(@PathVariable int id, @Valid @RequestBody User user) {
    return service.update(id, user);
}

3. PUT vs POST vs PATCH

  • POST: create a new resource (not idempotent).
  • PUT: replace the entire resource; idempotent — repeating the same PUT yields the same state.
  • PATCH: partial update of specific fields.

Because PUT replaces the whole resource, missing fields are typically set to null/default — send the complete representation.

subtopics.name: @DeleteMapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is @DeleteMapping?

@DeleteMapping maps HTTP DELETE requests, used to remove a resource.

2. Example

@DeleteMapping("/api/users/{id}")
ResponseEntity<Void> delete(@PathVariable int id) {
    service.delete(id);
    return ResponseEntity.noContent().build(); // 204
}

3. Key Points

  • DELETE is idempotent — deleting the same resource repeatedly leaves the same end state (already gone).
  • Common responses: 204 No Content (success, no body) or 200 OK.
  • Deleting a non-existent resource is often treated as success (204) or 404, depending on API design.

subtopics.name: @PatchMapping
subtopics.content:
1. What is @PatchMapping?

@PatchMapping maps HTTP PATCH requests, used for a partial update — modifying only the specified fields of a resource rather than replacing it.

2. Example

@PatchMapping("/api/users/{id}")
User patch(@PathVariable int id,
           @RequestBody Map<String,Object> updates) {
    return service.partialUpdate(id, updates); // only given fields
}

3. PUT vs PATCH

  • PUT: replaces the whole resource; send all fields.
  • PATCH: updates only provided fields; leaves the rest unchanged.

PATCH is efficient for updating one or two fields. Implementations often use a partial DTO or a map of changed fields.

subtopics.name: @PathVariable
subtopics.content:
1. What is @PathVariable?

@PathVariable binds a value from the URI path to a method parameter.

2. Example

@GetMapping("/users/{id}/orders/{orderId}")
Order get(@PathVariable int id,
          @PathVariable("orderId") int oid) { ... }

3. Key Points

  • Used for required, hierarchical identifiers that are part of the resource path.
  • If the variable name matches the parameter name, the explicit name can be omitted.
  • Can be made optional with required=false (rare for path variables).

4. @PathVariable vs @RequestParam

  • @PathVariable: part of the path — /users/5.
  • @RequestParam: query string — /users?id=5.

subtopics.name: @RequestParam
subtopics.content:
1. What is @RequestParam?

@RequestParam binds a query parameter (or form field) to a method parameter.

2. Example

// GET /users?page=2&size=10&sort=name
@GetMapping("/users")
List<User> list(
    @RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page,
    @RequestParam(defaultValue = "20") int size,
    @RequestParam(required = false) String sort) { ... }

3. Key Points

  • required defaults to true; use defaultValue or required=false for optional params.
  • Can bind to collections for repeated params (?id=1&id=2).
  • Ideal for filtering, pagination, sorting, and search.

Use @RequestParam for optional/query-style inputs and @PathVariable for resource identifiers.

subtopics.name: @RequestBody
subtopics.content:
1. What is @RequestBody?

@RequestBody binds the HTTP request body to a Java object. Spring uses an HttpMessageConverter (usually Jackson) to deserialize JSON/XML into the object.

2. Example

@PostMapping("/users")
User create(@Valid @RequestBody User user) {
    return service.save(user);
}

// Request body:
// { "name": "Alice", "email": "a@x.com" }

3. Key Points

  • Used with POST/PUT/PATCH to receive JSON payloads.
  • Combine with @Valid to trigger bean validation on the body.
  • The Content-Type header must match a registered converter (e.g., application/json).

subtopics.name: @ResponseBody
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ResponseBody?

@ResponseBody tells Spring to write the method's return value directly to the HTTP response body (serialized to JSON/XML), instead of resolving it as a view name.

2. Example

@Controller
class UserController {
    @GetMapping("/users/{id}")
    @ResponseBody
    User get(@PathVariable int id) {
        return service.find(id); // serialized to JSON
    }
}

3. Key Point

In a @RestController, @ResponseBody is applied automatically to every method, so you rarely write it explicitly. Serialization is handled by HttpMessageConverters.

subtopics.name: ResponseEntity
subtopics.content:
1. What is ResponseEntity?

ResponseEntity<T> represents the full HTTP response — status code, headers, and body — giving you complete control over what is returned.

2. Example

@GetMapping("/users/{id}")
ResponseEntity<User> get(@PathVariable int id) {
    User u = service.find(id);
    if (u == null) return ResponseEntity.notFound().build();
    return ResponseEntity.ok()
            .header("X-Custom", "value")
            .body(u);
}

// Created with Location header
return ResponseEntity.created(uri).body(saved);

3. Key Points

  • Set precise status codes (200, 201, 404, etc.).
  • Add custom headers.
  • Builder methods: ok(), created(), noContent(), badRequest(), status().

Preferred over returning a plain object when you need control over status/headers.

subtopics.name: HttpStatus
subtopics.content:
1. What is HttpStatus?

HttpStatus is an enum of standard HTTP status codes, used with ResponseEntity and @ResponseStatus to indicate the outcome of a request.

2. Common Categories

  • 2xx Success: 200 OK, 201 CREATED, 204 NO_CONTENT.
  • 3xx Redirection: 301 MOVED_PERMANENTLY, 304 NOT_MODIFIED.
  • 4xx Client Error: 400 BAD_REQUEST, 401 UNAUTHORIZED, 403 FORBIDDEN, 404 NOT_FOUND, 409 CONFLICT, 429 TOO_MANY_REQUESTS.
  • 5xx Server Error: 500 INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR, 503 SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE.

3. Example

@ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND)
class UserNotFoundException extends RuntimeException { }

return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(user);

Using the correct status code is a key REST best practice for meaningful, self-descriptive APIs.

subtopics.name: @ControllerAdvice
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ControllerAdvice?

@ControllerAdvice is a global component that applies to all controllers. Its primary use is centralized exception handling, but it also supports global model attributes and data binding.

2. Example

@RestControllerAdvice
class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(UserNotFoundException.class)
    ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleNotFound(UserNotFoundException ex) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND)
            .body(new ErrorResponse(ex.getMessage()));
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class)
    ResponseEntity<?> handleValidation(MethodArgumentNotValidException ex) { ... }
}

3. Key Points

  • @RestControllerAdvice = @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody.
  • Keeps error-handling logic out of individual controllers.
  • Produces consistent, structured error responses across the API.

subtopics.name: @ExceptionHandler
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ExceptionHandler?

@ExceptionHandler marks a method that handles specific exception types thrown by controller methods, letting you map exceptions to custom HTTP responses.

2. Example

@RestController
class UserController {

    @ExceptionHandler(IllegalArgumentException.class)
    ResponseEntity<String> handle(IllegalArgumentException ex) {
        return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(ex.getMessage());
    }
}

3. Scope

  • Local: inside a controller — handles exceptions from that controller only.
  • Global: inside a @ControllerAdvice — handles exceptions from all controllers.

One method can handle multiple exception types, and the handler can access the exception and request to build a detailed response.

subtopics.name: Validation
subtopics.content:
1. What is Validation?

Spring integrates with Bean Validation (Jakarta Validation / Hibernate Validator) to validate request data declaratively using constraint annotations on fields.

2. Common Constraints

  • @NotNull, @NotBlank, @NotEmpty
  • @Size(min, max), @Min, @Max
  • @Email, @Pattern(regexp)
  • @Positive, @Past, @Future

3. Example

class UserDto {
    @NotBlank private String name;
    @Email    private String email;
    @Min(18)  private int age;
}

@PostMapping("/users")
User create(@Valid @RequestBody UserDto dto) { ... }

Trigger validation with @Valid; failures throw MethodArgumentNotValidException, handled globally via @ControllerAdvice.

subtopics.name: @Valid
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Valid?

@Valid (from Jakarta Bean Validation) triggers validation of an object's constraint annotations. In controllers it validates request bodies or parameters.

2. Example

@PostMapping("/users")
User create(@Valid @RequestBody UserDto dto) {
    return service.save(dto);
}

3. Key Points

  • Validation failure throws MethodArgumentNotValidException → HTTP 400 by default.
  • Supports nested validation — annotate nested object fields with @Valid to cascade.
  • Pair with a BindingResult parameter to handle errors manually.

4. @Valid vs @Validated

@Valid is the standard annotation; @Validated (Spring) adds validation groups and enables method-level validation on beans.

subtopics.name: @Validated
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Validated?

@Validated is Spring's variant of @Valid that adds support for validation groups and enables method-level validation on Spring beans.

2. Validation Groups

interface OnCreate {}
interface OnUpdate {}

class UserDto {
    @Null(groups = OnCreate.class)
    @NotNull(groups = OnUpdate.class)
    private Long id;
}

@PostMapping
User create(@Validated(OnCreate.class) @RequestBody UserDto dto) { ... }

3. Method-Level Validation

@Service
@Validated
class UserService {
    void register(@NotBlank String name, @Min(18) int age) { ... }
}

4. @Valid vs @Validated

  • @Valid: standard, no groups.
  • @Validated: Spring-specific, supports groups and method validation.

subtopics.name: BindingResult
subtopics.content:
1. What is BindingResult?

BindingResult holds the result of validation and data binding. Placed immediately after a @Valid parameter, it lets you inspect and handle errors programmatically instead of letting Spring throw.

2. Example

@PostMapping("/users")
ResponseEntity<?> create(@Valid @RequestBody UserDto dto,
                          BindingResult result) {
    if (result.hasErrors()) {
        List<String> errors = result.getFieldErrors().stream()
            .map(e -> e.getField() + ": " + e.getDefaultMessage())
            .toList();
        return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(errors);
    }
    return ResponseEntity.ok(service.save(dto));
}

3. Key Point

It must directly follow the validated object. If present, Spring won't throw MethodArgumentNotValidException — you handle errors yourself. Without it, global @ControllerAdvice handling kicks in.

subtopics.name: REST API Best Practices
subtopics.content:
REST API Best Practices

  • Use nouns, not verbs in URLs: /users/5, not /getUser.
  • Use proper HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial), DELETE (remove).
  • Use correct status codes: 200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 404, 409, 500.
  • Version your API (e.g., /api/v1/...).
  • Use plural resource names and hierarchy: /users/5/orders.
  • Support pagination, filtering, sorting via query params.
  • Return consistent error responses via @ControllerAdvice.
  • Use DTOs instead of exposing entities.
  • Make writes idempotent where possible.
  • Secure with HTTPS, auth, and input validation.

Following REST conventions makes APIs predictable, self-descriptive, and easy to consume.

subtopics.name: Idempotent APIs
subtopics.content:
1. What is Idempotency?

An operation is idempotent if making the same request multiple times has the same effect as making it once. It is crucial for safe retries.

2. HTTP Methods

MethodIdempotent?
GETYes (also safe)
PUTYes
DELETEYes
POSTNo
PATCHNot guaranteed

3. Making POST Idempotent

Use an idempotency key: the client sends a unique key (header), and the server stores the result so duplicate requests return the same response instead of creating duplicates. This is essential for payment and order APIs where retries can cause double-processing.

subtopics.name: Pagination
subtopics.content:
1. What is Pagination?

Pagination breaks a large result set into smaller pages, improving performance and usability by returning only a chunk of data per request.

2. Spring Data Pagination

// GET /users?page=0&size=20&sort=name,asc
@GetMapping("/users")
Page<User> list(Pageable pageable) {
    return repository.findAll(pageable);
}

Spring auto-binds page, size, and sort query params to a Pageable. The Page<T> result includes content plus total elements, total pages, and current page.

3. Approaches

  • Offset-based: page + size — simple, but slow on huge tables.
  • Cursor/keyset-based: uses a marker (e.g., last id) — efficient for large, real-time data.

Pagination prevents loading entire tables into memory and keeps responses fast.

subtopics.name: Sorting
subtopics.content:
1. What is Sorting?

Sorting orders API results by one or more fields, typically controlled by a query parameter and handled by Spring Data.

2. Example

// GET /users?sort=name,asc&sort=age,desc
@GetMapping("/users")
List<User> list(Sort sort) {
    return repository.findAll(sort);
}

// Combined with pagination
@GetMapping("/users")
Page<User> list(Pageable pageable) { // page,size,sort together
    return repository.findAll(pageable);
}

3. Key Points

  • Sort and Pageable are auto-bound from request params.
  • Multiple sort fields are supported.
  • Whitelist sortable fields to avoid exposing internal columns or enabling injection-style abuse.

subtopics.name: CORS
subtopics.content:
1. What is CORS?

Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is a browser security mechanism that controls whether a web page from one origin can call an API on a different origin. Servers use response headers to allow it.

2. Enabling CORS in Spring

// Per controller/method
@CrossOrigin(origins = "https://app.example.com")
@RestController
class ApiController { ... }

// Global config
@Configuration
class CorsConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer {
    public void addCorsMappings(CorsRegistry r) {
        r.addMapping("/api/**")
         .allowedOrigins("https://app.example.com")
         .allowedMethods("GET","POST","PUT","DELETE");
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • Browsers send a preflight OPTIONS request for non-simple requests.
  • Avoid allowedOrigins("*") with credentials in production.
  • With Spring Security, CORS must also be enabled in the security filter chain.

subtopics.name: File Upload
subtopics.content:
1. Handling File Uploads

Spring handles file uploads via MultipartFile, using multipart/form-data requests.

2. Example

@PostMapping("/upload")
ResponseEntity<String> upload(@RequestParam("file") MultipartFile file)
        throws IOException {
    String name = file.getOriginalFilename();
    byte[] bytes = file.getBytes();
    file.transferTo(new File("/uploads/" + name));
    return ResponseEntity.ok("Uploaded: " + name);
}

3. Configuration

spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size=10MB
spring.servlet.multipart.max-request-size=10MB

4. Best Practices

  • Validate file type and size.
  • Sanitize filenames to prevent path traversal.
  • Store large files in cloud/object storage, not the app server.

subtopics.name: MultipartFile
subtopics.content:
1. What is MultipartFile?

MultipartFile is a Spring interface representing an uploaded file received in a multipart/form-data request.

2. Key Methods

  • getOriginalFilename() — client-provided filename.
  • getContentType() — MIME type.
  • getSize() — size in bytes.
  • getBytes() / getInputStream() — file content.
  • transferTo(dest) — save to disk.
  • isEmpty() — check if a file was provided.

3. Multiple Files

@PostMapping("/upload")
String upload(@RequestParam("files") MultipartFile[] files) {
    for (MultipartFile f : files) { ... }
    return "done";
}

Always validate the file and handle IOException. Configure size limits via spring.servlet.multipart.*.

subtopics.name: API Versioning
subtopics.content:
1. Why Version APIs?

API versioning lets you evolve an API (breaking changes) without breaking existing clients, by serving multiple versions concurrently.

2. Versioning Strategies

  • URI versioning: /api/v1/users — most common and visible.
  • Request parameter: /users?version=1.
  • Header versioning: a custom header like X-API-VERSION: 1.
  • Content negotiation (media type): Accept: application/vnd.app.v1+json.

3. Example

@GetMapping("/api/v1/users") List<UserV1> v1() { ... }
@GetMapping("/api/v2/users") List<UserV2> v2() { ... }

URI versioning is the most popular for its simplicity and clarity. Whichever you pick, keep it consistent and document the deprecation policy.

subtopics.name: HATEOAS
subtopics.content:
1. What is HATEOAS?

HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is a REST constraint where API responses include links to related actions/resources, so clients can navigate the API dynamically without hardcoding URLs.

2. Example (Spring HATEOAS)

EntityModel<User> model = EntityModel.of(user,
    linkTo(methodOn(UserController.class).get(id)).withSelfRel(),
    linkTo(methodOn(UserController.class).all()).withRel("users"));

// JSON:
// { "name": "Alice",
//   "_links": { "self": {"href": "/users/1"},
//               "users": {"href": "/users"} } }

3. Benefit

  • Clients discover available actions from the response itself.
  • Server can change URLs without breaking clients.
  • Represents the highest maturity level (Level 3) in the Richardson Maturity Model.

It increases decoupling, though many APIs skip it for simplicity.

subtopics.name: Content Negotiation
subtopics.content:
1. What is Content Negotiation?

Content negotiation lets a client and server agree on the response format (JSON, XML, etc.). The client expresses a preference and the server responds accordingly.

2. How the Client Requests Format

  • Accept header: Accept: application/xml (standard, preferred).
  • URL extension: /users.xml (legacy).
  • Query param: /users?format=xml.

3. In Spring

@GetMapping(value = "/users",
    produces = {"application/json", "application/xml"})
List<User> all() { ... }

Spring picks the right HttpMessageConverter based on the Accept header. JSON works out of the box; add Jackson XML for XML support. produces/consumes restrict the media types a handler serves.

subtopics.name: Message Converters
subtopics.content:
1. What are Message Converters?

HttpMessageConverters convert between Java objects and HTTP request/response bodies. They handle serialization (object → JSON) and deserialization (JSON → object).

2. Common Converters

  • MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter — JSON (default).
  • MappingJackson2XmlHttpMessageConverter — XML.
  • StringHttpMessageConverter — plain text.
  • ByteArrayHttpMessageConverter — binary data.

3. How They Work

When a controller uses @RequestBody/@ResponseBody, Spring selects a converter based on the request's Content-Type and the Accept header. This is the engine behind content negotiation.

4. Customization

@Configuration
class Config implements WebMvcConfigurer {
    public void extendMessageConverters(List<HttpMessageConverter<?>> c) {
        // add or reorder converters
    }
}

subtopics.name: Interceptors
subtopics.content:
1. What are Interceptors?

HandlerInterceptor lets you hook into the request lifecycle around controller execution — before, after, and on completion. They operate within Spring MVC (aware of the handler).

2. Three Methods

  • preHandle() — before the controller; return false to block the request.
  • postHandle() — after the controller, before the view renders.
  • afterCompletion() — after the full request completes (cleanup).

3. Example

@Component
class AuthInterceptor implements HandlerInterceptor {
    public boolean preHandle(HttpServletRequest req,
            HttpServletResponse res, Object handler) {
        return req.getHeader("Authorization") != null;
    }
}
// register via WebMvcConfigurer.addInterceptors()

Use cases: authentication, logging, request timing, and adding common attributes.

subtopics.name: Filters vs Interceptors
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

FilterInterceptor
Servlet API (javax/jakarta)Spring MVC concept
Runs outside DispatcherServletRuns inside DispatcherServlet
No access to handler/modelAware of the handler and model
Works on raw request/responseWorks with Spring context

2. When to Use Which

  • Filters: low-level concerns applying to all requests — encoding, CORS, security (Spring Security uses filters), request/response wrapping.
  • Interceptors: Spring-aware concerns needing handler/model access — auth checks tied to controllers, logging with handler info, adding model attributes.

3. Order

Filter -> DispatcherServlet -> Interceptor.preHandle
     -> Controller -> Interceptor.postHandle
     -> Interceptor.afterCompletion -> Filter

subtopics.name: What is JPA?
subtopics.content:
1. What is JPA?

JPA (Jakarta Persistence API) is a Java specification for object-relational mapping (ORM). It defines how Java objects are mapped to database tables and how they are persisted, without prescribing an implementation.

2. JPA vs Hibernate vs Spring Data JPA

  • JPA: the specification (interfaces and annotations).
  • Hibernate: the most popular JPA implementation.
  • Spring Data JPA: a Spring abstraction on top of JPA that removes boilerplate via repositories.

3. Key Concepts

  • @Entity — maps a class to a table.
  • EntityManager — manages persistence operations.
  • Persistence Context — first-level cache of managed entities.
  • JPQL — object-oriented query language.

JPA lets developers work with objects instead of SQL, improving productivity and portability.

subtopics.name: Hibernate Architecture
subtopics.content:
1. Hibernate Architecture

Hibernate sits between the application and the database, mapping objects to tables. Its core components are:

2. Core Components

  • SessionFactory: a thread-safe, heavyweight factory for sessions; created once per database.
  • Session: a single-threaded unit of work; wraps a JDBC connection and is the main interface for CRUD.
  • Transaction: abstracts the underlying transaction.
  • ConnectionProvider: manages JDBC connections/pool.
  • Dialect: generates database-specific SQL.

3. Flow

Application -> SessionFactory -> Session -> Transaction
            -> JDBC -> Database

In JPA terms, SessionFactory ≈ EntityManagerFactory and Session ≈ EntityManager. The dialect makes Hibernate database-portable.

subtopics.name: Entity Lifecycle
subtopics.content:
1. Entity States

A JPA entity moves through four states relative to the persistence context:

  • Transient (New): a new object not associated with the persistence context and no DB row.
  • Managed (Persistent): associated with the persistence context; changes are tracked and synced (dirty checking).
  • Detached: was managed, but the context closed; changes are no longer tracked.
  • Removed: marked for deletion; the row is deleted on flush/commit.

2. Transitions

new         -> Transient
persist()   -> Managed
detach()/close close -> Detached
merge()     -> Managed (from Detached)
remove()    -> Removed

Understanding these states explains why changes to a managed entity persist automatically, while changes to a detached one need merge().

subtopics.name: Persistence Context
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Persistence Context?

The persistence context is a set of managed entity instances tracked by the EntityManager. It acts as a first-level cache and manages entity state within a transaction.

2. What It Provides

  • Identity guarantee: one instance per entity id within the context.
  • First-level cache: repeated lookups of the same id hit memory, not the DB.
  • Dirty checking: tracks changes and auto-syncs on flush.
  • Write-behind: batches and delays SQL until flush/commit.

3. Scope

Typically bound to a transaction — it is created when the transaction starts and cleared when it ends. This is why lazy loading works inside a transaction but throws LazyInitializationException outside it.

subtopics.name: EntityManager
subtopics.content:
1. What is EntityManager?

The EntityManager is the primary JPA interface for interacting with the persistence context. It performs CRUD operations, manages entity state, and runs queries.

2. Key Methods

  • persist(entity) — make transient entity managed (insert).
  • find(Class, id) — retrieve by primary key.
  • merge(entity) — reattach a detached entity.
  • remove(entity) — delete.
  • flush() — sync changes to the DB.
  • createQuery(jpql) — run JPQL queries.

3. Example

@PersistenceContext
private EntityManager em;

@Transactional
void save(User u) { em.persist(u); }

Spring Data JPA repositories use the EntityManager internally, so you rarely use it directly — but it's available for custom queries.

subtopics.name: First Level Cache
subtopics.content:
1. What is the First-Level Cache?

The first-level (L1) cache is the persistence context itself, scoped to a single EntityManager/Session (usually a transaction). It is always on and cannot be disabled.

2. How it works

@Transactional
void demo() {
    User a = em.find(User.class, 1L); // hits DB
    User b = em.find(User.class, 1L); // from L1 cache, no DB
    // a == b (same instance)
}

3. Key Points

  • Repeated lookups of the same id within the session return the same cached instance — no extra queries.
  • Guarantees entity identity within the context.
  • Cleared when the session/transaction ends or on clear().

It is session-scoped and not shared across transactions — that role belongs to the second-level cache.

subtopics.name: Second Level Cache
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Second-Level Cache?

The second-level (L2) cache is a SessionFactory-scoped cache shared across sessions and transactions. Unlike L1, it is optional and must be explicitly enabled.

2. Enabling It

@Entity
@Cacheable
@Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE)
class Country { ... }

# properties
hibernate.cache.use_second_level_cache=true

It requires a provider like EhCache, Hazelcast, or Infinispan.

3. When to Use

  • Best for read-mostly, rarely-changing data (reference/lookup tables).
  • Reduces database round-trips across users/sessions.
  • Avoid for frequently-updated data (cache invalidation overhead).

There is also a query cache for caching query result sets, used alongside L2.

subtopics.name: Dirty Checking
subtopics.content:
1. What is Dirty Checking?

Dirty checking is Hibernate's automatic detection of changes to managed entities. If a managed entity's field changes, Hibernate generates the corresponding UPDATE at flush time — without an explicit save call.

2. Example

@Transactional
void updateName(Long id) {
    User u = em.find(User.class, id); // managed
    u.setName("New Name");             // just change the field
    // no save() needed — UPDATE runs on commit
}

3. How it works

  • When loaded, Hibernate stores a snapshot of each entity's state.
  • At flush, it compares current state with the snapshot.
  • Only changed entities (and columns, if configured) generate SQL.

Dirty checking works only on managed entities within a transaction — detached entities need merge().

subtopics.name: Lazy Loading
subtopics.content:
1. What is Lazy Loading?

Lazy loading defers fetching an associated entity/collection until it is actually accessed, rather than loading it upfront with the parent. It improves performance by avoiding unnecessary joins.

2. Example

@Entity
class User {
    @OneToMany(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    private List<Order> orders; // loaded only when accessed
}

3. How it works

Hibernate returns a proxy or lazy collection. The real data is fetched via a separate query on first access.

4. Common Pitfall

Accessing a lazy association after the session/transaction closes throws LazyInitializationException. Fixes: access within the transaction, use a JOIN FETCH query, or an entity graph. Note: @OneToMany/@ManyToMany are lazy by default; @ManyToOne/@OneToOne are eager by default.

subtopics.name: Eager Loading
subtopics.content:
1. What is Eager Loading?

Eager loading fetches an associated entity/collection immediately along with the parent, in the same query (or additional queries).

2. Example

@Entity
class Order {
    @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.EAGER)
    private User user; // loaded with the order
}

3. Default Fetch Types

  • Eager by default: @ManyToOne, @OneToOne.
  • Lazy by default: @OneToMany, @ManyToMany.

4. Trade-offs

  • Pro: avoids LazyInitializationException; data is always available.
  • Con: can load unnecessary data and cause performance issues (large joins, N+1).

Best practice: use lazy by default and fetch eagerly on demand with JOIN FETCH.

subtopics.name: FetchType
subtopics.content:
1. What is FetchType?

FetchType defines when an association is loaded from the database. It has two values: LAZY and EAGER.

2. Values

  • LAZY: load the association only when accessed.
  • EAGER: load the association immediately with the parent.

3. Defaults by Relationship

RelationshipDefault
@OneToManyLAZY
@ManyToManyLAZY
@ManyToOneEAGER
@OneToOneEAGER

4. Best Practice

@ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY) // override eager default
private User user;

Prefer LAZY everywhere and fetch what you need explicitly to prevent over-fetching and N+1 problems.

subtopics.name: Cascade Types
subtopics.content:
1. What are Cascade Types?

Cascading propagates entity operations (persist, merge, remove, etc.) from a parent to its associated child entities automatically.

2. Cascade Types

  • PERSIST — saving the parent saves children.
  • MERGE — merging the parent merges children.
  • REMOVE — deleting the parent deletes children.
  • REFRESH — refresh propagates.
  • DETACH — detach propagates.
  • ALL — all of the above.

3. Example

@OneToMany(mappedBy = "order", cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
private List<OrderItem> items;

// saving order also saves its items

Use CascadeType.ALL for true parent-child ownership (e.g., Order → OrderItems), but be careful with REMOVE on shared entities.

subtopics.name: Orphan Removal
subtopics.content:
1. What is Orphan Removal?

orphanRemoval = true automatically deletes a child entity when it is removed from its parent's collection — i.e., when it becomes an "orphan" with no parent reference.

2. Example

@OneToMany(mappedBy = "order",
           cascade = CascadeType.ALL,
           orphanRemoval = true)
private List<OrderItem> items;

// Removing an item deletes its DB row
order.getItems().remove(item);

3. orphanRemoval vs CascadeType.REMOVE

  • CascadeType.REMOVE: deletes children only when the parent is deleted.
  • orphanRemoval: also deletes a child when it is merely disassociated from the parent.

Use it for strict parent-child ownership where a child cannot exist without its parent.

subtopics.name: @Entity
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Entity?

@Entity marks a Java class as a JPA entity, mapping it to a database table so its instances can be persisted.

2. Example

@Entity
class User {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
    private String name;
    // no-arg constructor, getters, setters
}

3. Requirements

  • Must have a public/protected no-arg constructor.
  • Must define a primary key with @Id.
  • Cannot be final (Hibernate needs to proxy it).
  • By default maps to a table with the class name; override with @Table.

Entities represent persistent domain objects; avoid exposing them directly in APIs — use DTOs.

subtopics.name: @Table
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Table?

@Table customizes the mapping between an entity and its database table — name, schema, and constraints. It is optional; without it, the table name defaults to the entity name.

2. Example

@Entity
@Table(
    name = "app_users",
    schema = "public",
    uniqueConstraints = @UniqueConstraint(columnNames = "email"),
    indexes = @Index(name = "idx_name", columnList = "name"))
class User { ... }

3. Key Attributes

  • name — table name.
  • schema/catalog — database schema.
  • uniqueConstraints — table-level unique constraints.
  • indexes — index definitions.

Use it when the table name differs from the class name or you need constraints/indexes defined at the entity level.

subtopics.name: @Id
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Id?

@Id marks a field as the entity's primary key, uniquely identifying each row. Every entity must have one.

2. Example

@Entity
class User {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;
}

3. Composite Keys

For a key made of multiple columns, use either:

  • @EmbeddedId with an @Embeddable key class, or
  • @IdClass with a separate id class.

4. Key Points

  • Often combined with @GeneratedValue for auto-generation.
  • The id type should be a wrapper (e.g., Long) so a null indicates a transient entity.

subtopics.name: @GeneratedValue
subtopics.content:
1. What is @GeneratedValue?

@GeneratedValue tells JPA to auto-generate the primary key value, using a chosen strategy.

2. Strategies

  • IDENTITY: uses an auto-increment column; the DB generates the id on insert.
  • SEQUENCE: uses a database sequence (efficient, supports batching) — best for PostgreSQL/Oracle.
  • TABLE: uses a separate table to simulate a sequence (portable but slow).
  • AUTO: lets the provider pick based on the database.

3. Example

@Id
@GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.SEQUENCE,
                generator = "user_seq")
@SequenceGenerator(name = "user_seq", sequenceName = "user_sequence",
                   allocationSize = 50)
private Long id;

SEQUENCE is generally preferred over IDENTITY because IDENTITY disables JDBC batch inserts.

subtopics.name: @Column
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Column?

@Column customizes the mapping between an entity field and its database column. It is optional; without it, the column name defaults to the field name.

2. Example

@Column(
    name = "full_name",
    nullable = false,
    length = 100,
    unique = true)
private String name;

@Column(precision = 10, scale = 2)
private BigDecimal price;

3. Key Attributes

  • name — column name.
  • nullable — NOT NULL constraint.
  • length — column length (for strings).
  • unique — unique constraint.
  • precision/scale — for decimals.
  • insertable/updatable — control participation in SQL.

Use it to enforce database constraints and map fields to differently-named columns.

subtopics.name: @Transient
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Transient?

@Transient marks a field to be excluded from persistence — JPA will not map it to a column or save/load it. It is used for derived or temporary values.

2. Example

@Entity
class User {
    private String firstName;
    private String lastName;

    @Transient
    private String fullName; // computed, not stored

    public String getFullName() {
        return firstName + " " + lastName;
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • Use it for calculated fields, caches, or helper flags.
  • It is the JPA @Transient (jakarta.persistence), not the Java transient keyword — though the keyword also excludes a field from JPA mapping.

subtopics.name: @OneToOne
subtopics.content:
1. What is @OneToOne?

@OneToOne maps a one-to-one relationship, where one entity is associated with exactly one instance of another (e.g., User ↔ Profile).

2. Example

@Entity
class User {
    @OneToOne(cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    @JoinColumn(name = "profile_id")
    private Profile profile; // owning side (has FK)
}

@Entity
class Profile {
    @OneToOne(mappedBy = "profile")
    private User user; // inverse side
}

3. Key Points

  • The owning side holds the foreign key via @JoinColumn.
  • The inverse side uses mappedBy.
  • Default fetch is EAGER; consider LAZY for performance.
  • Can also be modeled with a shared primary key (@MapsId).

subtopics.name: @OneToMany
subtopics.content:
1. What is @OneToMany?

@OneToMany maps a one-to-many relationship — one parent entity related to many children (e.g., Order → OrderItems).

2. Example (Bidirectional)

@Entity
class Order {
    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "order",
               cascade = CascadeType.ALL,
               orphanRemoval = true)
    private List<OrderItem> items = new ArrayList<>();
}

@Entity
class OrderItem {
    @ManyToOne
    @JoinColumn(name = "order_id")
    private Order order; // owning side
}

3. Key Points

  • The many side (@ManyToOne) owns the foreign key; the one side uses mappedBy.
  • Default fetch is LAZY.
  • Prefer mappedBy (bidirectional) over a unidirectional @OneToMany with a join table to avoid extra tables and N+1 issues.

subtopics.name: @ManyToOne
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ManyToOne?

@ManyToOne maps the many side of a relationship — many entities reference one instance of another (e.g., many OrderItems belong to one Order). This side owns the foreign key.

2. Example

@Entity
class OrderItem {
    @ManyToOne(fetch = FetchType.LAZY)
    @JoinColumn(name = "order_id")
    private Order order;
}

3. Key Points

  • It is the owning side — the FK column lives in this entity's table.
  • Default fetch is EAGER; overriding to LAZY is a common best practice.
  • Usually paired with a @OneToMany(mappedBy=...) on the parent for a bidirectional relationship.

@ManyToOne is the most common and efficient way to model associations.

subtopics.name: @ManyToMany
subtopics.content:
1. What is @ManyToMany?

@ManyToMany maps a many-to-many relationship, where many instances of one entity relate to many of another (e.g., Students ↔ Courses). It requires a join table.

2. Example

@Entity
class Student {
    @ManyToMany
    @JoinTable(name = "student_course",
        joinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "student_id"),
        inverseJoinColumns = @JoinColumn(name = "course_id"))
    private Set<Course> courses = new HashSet<>();
}

@Entity
class Course {
    @ManyToMany(mappedBy = "courses")
    private Set<Student> students = new HashSet<>();
}

3. Key Points

  • A join table holds the two foreign keys.
  • Default fetch is LAZY.
  • If the relationship needs extra columns (e.g., enrollment date), model the join table as its own entity with two @ManyToOne instead.
  • Prefer Set over List to avoid duplicate-related issues.

subtopics.name: mappedBy
subtopics.content:
1. What is mappedBy?

mappedBy designates the inverse (non-owning) side of a bidirectional relationship. It tells JPA that the relationship is already mapped by a field on the other entity, so no extra foreign key or join table is created for this side.

2. Example

@Entity
class Order {
    @OneToMany(mappedBy = "order") // inverse side
    private List<OrderItem> items;
}

@Entity
class OrderItem {
    @ManyToOne
    @JoinColumn(name = "order_id")
    private Order order; // owning side (has FK)
}

3. Key Points

  • The value of mappedBy is the field name on the owning side.
  • The owning side controls the FK and persists the relationship.
  • Updating only the inverse side won't persist the association — always set the owning side.

subtopics.name: JPQL
subtopics.content:
1. What is JPQL?

JPQL (Jakarta Persistence Query Language) is an object-oriented query language that operates on entities and their fields, not database tables and columns. It is database-independent.

2. Example

// Uses entity name 'User' and field 'name', not table/columns
@Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.age > :age")
List<User> findOlderThan(@Param("age") int age);

// JOIN FETCH to avoid N+1
@Query("SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items WHERE o.id = :id")
Order findWithItems(@Param("id") Long id);

3. Key Points

  • References entity names and field names (case-sensitive), which JPA translates to SQL.
  • Portable across databases (the provider generates dialect-specific SQL).
  • Supports joins, aggregates, subqueries, and JOIN FETCH for eager loading.

Use native SQL only when JPQL can't express something DB-specific.

subtopics.name: Native Query
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Native Query?

A native query is raw SQL executed directly against the database, bypassing JPQL. Use it for database-specific features or complex queries JPQL cannot express.

2. Example

@Query(value = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > :age",
       nativeQuery = true)
List<User> findOlder(@Param("age") int age);

3. JPQL vs Native Query

JPQLNative Query
Works on entitiesWorks on tables/columns
Database-independentDatabase-specific
Limited to JPA featuresFull SQL power

4. Trade-offs

  • Pros: full SQL, DB-specific optimizations, window functions, etc.
  • Cons: not portable, bypasses some JPA features, harder to maintain.

Prefer JPQL; drop to native SQL only when necessary.

subtopics.name: Criteria API
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Criteria API?

The Criteria API builds queries programmatically and type-safely in Java code, instead of writing query strings. It is ideal for dynamic queries built from variable conditions.

2. Example

CriteriaBuilder cb = em.getCriteriaBuilder();
CriteriaQuery<User> cq = cb.createQuery(User.class);
Root<User> root = cq.from(User.class);

cq.select(root)
  .where(cb.greaterThan(root.get("age"), 18));

List<User> result = em.createQuery(cq).getResultList();

3. Pros and Cons

  • Pros: type-safe, compile-time checked, great for dynamic filters.
  • Cons: verbose and harder to read than JPQL.

For complex dynamic queries in Spring Data, Specifications (built on the Criteria API) offer a cleaner, composable alternative.

subtopics.name: Specifications
subtopics.content:
1. What are Specifications?

Spring Data Specifications wrap the JPA Criteria API to build dynamic, composable queries. Each specification represents a predicate that can be combined with and()/or().

2. Example

interface UserRepo extends JpaRepository<User,Long>,
                            JpaSpecificationExecutor<User> {}

static Specification<User> ageGreater(int age) {
    return (root, query, cb) ->
        cb.greaterThan(root.get("age"), age);
}

// Compose
repo.findAll(ageGreater(18).and(nameLike("A%")));

3. Benefits

  • Build queries dynamically based on optional filters (e.g., search forms).
  • Reusable, composable predicates.
  • Type-safe (Criteria-based) and integrates with pagination/sorting.

Specifications are the go-to for dynamic filtering in Spring Data JPA.

subtopics.name: PagingAndSortingRepository
subtopics.content:
1. What is PagingAndSortingRepository?

PagingAndSortingRepository extends CrudRepository and adds methods for pagination and sorting out of the box.

2. Added Methods

  • findAll(Sort sort) — return all results sorted.
  • findAll(Pageable pageable) — return a page of results.

3. Example

interface UserRepo extends PagingAndSortingRepository<User,Long> {}

Pageable pageable = PageRequest.of(0, 20, Sort.by("name"));
Page<User> page = repo.findAll(pageable);
page.getContent();      // the 20 users
page.getTotalElements(); // total count

4. Repository Hierarchy

Repository
 └─ CrudRepository
     └─ PagingAndSortingRepository
         └─ JpaRepository

JpaRepository includes all of this plus JPA-specific methods.

subtopics.name: CrudRepository vs JpaRepository
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Both are Spring Data repository interfaces; JpaRepository extends CrudRepository (via PagingAndSortingRepository) and adds more features.

2. Comparison

CrudRepositoryJpaRepository
Basic CRUD methodsCRUD + paging + sorting + JPA extras
save, findById, delete, countAdds findAll(Pageable), flush, saveAndFlush, batch ops, getReferenceById
Returns IterableReturns List
Generic (any store)JPA-specific

3. Which to Use

Use JpaRepository in most JPA applications — it provides the richest API (pagination, flushing, batch deletes). Use CrudRepository when you want a minimal surface or a store-agnostic abstraction.

subtopics.name: save() vs saveAndFlush()
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

  • save() — persists the entity to the persistence context; the actual SQL may be delayed until the transaction commits (write-behind).
  • saveAndFlush() — persists and immediately flushes changes to the database within the current transaction.

2. Example

repo.save(user);          // INSERT may run at commit
repo.saveAndFlush(user);  // INSERT runs now

3. When to Use saveAndFlush()

  • You need the generated ID or DB-computed values immediately.
  • You must run a native query in the same transaction that depends on the saved data.
  • You want DB constraint violations to surface right away.

For most cases, save() is sufficient and more efficient (batched flush). Use saveAndFlush() only when immediate synchronization is required.

subtopics.name: flush()
subtopics.content:
1. What is flush()?

flush() forces the persistence context to synchronize pending changes to the database immediately, executing the queued SQL — without committing the transaction.

2. Flush vs Commit

  • flush(): sends SQL to the DB; changes are visible within the transaction but can still be rolled back.
  • commit(): makes the changes permanent and ends the transaction (also triggers a flush).

3. Flush Modes

  • AUTO (default): flushes before query execution and at commit.
  • COMMIT: flushes only at commit.

4. Why Manually Flush?

To surface constraint violations early, obtain generated keys, or ensure a subsequent native query sees the changes. Excessive manual flushing hurts batching performance.

subtopics.name: Transaction Propagation
subtopics.content:
1. What is Transaction Propagation?

Propagation defines how a transactional method behaves when called within an existing transaction — whether it joins, suspends, or creates a new one.

2. Common Types

  • REQUIRED (default): join the current transaction, or create one if none exists.
  • REQUIRES_NEW: always start a new transaction, suspending any existing one.
  • SUPPORTS: join if a transaction exists, otherwise run non-transactionally.
  • MANDATORY: must run within an existing transaction, else error.
  • NOT_SUPPORTED: run non-transactionally, suspending any existing transaction.
  • NEVER: fail if a transaction exists.
  • NESTED: run in a nested transaction with a savepoint.

3. Example

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
void auditLog() { ... } // commits independently

REQUIRES_NEW is common for audit logging that must persist even if the outer transaction rolls back.

subtopics.name: Isolation Levels
subtopics.content:
1. What are Isolation Levels?

Isolation levels control how/when changes made by one transaction become visible to others, balancing consistency vs concurrency.

2. Levels (lowest to highest)

  • READ_UNCOMMITTED: can read uncommitted changes (dirty reads).
  • READ_COMMITTED: reads only committed data; prevents dirty reads (common default).
  • REPEATABLE_READ: same rows read twice give the same result; prevents non-repeatable reads.
  • SERIALIZABLE: full isolation; prevents phantom reads but lowest concurrency.

3. Concurrency Problems

LevelDirtyNon-repeatablePhantom
READ_UNCOMMITTEDYesYesYes
READ_COMMITTEDNoYesYes
REPEATABLE_READNoNoYes
SERIALIZABLENoNoNo
@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.REPEATABLE_READ)

subtopics.name: Optimistic Locking
subtopics.content:
1. What is Optimistic Locking?

Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare. It doesn't lock rows; instead it uses a version column to detect concurrent modifications at update time.

2. How it works

@Entity
class Account {
    @Version
    private int version;
    private BigDecimal balance;
}

On update, Hibernate adds WHERE version = ?. If the row's version changed since it was read, the update affects 0 rows and throws OptimisticLockException.

3. Key Points

  • No database locks — high concurrency.
  • Best for read-heavy systems with rare conflicts.
  • The application must handle the exception (e.g., retry or notify the user).

Contrast with pessimistic locking, which locks rows upfront.

subtopics.name: Pessimistic Locking
subtopics.content:
1. What is Pessimistic Locking?

Pessimistic locking assumes conflicts are likely and locks the database rows when read, preventing other transactions from modifying (or sometimes reading) them until the lock is released.

2. Lock Modes

  • PESSIMISTIC_READ: shared lock — others can read but not write.
  • PESSIMISTIC_WRITE: exclusive lock — others cannot read or write.

3. Example

@Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
@Query("SELECT a FROM Account a WHERE a.id = :id")
Account findForUpdate(@Param("id") Long id);
// generates SELECT ... FOR UPDATE

4. Optimistic vs Pessimistic

  • Optimistic: no locks, version check — best for low contention.
  • Pessimistic: real DB locks — best for high contention (e.g., inventory, banking), but can cause blocking/deadlocks.

subtopics.name: N+1 Problem
subtopics.content:
1. What is the N+1 Problem?

The N+1 problem is a performance issue where fetching N parent entities triggers N additional queries — one per parent — to load their lazy associations, resulting in N+1 total queries.

2. Example of the Problem

List<Order> orders = repo.findAll();   // 1 query
for (Order o : orders) {
    o.getItems().size();               // N queries (one each)
}

3. Solutions

  • JOIN FETCH: SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items — one query.
  • @EntityGraph: declaratively fetch associations.
