  • Batch fetching: @BatchSize / hibernate.default_batch_fetch_size — loads children in IN-clause batches.
  • DTO projections: select only needed fields.

The N+1 problem is one of the most common JPA performance pitfalls — always check generated SQL.

subtopics.name: Batch Insert
subtopics.content:
1. What is Batch Insert?

Batch insert groups multiple INSERT statements into a single database round-trip using JDBC batching, dramatically improving performance for bulk data.

2. Configuration

spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.order_inserts=true
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.order_updates=true

3. Example

@Transactional
void saveAll(List<User> users) {
    for (int i = 0; i < users.size(); i++) {
        em.persist(users.get(i));
        if (i % 50 == 0) { em.flush(); em.clear(); }
    }
}

4. Key Points

  • IDENTITY generation disables batch inserts — use SEQUENCE.
  • Periodically flush() and clear() to control memory.
  • Batching turns hundreds of round-trips into a few, hugely reducing latency.

subtopics.name: Hibernate Interview Questions
subtopics.content:
Common Hibernate Interview Topics

  • get() vs load(): get() hits the DB immediately and returns null if absent; load() returns a lazy proxy and throws if the entity doesn't exist on access.
  • save() vs persist(): save() returns the generated id and can run outside a transaction; persist() is JPA-standard, returns void, and is transaction-bound.
  • merge() vs update(): update() reattaches a detached entity (fails if one is already managed); merge() copies state into a managed instance and returns it.
  • Session vs SessionFactory: factory is heavyweight/thread-safe; session is lightweight/single-threaded.
  • Caching: L1 (session) always on; L2 (factory) optional.
  • Dirty checking, lazy loading, N+1, cascade, fetch types — recurring themes.

Key Advice

Know the entity lifecycle, the difference between JPA-standard and Hibernate-specific methods, and how to diagnose performance issues (enable show_sql and inspect generated queries).

subtopics.name: JPA Best Practices
subtopics.content:
JPA / Hibernate Best Practices

  • Use LAZY fetching by default and fetch eagerly on demand with JOIN FETCH/entity graphs.
  • Avoid the N+1 problem — inspect generated SQL and use batch fetching.
  • Use DTO projections for read-only queries instead of loading full entities.
  • Prefer SEQUENCE over IDENTITY to allow batch inserts.
  • Don't expose entities in APIs — map to DTOs.
  • Keep transactions short and at the service layer.
  • Use optimistic locking (@Version) for concurrent updates.
  • Implement equals()/hashCode() carefully — base on a business key, not the generated id.
  • Enable batch processing for bulk operations; flush/clear periodically.
  • Use pagination for large result sets.
  • Avoid CascadeType.REMOVE on shared entities.

These practices keep persistence efficient, safe, and maintainable.

subtopics.name: @Transactional
subtopics.content:
1. What is @Transactional?

@Transactional declaratively manages database transactions. Spring begins a transaction before the annotated method and commits on success or rolls back on failure, using AOP proxies behind the scenes.

2. Example

@Service
class TransferService {
    @Transactional
    public void transfer(Long from, Long to, BigDecimal amt) {
        debit(from, amt);
        credit(to, amt);
        // both succeed or both roll back
    }
}

3. Key Attributes

  • propagation — how it joins/creates transactions.
  • isolation — concurrency isolation level.
  • readOnly — optimization hint for reads.
  • rollbackFor/noRollbackFor — which exceptions trigger rollback.
  • timeout — max duration.

Apply it at the service layer, on public methods. Self-invocation bypasses the proxy.

subtopics.name: Transaction Lifecycle
subtopics.content:
Transaction Lifecycle

  1. Begin: the proxy starts a transaction (via the PlatformTransactionManager) before the method runs.
  2. Execute: the business method runs; SQL is issued within the transaction.
  3. Flush: pending changes are synchronized to the DB.
  4. Commit: if the method returns normally, changes are made permanent.
  5. Rollback: if an unchecked exception is thrown, all changes are undone.
  6. Cleanup: the connection/persistence context is released.

Behind the Scenes

proxy: begin
try {
    method();
    commit;
} catch (RuntimeException e) {
    rollback;
    throw e;
}

This around-advice behavior is managed by Spring's TransactionInterceptor.

subtopics.name: Propagation Types
subtopics.content:
1. What is Propagation?

Propagation determines how a transactional method behaves relative to an existing transaction.

2. The Seven Types

  • REQUIRED (default): join existing, or create new.
  • REQUIRES_NEW: always create a new transaction, suspend the current.
  • SUPPORTS: join if present, else non-transactional.
  • NOT_SUPPORTED: run non-transactionally, suspend current.
  • MANDATORY: require an existing transaction, else throw.
  • NEVER: throw if a transaction exists.
  • NESTED: nested transaction with a savepoint.

3. Example

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED)
void process() { ... }

REQUIRED covers most cases; REQUIRES_NEW is used for independent units like audit logs.

subtopics.name: REQUIRED vs REQUIRES_NEW
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

REQUIRED (default)REQUIRES_NEW
Joins existing transactionAlways creates a new one
Creates one if none existsSuspends the current transaction
Rollback affects the whole unitInner commits/rolls back independently

2. Example

@Transactional // REQUIRED
void placeOrder() {
    saveOrder();
    auditLog(); // REQUIRES_NEW -> persists even if placeOrder fails
}

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
void auditLog() { ... }

3. When to Use REQUIRES_NEW

  • Audit logging that must survive an outer rollback.
  • Sending notifications independently of the main transaction.

Caveat: REQUIRES_NEW uses a separate connection; overuse can exhaust the connection pool and cause self-deadlocks.

subtopics.name: Isolation Levels
subtopics.content:
1. What are Isolation Levels?

Isolation levels define how concurrent transactions interact, controlling the trade-off between data consistency and performance.

2. Levels & Problems Prevented

LevelDirtyNon-repeatablePhantom
READ_UNCOMMITTEDYesYesYes
READ_COMMITTEDNoYesYes
REPEATABLE_READNoNoYes
SERIALIZABLENoNoNo

3. Example

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.READ_COMMITTED)
void process() { ... }

Default is Isolation.DEFAULT (uses the database's default, usually READ_COMMITTED). Higher isolation means more consistency but less concurrency.

subtopics.name: Rollback Rules
subtopics.content:
1. Default Rollback Behavior

By default, Spring rolls back a transaction only on unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException and Error). Checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default.

2. Customizing Rollback

// Roll back on a checked exception too
@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)
void process() throws IOException { ... }

// Do NOT roll back on a specific exception
@Transactional(noRollbackFor = ValidationException.class)
void save() { ... }

3. Key Points

  • Use rollbackFor to force rollback on checked exceptions.
  • Use noRollbackFor to commit despite certain runtime exceptions.
  • Catching an exception inside the method silently prevents rollback — rethrow if needed.

This checked-vs-unchecked default is a very common interview and bug source.

subtopics.name: Checked vs Runtime Exception Rollback
subtopics.content:
1. The Default Rule

  • Runtime (unchecked) exceptions → transaction rolls back automatically.
  • Checked exceptions → transaction commits (no rollback) by default.

2. Why?

Spring follows the convention that checked exceptions are recoverable business conditions, while unchecked exceptions indicate unexpected failures that should undo the work.

3. Overriding the Default

// Force rollback on a checked exception
@Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class)
void transfer() throws BusinessException { ... }

4. Common Pitfall

@Transactional
void save() {
    try {
        risky();
    } catch (RuntimeException e) {
        // swallowed -> NO rollback happens!
    }
}

If you catch and swallow the exception, Spring never sees it and will commit. Rethrow or call setRollbackOnly().

subtopics.name: Read Only Transactions
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Read-Only Transaction?

@Transactional(readOnly = true) marks a transaction that only reads data, giving the persistence provider and database a hint to optimize.

2. Example

@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public List<User> findAll() {
    return repository.findAll();
}

3. Benefits

  • Hibernate skips dirty checking and can disable automatic flushing — better performance.
  • Some databases route read-only transactions to read replicas.
  • Signals intent clearly and helps prevent accidental writes.

4. Key Point

readOnly = true is primarily an optimization hint, not a strict guarantee — it doesn't necessarily block all writes at the DB level, but Hibernate avoids flushing changes. Use it on all query/read service methods.

subtopics.name: Nested Transactions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Nested Transactions?

A nested transaction (Propagation.NESTED) runs within an outer transaction but uses a savepoint. The inner part can roll back to the savepoint without rolling back the entire outer transaction.

2. Example

@Transactional // outer
void process() {
    saveMain();
    try {
        saveOptional(); // NESTED
    } catch (Exception e) {
        // only the nested part rolls back to the savepoint
    }
}

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NESTED)
void saveOptional() { ... }

3. NESTED vs REQUIRES_NEW

  • NESTED: uses a savepoint in the same physical transaction/connection; if the outer rolls back, the nested changes are lost too.
  • REQUIRES_NEW: a fully independent transaction on a separate connection.

NESTED requires a JDBC driver/manager that supports savepoints (e.g., DataSourceTransactionManager).

subtopics.name: Transaction Manager
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Transaction Manager?

The PlatformTransactionManager is the central abstraction that actually begins, commits, and rolls back transactions. @Transactional delegates to it.

2. Common Implementations

  • DataSourceTransactionManager: for plain JDBC.
  • JpaTransactionManager: for JPA/Hibernate.
  • HibernateTransactionManager: for native Hibernate.
  • JtaTransactionManager: for distributed (XA) transactions across multiple resources.

3. Auto-Configuration

Spring Boot auto-configures the right transaction manager based on the classpath — e.g., JpaTransactionManager when JPA is present — so you rarely configure it manually.

4. Programmatic Use

// TransactionTemplate for programmatic control
transactionTemplate.execute(status -> {
    // work; call status.setRollbackOnly() to roll back
    return result;
});

subtopics.name: Distributed Transactions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Distributed Transactions?

A distributed transaction spans multiple resources (e.g., two databases, or a database and a message queue) and must commit or roll back atomically across all of them.

2. Two-Phase Commit (2PC)

  1. Prepare: a coordinator asks each resource to prepare and vote.
  2. Commit: if all vote yes, commit all; if any votes no, roll back all.

Managed via JTA and JtaTransactionManager (with an XA-capable driver and transaction coordinator like Atomikos/Narayana).

3. Microservices Alternatives

  • Saga pattern: a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions on failure.
  • Outbox pattern + eventual consistency.

2PC is powerful but slow and reduces availability, so distributed systems often prefer sagas and eventual consistency.

subtopics.name: XA Transactions
subtopics.content:
1. What are XA Transactions?

XA is a standard (X/Open) for distributed transactions using two-phase commit. An XA transaction coordinates multiple XA-compliant resources (databases, JMS brokers) so they commit or roll back together atomically.

2. Components

  • Transaction Manager (coordinator): drives the 2PC protocol.
  • Resource Managers: XA-capable resources (e.g., a database's XA data source).

3. In Spring

Use JtaTransactionManager with an embedded JTA provider like Atomikos or Narayana, plus XA data sources.

4. Trade-offs

  • Pro: strong atomicity across resources.
  • Con: slower (extra round-trips), locks held longer, reduced availability, complex recovery.

Modern microservices often avoid XA in favor of the saga pattern and eventual consistency.

subtopics.name: Transaction Synchronization
subtopics.content:
1. What is Transaction Synchronization?

Transaction synchronization lets you hook custom logic into transaction lifecycle events — most importantly, to run code after commit or after rollback.

2. Example (after commit)

TransactionSynchronizationManager.registerSynchronization(
    new TransactionSynchronization() {
        public void afterCommit() {
            sendNotification(); // runs only if committed
        }
    });

The modern, cleaner approach uses @TransactionalEventListener: 

@TransactionalEventListener(phase = AFTER_COMMIT)
void onOrderPlaced(OrderPlacedEvent e) { ... }

3. Why It Matters

  • Send emails/messages only after the DB commit succeeds, avoiding notifications for rolled-back work.
  • Coordinate caches or external systems with transaction outcome.

Spring internally uses synchronization to manage the persistence context and connection binding per transaction.

subtopics.name: Self Invocation Problem
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Self-Invocation Problem?

@Transactional works through a proxy. When a method calls another method in the same class directly, the call bypasses the proxy, so the transactional annotation on the inner method is ignored.

2. Example of the Problem

@Service
class OrderService {
    public void process() {
        save(); // internal call -> proxy bypassed, NOT transactional
    }

    @Transactional
    public void save() { ... }
}

3. Solutions

  • Move the transactional method to a separate bean and inject it.
  • Self-inject the proxy (inject the bean into itself) and call through it.
  • Use AopContext.currentProxy() (requires exposeProxy=true).
  • Use programmatic transactions (TransactionTemplate).

The same limitation applies to other proxy-based annotations like @Async and @Cacheable.

subtopics.name: Transaction Best Practices
subtopics.content:
Transaction Best Practices

  • Apply @Transactional at the service layer, not in controllers or repositories.
  • Keep transactions short — avoid long-running logic, remote/API calls, or user waits inside a transaction.
  • Use readOnly = true for query methods.
  • Beware self-invocation — internal calls bypass the proxy.
  • Understand rollback rules — checked exceptions don't roll back by default; set rollbackFor as needed.
  • Don't swallow exceptions that should trigger rollback.
  • Use REQUIRES_NEW sparingly — it consumes extra connections.
  • Choose the right isolation level — higher isn't always better.
  • Only public methods are transactional (proxy limitation).
  • Do post-commit side effects (emails, events) with @TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT).

These practices ensure data integrity while keeping the system performant and predictable.

subtopics.name: Spring Security Architecture
subtopics.content:
1. Overview

Spring Security is a framework for authentication and authorization. Its architecture is built on a chain of servlet filters that intercept every request before it reaches the application.

2. Core Components

  • SecurityFilterChain: ordered filters processing each request.
  • AuthenticationManager: performs authentication.
  • AuthenticationProvider: validates credentials (e.g., against a UserDetailsService).
  • UserDetailsService: loads user data.
  • SecurityContext: holds the authenticated principal.
  • AccessDecisionManager / AuthorizationManager: enforces authorization.

3. Flow

Request -> Filter Chain -> Authentication
        -> AuthenticationManager -> Provider
        -> UserDetailsService + PasswordEncoder
        -> SecurityContext -> Authorization -> Controller

The filter-based design makes security modular and pluggable.

subtopics.name: Authentication vs Authorization
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

AuthenticationAuthorization
Who are you?What are you allowed to do?
Verifies identity (credentials)Verifies permissions/roles
Happens firstHappens after authentication
e.g., login with passworde.g., only ADMIN can delete

2. Examples

  • Authentication: validating a username/password or JWT.
  • Authorization: checking a user has ROLE_ADMIN before allowing an action.

3. In Spring Security

http
  .authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a
     .requestMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")  // authorization
     .anyRequest().authenticated())                  // authentication
  .formLogin();

Remember: Authentication before Authorization — you must know who someone is before deciding what they can do.

subtopics.name: Security Filter Chain
subtopics.content:
1. What is the Security Filter Chain?

The SecurityFilterChain is an ordered list of servlet filters that every request passes through. Each filter handles a specific concern (authentication, CSRF, authorization, etc.).

2. Key Filters (in order)

  • SecurityContextPersistenceFilter — loads/stores the SecurityContext.
  • UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter — form login.
  • BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter / custom JWT filter — token auth.
  • CsrfFilter — CSRF protection.
  • FilterSecurityInterceptor / AuthorizationFilter — enforces access rules.

3. Configuration

@Bean
SecurityFilterChain chain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    http.authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a.anyRequest().authenticated())
        .httpBasic(withDefaults());
    return http.build();
}

Requests flow through the chain in order; a filter can stop the chain (e.g., reject unauthenticated access).

subtopics.name: UserDetailsService
subtopics.content:
1. What is UserDetailsService?

UserDetailsService is the interface Spring Security uses to load user-specific data during authentication. It has a single method: loadUserByUsername(String username).

2. Example

@Service
class MyUserDetailsService implements UserDetailsService {
    private final UserRepository repo;

    public UserDetails loadUserByUsername(String username) {
        User u = repo.findByUsername(username)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new UsernameNotFoundException(username));
        return org.springframework.security.core.userdetails.User
            .withUsername(u.getUsername())
            .password(u.getPassword())
            .roles(u.getRole())
            .build();
    }
}

3. Key Points

  • Returns a UserDetails with username, (encoded) password, and authorities.
  • The AuthenticationProvider compares the submitted password (via PasswordEncoder) with the stored one.
  • Throw UsernameNotFoundException if the user doesn't exist.

subtopics.name: PasswordEncoder
subtopics.content:
1. What is PasswordEncoder?

PasswordEncoder is the interface for hashing and verifying passwords. Passwords must never be stored in plain text; they are hashed one-way.

2. Methods

  • encode(rawPassword) — hash the password for storage.
  • matches(raw, encoded) — verify a submitted password.

3. Example

@Bean
PasswordEncoder passwordEncoder() {
    return new BCryptPasswordEncoder();
}

// Registration
user.setPassword(passwordEncoder.encode(rawPassword));

4. Implementations

  • BCryptPasswordEncoder — most common, includes a salt.
  • Argon2 / SCrypt / PBKDF2 — strong alternatives.
  • DelegatingPasswordEncoder — supports multiple formats with a prefix, enabling migration.

Never use plain text or fast hashes like MD5/SHA-1 for passwords.

subtopics.name: BCrypt
subtopics.content:
1. What is BCrypt?

BCrypt is a password-hashing algorithm designed to be slow and adaptive, making brute-force attacks expensive. It automatically incorporates a random salt into each hash.

2. Key Features

  • Built-in salt: the same password produces different hashes, preventing rainbow-table attacks.
  • Work factor (cost): configurable strength; higher cost = slower = more secure. Default is 10.
  • Adaptive: the cost can be raised over time as hardware improves.

3. Example

BCryptPasswordEncoder encoder = new BCryptPasswordEncoder(12); // cost 12
String hash = encoder.encode("myPassword");
boolean ok = encoder.matches("myPassword", hash);

4. Why the Salt Is Embedded

The generated hash string contains the algorithm, cost, and salt, so matches() can re-hash the input with the same parameters — no separate salt storage needed.

subtopics.name: JWT Basics
subtopics.content:
1. What is JWT?

A JSON Web Token (JWT) is a compact, self-contained token for securely transmitting claims between parties. It is commonly used for stateless authentication.

2. Structure

A JWT has three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: 

header.payload.signature
  • Header: token type and signing algorithm.
  • Payload: claims (user id, roles, expiry).
  • Signature: verifies integrity, created with a secret/private key.

3. How it works

  1. User logs in; server issues a signed JWT.
  2. Client sends it in the Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  3. Server verifies the signature and reads claims — no server-side session needed.

4. Key Points

  • Stateless and scalable.
  • Payload is encoded, not encrypted — never put secrets in it.
  • Use short expiry + refresh tokens; can't be easily revoked before expiry.

subtopics.name: OAuth2 Basics
subtopics.content:
1. What is OAuth2?

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that lets an application access resources on behalf of a user without sharing the user's password. It issues access tokens instead.

2. Roles

  • Resource Owner: the user.
  • Client: the app requesting access.
  • Authorization Server: issues tokens (e.g., Google, Keycloak).
  • Resource Server: hosts the protected API.

3. Common Grant Types

  • Authorization Code: for web apps (with PKCE for SPAs/mobile).
  • Client Credentials: for service-to-service.
  • Refresh Token: obtain a new access token.

4. OAuth2 vs OIDC

OAuth2 handles authorization; OpenID Connect (OIDC) is a layer on top that adds authentication (identity via an ID token). Spring Security provides spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client and -resource-server.

subtopics.name: Session vs Token Authentication
subtopics.content:
1. Difference

Session-BasedToken-Based (JWT)
Server stores session stateStateless — state in the token
Session ID in a cookieToken in Authorization header
Harder to scale (sticky sessions/shared store)Scales easily across servers
Easy to revoke (delete session)Hard to revoke before expiry

2. Session-Based

After login, the server creates a session and returns a JSESSIONID cookie. Each request carries the cookie; the server looks up the session. Good for traditional server-rendered web apps.

3. Token-Based

The server issues a signed JWT; the client sends it in each request. No server-side storage — ideal for REST APIs, SPAs, and mobile, and for horizontal scaling.

Choose sessions for monoliths with server-side rendering, tokens for stateless APIs and microservices.

subtopics.name: CSRF
subtopics.content:
1. What is CSRF?

Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) is an attack where a malicious site tricks an authenticated user's browser into sending an unwanted request to a trusted site, abusing the user's existing session/cookies.

2. How Protection Works

Spring Security issues a unique, unpredictable CSRF token that must be included in state-changing requests (POST/PUT/DELETE). Since an attacker's site can't read this token, forged requests are rejected.

3. Configuration

// Enabled by default for browser clients
http.csrf(csrf -> csrf
    .csrfTokenRepository(CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse()));

// Often disabled for stateless JWT APIs
http.csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable());

4. Key Points

  • CSRF matters for cookie/session-based auth.
  • For stateless token (JWT) APIs, CSRF is commonly disabled since tokens aren't sent automatically by the browser.

subtopics.name: CORS in Security
subtopics.content:
1. CORS with Spring Security

CORS controls cross-origin browser requests. When Spring Security is present, CORS must be configured within the security filter chain — otherwise preflight requests may be blocked before your MVC CORS config runs.

2. Configuration

@Bean
SecurityFilterChain chain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    http.cors(withDefaults())  // enable CORS in the chain
        .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
        .authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a.anyRequest().authenticated());
    return http.build();
}

@Bean
CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() {
    CorsConfiguration c = new CorsConfiguration();
    c.setAllowedOrigins(List.of("https://app.example.com"));
    c.setAllowedMethods(List.of("GET","POST","PUT","DELETE"));
    c.setAllowedHeaders(List.of("*"));
    UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource s = new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource();
    s.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", c);
    return s;
}

3. Key Points

  • Call http.cors() so Security honors the CORS config.
  • Preflight OPTIONS requests must be permitted.
  • Avoid wildcard origins with credentials in production.

subtopics.name: Method Security
subtopics.content:
1. What is Method Security?

Method-level security applies authorization rules directly on methods (usually in the service layer) using annotations, complementing URL-based security.

2. Enabling It

@Configuration
@EnableMethodSecurity   // Spring Security 6+
class SecurityConfig { }

3. Annotations

  • @PreAuthorize: checks before the method — supports SpEL.
  • @PostAuthorize: checks after, can inspect the return value.
  • @Secured: role-based, simpler syntax.
  • @RolesAllowed: JSR-250 equivalent.

4. Example

@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
void deleteUser(Long id) { ... }

@PreAuthorize("#userId == authentication.principal.id")
User getProfile(Long userId) { ... }

@PreAuthorize is the most powerful due to SpEL, enabling fine-grained, expression-based rules.

subtopics.name: SecurityContext
subtopics.content:
1. What is SecurityContext?

The SecurityContext holds the details of the currently authenticated user — the Authentication object (principal, credentials, authorities). It is stored in the SecurityContextHolder.

2. Accessing the Current User

Authentication auth =
    SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication();
String username = auth.getName();
Collection<? extends GrantedAuthority> roles = auth.getAuthorities();

// In a controller
MyUser user = (MyUser) auth.getPrincipal();

3. Storage Strategy

  • By default, the context is stored in a ThreadLocal — bound to the current request thread.
  • For async work, use MODE_INHERITABLETHREADLOCAL or propagate the context manually.

4. Key Point

Spring populates the SecurityContext during authentication and clears it at the end of the request. It's the single source of truth for "who is the current user."

subtopics.name: AuthenticationManager
subtopics.content:
1. What is AuthenticationManager?

The AuthenticationManager is the central interface that performs authentication. Its authenticate() method takes an Authentication request and returns a fully populated one if credentials are valid, or throws an AuthenticationException.

2. How it works

The default implementation, ProviderManager, delegates to a list of AuthenticationProviders. Each provider handles a type of authentication (e.g., DaoAuthenticationProvider uses a UserDetailsService + PasswordEncoder). 

AuthenticationManager
   -> ProviderManager
       -> DaoAuthenticationProvider
           -> UserDetailsService + PasswordEncoder

3. Example (custom login / JWT)

Authentication auth = authenticationManager.authenticate(
    new UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken(username, password));
SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(auth);

You typically expose it as a bean to build custom login endpoints (e.g., issuing a JWT after authentication).

subtopics.name: Spring Security Best Practices
subtopics.content:
Spring Security Best Practices

  • Always hash passwords with BCrypt/Argon2 — never store plain text.
  • Use HTTPS everywhere to protect credentials and tokens in transit.
  • Apply least privilege — grant the minimum roles/authorities needed.
  • Validate and sanitize all inputs to prevent injection and XSS.
  • Enable CSRF for session-based apps; disable only for stateless token APIs.
  • Use short-lived JWTs with refresh tokens; store secrets securely.
  • Configure CORS in the security filter chain with specific origins.
  • Use method security (@PreAuthorize) for fine-grained rules.
  • Secure Actuator and admin endpoints.
  • Don't expose stack traces or sensitive error details.
  • Keep dependencies updated to patch known vulnerabilities.
  • Log and monitor authentication failures and suspicious activity.

Security is layered — combine authentication, authorization, transport security, and input validation.

subtopics.name: Singleton Pattern
subtopics.content:
Singleton Pattern

The Singleton Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that ensures only one instance of a class exists throughout the application and provides a global access point to that instance.

Why Do We Need Singleton?

Some resources should have only one instance in the entire application.

Creating multiple instances may lead to inconsistent behavior, unnecessary memory usage, or resource conflicts.

Examples

  • Database Connection Manager
  • Logger
  • Configuration Manager
  • Cache Manager
  • Thread Pool Manager

Imagine every service creating its own database connection manager. 


Service A -> DB Manager 1

Service B -> DB Manager 2

Service C -> DB Manager 3

This leads to wasted resources and management complexity.

Instead: 


Service A
      \
       \
Service B -----> Singleton DB Manager
       /
      /
Service C

Problem Without Singleton


DatabaseManager db1 = new DatabaseManager();

DatabaseManager db2 = new DatabaseManager();

DatabaseManager db3 = new DatabaseManager();

Now three different objects exist.

This may create:

  • Memory wastage.
  • Resource contention.
  • Data inconsistency.

Singleton Solution

The Singleton Pattern restricts object creation by:

  1. Making the constructor private.
  2. Creating a static instance.
  3. Providing a public method to access the instance.

Basic Singleton Implementation


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Usage


Singleton s1 = Singleton.getInstance();

Singleton s2 = Singleton.getInstance();

System.out.println(s1 == s2);

Output: 


true

Both references point to the same object.

How It Works


First Call

getInstance()

instance == null

Create Object

Return Object

Subsequent calls: 


getInstance()

instance != null

Return Existing Object

Characteristics of Singleton

  • Only one object exists.
  • Global access point.
  • Object created on demand.
  • Controlled object lifecycle.

Eager Initialization Singleton

The object is created when the class is loaded. 


public class Singleton {

    private static final Singleton instance =
            new Singleton();

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Object created even if not used.
  • Memory wastage possible.

Lazy Initialization Singleton

Object is created only when required. 


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Saves memory.
  • Object created only when needed.

Disadvantages

  • Not thread safe.

Thread Safety Problem

Suppose two threads call getInstance() simultaneously. 


Thread 1 -> instance == null

Thread 2 -> instance == null

Both threads create separate objects. 


Singleton Object 1

Singleton Object 2

Singleton guarantee is broken.

Thread Safe Singleton


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static synchronized Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Performance overhead due to synchronization.

Double Checked Locking

This is the most common interview implementation. 


public class Singleton {

    private static volatile Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {

            synchronized (Singleton.class) {

                if(instance == null) {
                    instance = new Singleton();
                }
            }
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • High performance.
  • Object created only when required.

Best Singleton Implementation (Enum)

Joshua Bloch recommends using Enum Singleton. 


public enum Singleton {

    INSTANCE;

    public void show() {
        System.out.println("Singleton");
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Serialization safe.
  • Reflection safe.
  • Simplest implementation.

Real World Examples

Logger


Logger logger = Logger.getInstance();

All application components use the same logger instance.

Configuration Manager


ConfigManager.getInstance();

Single source of configuration data.

Cache Manager


CacheManager.getInstance();

Shared cache across the application.

Singleton in Spring Framework

By default, Spring Beans are Singleton scoped. 


@Service
public class UserService {
}

Spring creates only one instance of UserService and shares it across the application.

Advantages

  • Controlled object creation.
  • Memory efficient.
  • Global access point.
  • Useful for shared resources.
  • Easy lifecycle management.

Disadvantages

  • Acts like a global variable.
  • Difficult to unit test.
  • Can introduce hidden dependencies.
  • May violate Single Responsibility Principle.
  • Thread safety must be handled carefully.

Common Interview Questions

Why is Singleton a Creational Pattern?

Because it controls how objects are created.

How do you make Singleton thread safe?

Using synchronization, double-checked locking, or Enum.

What is the best Singleton implementation in Java?

Enum Singleton is generally considered the safest implementation.

Are Spring Beans Singleton?

Yes. Singleton is the default bean scope in Spring.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Singleton ensures only one object exists.
  • Private constructor prevents external object creation.
  • Global access is provided through getInstance().
  • Lazy initialization creates object on demand.
  • Double Checked Locking is a common production implementation.
  • Enum Singleton is considered the safest Java implementation.
  • Spring Beans are Singleton by default.

subtopics.name: Factory Method Pattern
subtopics.content:
Factory Method Pattern

The Factory Method Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that provides an interface for creating objects while allowing subclasses to decide which specific object to instantiate.

Why Do We Need Factory Method?

Direct object creation using the new keyword tightly couples the client code with concrete classes.

Whenever a new implementation is introduced, client code must be modified. 


Payment payment = new CreditCardPayment();

If tomorrow PayPalPayment is introduced, existing code needs modification.

Factory Method solves this by moving object creation logic into a dedicated factory.

Problem Without Factory Method


if(type.equals("CREDIT_CARD")) {
    payment = new CreditCardPayment();
}
else if(type.equals("UPI")) {
    payment = new UpiPayment();
}
else if(type.equals("PAYPAL")) {
    payment = new PaypalPayment();
}

Every new payment method requires code changes.

Factory Method Solution

Instead of creating objects directly, the client asks a factory to create them. 


Client
   |
   v
Factory
   |
   +--> CreditCardPayment
   |
   +--> UpiPayment
   |
   +--> PaypalPayment

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Interface


public interface Payment {

    void pay();
}

Step 2: Concrete Products


public class CreditCardPayment
        implements Payment {

    @Override
    public void pay() {
        System.out.println(
            "Credit Card Payment");
    }
}


public class UpiPayment
        implements Payment {

    @Override
    public void pay() {
        System.out.println(
            "UPI Payment");
    }
}

Step 3: Create Factory


public class PaymentFactory {

    public static Payment getPayment(
            String type) {

        if(type.equals("CARD")) {
            return new CreditCardPayment();
        }

        if(type.equals("UPI")) {
            return new UpiPayment();
        }

        throw new IllegalArgumentException();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Payment payment =
    PaymentFactory.getPayment("UPI");

payment.pay();

How It Works

  1. Client requests an object.
  2. Factory decides which implementation to create.
  3. Factory returns the object.
  4. Client uses the interface.

Client remains unaware of concrete implementations.

Real World Example

Consider a logistics company. 


Road Transport
Air Transport
Sea Transport

The client asks for a transport service.

The factory determines which transport implementation to create.

Factory Method vs Direct Object Creation

Direct Creation Factory Method
Tightly Coupled Loosely Coupled
Hard to Extend Easy to Extend
Client Knows Class Client Knows Interface

Factory Method in Spring

Spring uses Factory Method extensively. 


@Bean
public PaymentService paymentService() {
    return new PaymentServiceImpl();
}

The framework calls the factory method to create the bean.

Advantages

  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Centralized object creation.
  • Easier maintenance.
  • Improves scalability.

Disadvantages

  • Additional classes required.
  • Increased complexity for simple applications.
  • More abstraction layers.

When to Use

  • Multiple implementations exist.
  • Object creation logic is complex.
  • Client should not know concrete classes.
  • Future extensions are expected.

When Not to Use

  • Only one implementation exists.
  • Object creation is extremely simple.
  • Abstraction adds unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Factory Method solve?

It decouples object creation from client code.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle.

How is it different from Abstract Factory?

Factory Method creates one product type, whereas Abstract Factory creates families of related products.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Factory Method is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Object creation is delegated to a factory.
  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Client depends on abstraction, not implementation.
  • Commonly used in Spring Bean creation.

subtopics.name: Abstract Factory Pattern
subtopics.content:
Abstract Factory Pattern

The Abstract Factory Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.

Why Do We Need Abstract Factory?

In Factory Method Pattern, we create one object at a time.

However, many applications require creating multiple related objects that belong to the same family.

For example:

  • Windows Button + Windows Checkbox
  • Mac Button + Mac Checkbox
  • Linux Button + Linux Checkbox

We should ensure that related objects belong to the same family.

Problem Without Abstract Factory


Button button =
    new WindowsButton();

Checkbox checkbox =
    new MacCheckbox();

Now the UI contains components from different operating systems.

This creates inconsistency.

Abstract Factory Solution

The Abstract Factory creates an entire family of related objects. 


Client
   |
   v
GUI Factory
   |
   +--> Button
   |
   +--> Checkbox

Depending on the selected factory: 


Windows Factory
    |
    +--> Windows Button
    |
    +--> Windows Checkbox

Mac Factory
    |
    +--> Mac Button
    |
    +--> Mac Checkbox

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Interfaces


public interface Button {
    void paint();
}

public interface Checkbox {
    void check();
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Products


public class WindowsButton
        implements Button {

    public void paint() {
        System.out.println(
          "Windows Button");
    }
}


public class WindowsCheckbox
        implements Checkbox {

    public void check() {
        System.out.println(
          "Windows Checkbox");
    }
}


public class MacButton
        implements Button {

    public void paint() {
        System.out.println(
          "Mac Button");
    }
}


public class MacCheckbox
        implements Checkbox {

    public void check() {
        System.out.println(
          "Mac Checkbox");
    }
}

Step 3: Create Abstract Factory


public interface GUIFactory {

    Button createButton();

    Checkbox createCheckbox();
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Factories


public class WindowsFactory
        implements GUIFactory {

    public Button createButton() {
        return new WindowsButton();
    }

    public Checkbox createCheckbox() {
        return new WindowsCheckbox();
    }
}


public class MacFactory
        implements GUIFactory {

    public Button createButton() {
        return new MacButton();
    }

    public Checkbox createCheckbox() {
        return new MacCheckbox();
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


GUIFactory factory =
    new WindowsFactory();

Button button =
    factory.createButton();

Checkbox checkbox =
    factory.createCheckbox();

button.paint();
checkbox.check();

How It Works

  1. Client selects a factory.
  2. Factory creates related products.
  3. Client works with interfaces.
  4. Concrete implementations remain hidden.

Factory Method vs Abstract Factory

Factory Method Abstract Factory
Creates one product Creates product families
Single factory method Multiple factory methods
Simpler More complex
One object creation Group object creation

Real World Example

Imagine a car manufacturing company.

Different factories produce different vehicle families. 


Toyota Factory

Toyota Car
Toyota Engine
Toyota Dashboard


BMW Factory

BMW Car
BMW Engine
BMW Dashboard

The client simply chooses a factory.

Abstract Factory in Spring

Spring's BeanFactory and ApplicationContext are examples of Abstract Factory concepts. 


ApplicationContext context =
    new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext();

UserService service =
    context.getBean(UserService.class);

The framework decides which implementation to provide.

Advantages

  • Ensures compatibility between products.
  • Reduces coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Centralized object creation.
  • Easy to switch product families.

Disadvantages

  • Many classes and interfaces required.
  • Complex design.
  • Difficult to add new product types.

When to Use

  • Multiple product families exist.
  • Products must work together.
  • Need to switch entire families dynamically.
  • Object creation should be hidden.

When Not to Use

  • Only one product exists.
  • No related object families exist.
  • Simple applications.

Common Interview Questions

What is the difference between Factory Method and Abstract Factory?

Factory Method creates one object. Abstract Factory creates families of related objects.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle.

Where is it used in Spring?

BeanFactory and ApplicationContext follow Abstract Factory concepts.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Abstract Factory creates families of related objects.
  • Client depends on interfaces, not implementations.
  • Ensures product compatibility.
  • More powerful than Factory Method.
  • Commonly used in UI frameworks.
  • Spring BeanFactory is a practical example.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Builder Pattern
subtopics.content:
Builder Pattern

The Builder Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern used to construct complex objects step by step.

It allows the creation of an object with multiple optional parameters while keeping the code readable and maintainable.

Why Do We Need Builder Pattern?

As objects become more complex, constructors often require many parameters.

This leads to the Telescoping Constructor Problem. 


public User(
    String name,
    String email,
    String phone,
    String address,
    String city,
    String state,
    String country
)

Such constructors become difficult to read and maintain.

Builder Pattern solves this problem by constructing objects step by step.

Problem Without Builder Pattern


User user = new User(
    "John",
    "john@gmail.com",
    "9999999999",
    "Street 1",
    "Delhi",
    "Delhi",
    "India"
);

It is difficult to understand which value belongs to which field.

Builder Pattern Solution

Instead of passing all parameters in a constructor, the Builder Pattern allows setting fields one by one. 


User user = User.builder()
                .name("John")
                .email("john@gmail.com")
                .phone("9999999999")
                .city("Delhi")
                .build();

This makes object creation more readable and flexible.

Structure of Builder Pattern


Client
  |
  v
Builder
  |
  v
Product

The Builder creates and configures the Product object.

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Class


public class User {

    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phone;

    private User(UserBuilder builder) {
        this.name = builder.name;
        this.email = builder.email;
        this.phone = builder.phone;
    }

    public static UserBuilder builder() {
        return new UserBuilder();
    }

    public static class UserBuilder {

        private String name;
        private String email;
        private String phone;

        public UserBuilder name(String name) {
            this.name = name;
            return this;
        }

        public UserBuilder email(String email) {
            this.email = email;
            return this;
        }

        public UserBuilder phone(String phone) {
            this.phone = phone;
            return this;
        }

        public User build() {
            return new User(this);
        }
    }
}

Step 2: Client Code


User user = User.builder()
        .name("John")
        .email("john@gmail.com")
        .phone("9999999999")
        .build();

How It Works

  1. Client requests a Builder.
  2. Builder sets required and optional fields.
  3. Builder validates data if required.
  4. Builder creates the final object.
  5. Fully configured object is returned.

Real World Example

Consider ordering a custom burger.

Different customers may choose different combinations. 


Burger Builder

+ Cheese
+ Extra Patty
+ Lettuce
+ Onion
+ Sauce

Instead of creating dozens of constructors, a Builder allows flexible configuration.

Builder Pattern with Lombok

Lombok provides built-in support for Builder Pattern. 


@Builder
public class User {

    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phone;
}

Usage


User user = User.builder()
        .name("John")
        .email("john@gmail.com")
        .build();

Lombok automatically generates the builder code.

Builder vs Constructor

Constructor Builder
Hard to read Highly readable
Limited flexibility Highly flexible
Difficult with many fields Ideal for many fields
Error-prone Less error-prone

Builder Pattern in Spring

ResponseEntity Builder


return ResponseEntity
        .ok()
        .body(user);

UriComponentsBuilder


UriComponentsBuilder
        .fromUriString(url)
        .queryParam("id", 1)
        .build();

Spring Framework heavily uses Builder Pattern.

Advantages

  • Readable object creation.
  • Handles optional fields elegantly.
  • Avoids telescoping constructors.
  • Supports immutable objects.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Encapsulates construction logic.

Disadvantages

  • Additional code required.
  • More classes and complexity.
  • Not useful for very simple objects.

When to Use

  • Objects contain many fields.
  • Several optional parameters exist.
  • Immutable objects are required.
  • Object creation logic is complex.

When Not to Use

  • Object contains only a few fields.
  • Simple constructor is sufficient.
  • No optional parameters exist.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Builder Pattern solve?

It solves the Telescoping Constructor Problem and improves readability.

Why is Builder commonly used with immutable classes?

Because all fields can be initialized during object creation and remain unchanged afterward.

Where is Builder Pattern used in Spring?

ResponseEntity, UriComponentsBuilder, WebClient, and many Spring APIs use Builder Pattern.

What Lombok annotation implements Builder Pattern?

@Builder

Interview Quick Notes

  • Builder is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Used to create complex objects step by step.
  • Solves the Telescoping Constructor Problem.
  • Improves readability and maintainability.
  • Supports immutable object creation.
  • Lombok provides @Builder annotation.
  • Widely used in Spring Framework APIs.

subtopics.name: Prototype Pattern
subtopics.content:
Prototype Pattern

The Prototype Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that creates new objects by cloning existing objects instead of creating them from scratch.

It is particularly useful when object creation is expensive, time-consuming, or requires complex initialization.

Why Do We Need Prototype Pattern?

Some objects require significant resources to create.

Examples include:

  • Database-loaded objects.
  • Large configuration objects.
  • Game characters.
  • Document templates.
  • Machine learning models.

Creating such objects repeatedly can impact performance.

Instead of creating a new object from scratch, we can clone an existing object.

Problem Without Prototype Pattern


Employee employee = new Employee();

employee.loadDataFromDatabase();

Every time a new Employee object is needed, the application must fetch data and initialize the object again.

This increases execution time and resource usage.

Prototype Pattern Solution

Create one fully initialized object and use it as a prototype.

Whenever a new object is required, clone the prototype instead of recreating it. 


Prototype Object
        |
        v
      Clone
        |
        +--> Object 1
        |
        +--> Object 2
        |
        +--> Object 3

Structure of Prototype Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Prototype Interface
   |
   v
Concrete Prototype
   |
 clone()

Implementation

Step 1: Implement Cloneable


public class Employee
        implements Cloneable {

    private String name;

    public Employee(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    @Override
    protected Employee clone()
            throws CloneNotSupportedException {

        return (Employee) super.clone();
    }
}

Step 2: Clone Object


Employee emp1 =
    new Employee("John");

Employee emp2 =
    emp1.clone();

System.out.println(emp1);
System.out.println(emp2);

Output: 


Different Memory Addresses

Same Data

How It Works

  1. Create a prototype object.
  2. Initialize the prototype.
  3. Clone the prototype whenever needed.
  4. Modify cloned object if required.

Real World Example

Document Templates

Consider a company that generates offer letters.

Instead of creating every document from scratch: 


Offer Letter Template
        |
        v
      Clone
        |
        +--> Employee A
        |
        +--> Employee B
        |
        +--> Employee C

Only employee-specific information changes.

Shallow Copy

Shallow Copy copies primitive fields but shares referenced objects. 


Employee
   |
   +--> Address Object

Both original and cloned objects reference the same Address object.

Example


@Override
protected Employee clone()
        throws CloneNotSupportedException {

    return (Employee) super.clone();
}

Deep Copy

Deep Copy creates copies of referenced objects as well. 


Employee
   |
   +--> Address Copy

Changes in one object do not affect the other.

Example


@Override
protected Employee clone()
        throws CloneNotSupportedException {

    Employee clone =
        (Employee) super.clone();

    clone.address =
        new Address(
            address.getCity()
        );

    return clone;
}

Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy

Feature Shallow Copy Deep Copy
Primitive Fields Copied Copied
Reference Objects Shared Copied
Performance Fast Slower
Safety Lower Higher

Prototype Registry

Large applications often maintain a collection of prototypes. 


Prototype Registry

CAR
BIKE
TRUCK
BUS

Clients request a prototype and receive a cloned object. 


Vehicle vehicle =
    registry.get("CAR").clone();

Cloneable Interface in Java

Java provides the Cloneable interface to support object cloning. 


public class Employee
        implements Cloneable {
}

The clone() method is inherited from Object class.

Prototype vs Builder

Builder Prototype
Creates step-by-step Creates by cloning
Complex construction Fast duplication
New object creation Copy existing object

Prototype Scope in Spring

Spring provides Prototype Bean Scope. 


@Component
@Scope("prototype")
public class Employee {
}

Every request creates a new bean instance. 


Employee e1 =
    context.getBean(Employee.class);

Employee e2 =
    context.getBean(Employee.class);

e1 and e2 are different objects.

Advantages

  • Faster object creation.
  • Reduces initialization cost.
  • Useful for expensive objects.
  • Improves performance.
  • Supports runtime object creation.

Disadvantages

  • Deep copy implementation can be difficult.
  • Cloning complex object graphs is challenging.
  • Cloneable API has design limitations.
  • Maintaining cloned state can become complex.

When to Use

  • Object creation is expensive.
  • Objects are frequently duplicated.
  • Initialization involves database or network calls.
  • Performance optimization is required.

When Not to Use

  • Objects are simple.
  • Object creation cost is negligible.
  • Deep copying introduces unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Prototype Pattern solve?

It avoids expensive object creation by cloning existing objects.

What is the difference between Shallow Copy and Deep Copy?

Shallow Copy shares references while Deep Copy creates independent copies.

Which Java interface supports Prototype Pattern?

Cloneable.

How is Prototype used in Spring?

Through Prototype Bean Scope using @Scope("prototype").

Interview Quick Notes

  • Prototype is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Creates objects by cloning existing objects.
  • Useful when object creation is expensive.
  • Supports Shallow Copy and Deep Copy.
  • Cloneable interface enables cloning in Java.
  • Spring supports Prototype Scope.
  • Improves performance for frequently duplicated objects.

subtopics.name: Adapter Pattern
subtopics.content:
Adapter Pattern

The Adapter Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that allows two incompatible interfaces to work together.

It acts as a bridge between an existing class and a client that expects a different interface.

Why Do We Need Adapter Pattern?

In real-world applications, we often need to integrate third-party libraries, legacy systems, or external APIs whose interfaces do not match our application's requirements.

Instead of modifying existing code, we create an Adapter that translates one interface into another.

Real World Example

Mobile Charger Adapter

A mobile charger cannot be plugged directly into a high-voltage power source. 


Power Socket
      |
      v
   Adapter
      |
      v
Mobile Charger

The adapter converts the interface and voltage into a compatible format.

Similarly, the Adapter Pattern converts one software interface into another.

Problem Without Adapter Pattern

Suppose our application expects JSON notifications. 


NotificationService
       |
       v
JSON Notification

A third-party library provides XML notifications. 


Third Party Service
       |
       v
XML Notification

The interfaces are incompatible.

Adapter Pattern Solution

Create an Adapter that converts XML into JSON. 


Client
   |
   v
Adapter
   |
   v
Third Party Service

The client works with its expected interface while the adapter handles conversion.

Structure of Adapter Pattern


Client
   |
Target Interface
   |
Adapter
   |
Adaptee

Where:

  • Client uses Target Interface.
  • Adapter implements Target Interface.
  • Adaptee contains existing incompatible code.

Implementation

Step 1: Target Interface


public interface PaymentProcessor {

    void processPayment();
}

Step 2: Existing Incompatible Class


public class LegacyPaymentSystem {

    public void makePayment() {

        System.out.println(
            "Payment Processed"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Adapter


public class PaymentAdapter
        implements PaymentProcessor {

    private LegacyPaymentSystem
            legacyPaymentSystem;

    public PaymentAdapter(
        LegacyPaymentSystem
            legacyPaymentSystem) {

        this.legacyPaymentSystem =
                legacyPaymentSystem;
    }

    @Override
    public void processPayment() {

        legacyPaymentSystem
            .makePayment();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


PaymentProcessor processor =
    new PaymentAdapter(
        new LegacyPaymentSystem()
    );

processor.processPayment();

How It Works

  1. Client calls the Target Interface.
  2. Adapter receives the request.
  3. Adapter converts the request.
  4. Adapter invokes the Adaptee.
  5. Response is returned to the Client.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
          PaymentProcessor
                    ^
                    |
             PaymentAdapter
                    |
                    v
        LegacyPaymentSystem

Object Adapter

Object Adapter uses composition. 


Adapter HAS-A Adaptee

This is the most commonly used implementation.

Advantages

  • Flexible.
  • Supports multiple adaptees.
  • Follows composition over inheritance.

Class Adapter

Class Adapter uses inheritance. 


Adapter EXTENDS Adaptee

Java does not support multiple class inheritance, so this approach is less common.

Adapter vs Facade

Adapter Facade
Makes incompatible interfaces compatible Simplifies complex interfaces
Focuses on compatibility Focuses on simplification
Wraps one object May wrap multiple subsystems

Adapter vs Decorator

Adapter Decorator
Changes interface Adds behavior
Solves compatibility issues Enhances functionality

Adapter Pattern in Spring Framework

HandlerAdapter

Spring MVC uses HandlerAdapter to allow different controller implementations to work through a common interface. 


DispatcherServlet
        |
        v
   HandlerAdapter
        |
        v
Controller

The DispatcherServlet does not need to know the exact controller type.

JdbcTemplate

JdbcTemplate internally adapts low-level JDBC APIs into simpler operations.

Real Life Use Cases

  • Third-party API integration.
  • Legacy system integration.
  • Payment gateway integration.
  • Database driver adaptation.
  • XML to JSON conversion.
  • Spring MVC HandlerAdapter.

Advantages

  • Promotes code reusability.
  • Integrates legacy systems easily.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Avoids modifying existing code.
  • Improves flexibility.

Disadvantages

  • Additional abstraction layer.
  • Increases complexity.
  • Too many adapters can make code difficult to maintain.

When to Use

  • Integrating third-party libraries.
  • Working with legacy code.
  • Interfaces are incompatible.
  • Modification of existing code is not possible.

When Not to Use

  • Interfaces are already compatible.
  • The adaptee can be modified directly.
  • Additional abstraction is unnecessary.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Adapter Pattern solve?

It allows incompatible interfaces to work together.

What is the difference between Adapter and Facade?

Adapter focuses on compatibility while Facade focuses on simplification.

What is the difference between Adapter and Decorator?

Adapter changes an interface, whereas Decorator adds functionality.

Where is Adapter Pattern used in Spring?

HandlerAdapter in Spring MVC is a classic example.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Adapter is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • It converts one interface into another.
  • Used to integrate incompatible systems.
  • Object Adapter uses composition.
  • Class Adapter uses inheritance.
  • Spring MVC HandlerAdapter is a common example.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Frequently used in third-party integrations.

subtopics.name: Bridge Pattern
subtopics.content:
Bridge Pattern

The Bridge Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that separates an abstraction from its implementation so that both can evolve independently.

Instead of tightly coupling abstraction and implementation through inheritance, the Bridge Pattern uses composition.

Why Do We Need Bridge Pattern?

As applications grow, inheritance hierarchies can become very large and difficult to maintain.

Consider a Remote Control application.

We have:

  • Basic Remote
  • Advanced Remote

And devices:

  • TV
  • Radio
  • Speaker

Without Bridge Pattern: 


BasicTVRemote
AdvancedTVRemote

BasicRadioRemote
AdvancedRadioRemote

BasicSpeakerRemote
AdvancedSpeakerRemote

The number of classes keeps growing as new remotes and devices are added.

Problem Without Bridge Pattern


              Remote
                 |
        -----------------
        |               |
      TV             Radio
        |               |
   AdvancedTV     AdvancedRadio

This creates a class explosion problem.

Every new device requires new remote implementations.

Bridge Pattern Solution

Separate abstraction (Remote) from implementation (Device). 


Remote
   |
   +--------> Device

Now both hierarchies can evolve independently.

Real World Example

Remote and Devices


Basic Remote
Advanced Remote
       |
       v
      Device
       |
  +----+----+
  |         |
 TV      Radio

The remote works with any device through a common interface.

Structure of Bridge Pattern


Abstraction
    |
Refined Abstraction
    |
    v
Implementation Interface
    |
Concrete Implementations

Implementation

Step 1: Create Implementation Interface


public interface Device {

    void turnOn();

    void turnOff();

    void setVolume(int volume);
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Implementations


public class TV
        implements Device {

    @Override
    public void turnOn() {
        System.out.println("TV ON");
    }

    @Override
    public void turnOff() {
        System.out.println("TV OFF");
    }

    @Override
    public void setVolume(int volume) {
        System.out.println(
            "TV Volume: " + volume
        );
    }
}


public class Radio
        implements Device {

    @Override
    public void turnOn() {
        System.out.println("Radio ON");
    }

    @Override
    public void turnOff() {
        System.out.println("Radio OFF");
    }

    @Override
    public void setVolume(int volume) {
        System.out.println(
            "Radio Volume: " + volume
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Abstraction


public abstract class Remote {

    protected Device device;

    public Remote(Device device) {
        this.device = device;
    }

    public abstract void power();
}

Step 4: Create Refined Abstraction


public class BasicRemote
        extends Remote {

    public BasicRemote(Device device) {
        super(device);
    }

    @Override
    public void power() {

        device.turnOn();
    }
}


public class AdvancedRemote
        extends Remote {

    public AdvancedRemote(
            Device device) {

        super(device);
    }

    @Override
    public void power() {

        device.turnOn();
    }

    public void mute() {

        device.setVolume(0);
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


Device tv = new TV();

Remote remote =
    new AdvancedRemote(tv);

remote.power();

How It Works

  1. Remote contains a Device reference.
  2. Remote delegates operations to Device.
  3. Different remotes can work with different devices.
  4. Both hierarchies evolve independently.

Class Diagram


             Remote
                |
      -------------------
      |                 |
BasicRemote    AdvancedRemote
      |
      v
    Device
      |
   --------
   |      |
  TV    Radio

Bridge vs Adapter

Bridge Adapter
Separates abstraction and implementation Makes incompatible interfaces compatible
Designed upfront Usually applied later
Focuses on flexibility Focuses on compatibility

Bridge vs Strategy

Bridge Strategy
Separates abstraction and implementation Encapsulates algorithms
Structural Pattern Behavioral Pattern
Multiple dimensions of variation Multiple algorithms

Real Life Use Cases

  • Remote Controls and Devices.
  • Database Drivers.
  • Cross-platform UI Frameworks.
  • Messaging Systems.
  • Payment Gateways.
  • Cloud Service Integrations.

Bridge Pattern in Spring

Spring Data demonstrates Bridge-like behavior. 


Repository
      |
      +--> MySQL
      |
      +--> PostgreSQL
      |
      +--> MongoDB

Repositories remain unchanged while implementations vary.

Advantages

  • Avoids class explosion.
  • Supports composition over inheritance.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Abstraction and implementation evolve independently.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.

Disadvantages

  • Additional abstraction layers.
  • Increased design complexity.
  • May be overkill for simple systems.

When to Use

  • Multiple independent dimensions exist.
  • Inheritance hierarchy is becoming large.
  • Need runtime implementation switching.
  • Want composition over inheritance.

When Not to Use

  • Simple applications.
  • Only one implementation exists.
  • No risk of class explosion.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Bridge Pattern solve?

It prevents class explosion by separating abstraction from implementation.

What principle does Bridge Pattern promote?

Composition over inheritance.

How is Bridge different from Adapter?

Bridge is designed to separate abstraction and implementation, whereas Adapter is used to make incompatible interfaces work together.

When should Bridge Pattern be used?

When multiple independent hierarchies need to evolve separately.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Bridge is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • Separates abstraction from implementation.
  • Uses composition instead of inheritance.
  • Prevents class explosion.
  • Supports independent evolution of hierarchies.
  • Remote-Control and Device example is the classic interview explanation.
  • Promotes Open/Closed Principle and composition over inheritance.

subtopics.name: Composite Pattern
subtopics.content:
Composite Pattern

The Composite Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that allows individual objects and groups of objects to be treated uniformly.

It organizes objects into a tree structure where both individual objects (Leaf Nodes) and collections of objects (Composite Nodes) implement the same interface.

Why Do We Need Composite Pattern?

Many applications deal with hierarchical structures.

Examples include:

  • File Systems
  • Organization Hierarchies
  • Menu Structures
  • HTML DOM Trees
  • Company Departments

Without Composite Pattern, client code must handle individual objects and groups differently.

Real World Example

File System


Root Folder
|
+-- Documents
|     |
|     +-- Resume.pdf
|     |
|     +-- Notes.txt
|
+-- Pictures
      |
      +-- Photo1.jpg
      |
      +-- Photo2.jpg

A folder can contain files and other folders.

The client should be able to perform operations on both files and folders using the same interface.

Problem Without Composite Pattern


if(object instanceof File) {
   // Process File
}
else if(object instanceof Folder) {
   // Process Folder
}

As the hierarchy grows, client code becomes complicated.

Composite Pattern Solution

Create a common interface for both individual objects and groups. 


Component
   |
   +--> File
   |
   +--> Folder

Now the client can treat both uniformly.

Structure of Composite Pattern


           Component
               |
       ------------------
       |                |
      Leaf         Composite

Where:

  • Component = Common interface.
  • Leaf = Individual object.
  • Composite = Collection of objects.

Implementation

Step 1: Create Component Interface


public interface FileSystemComponent {

    void showDetails();
}

Step 2: Create Leaf Class


public class File
        implements FileSystemComponent {

    private String name;

    public File(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    @Override
    public void showDetails() {

        System.out.println(
            "File: " + name
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Composite Class


import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class Folder
        implements FileSystemComponent {

    private String name;

    private List<FileSystemComponent>
            components =
            new ArrayList<>();

    public Folder(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    public void add(
            FileSystemComponent component) {

        components.add(component);
    }

    @Override
    public void showDetails() {

        System.out.println(
            "Folder: " + name
        );

        for(FileSystemComponent component
                : components) {

            component.showDetails();
        }
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


File file1 =
    new File("Resume.pdf");

File file2 =
    new File("Notes.txt");

Folder documents =
    new Folder("Documents");

documents.add(file1);
documents.add(file2);

documents.showDetails();

How It Works

  1. Client interacts with Component interface.
  2. Leaf objects perform individual operations.
  3. Composite objects delegate operations to children.
  4. Client does not need to know whether an object is a Leaf or Composite.

Class Diagram


          Component
              |
      -----------------
      |               |
     File          Folder
                      |
                Contains
                      |
               Component(s)

Tree Structure Example


Company

├── HR Department
│     ├── Employee A
│     └── Employee B
│
├── IT Department
│     ├── Employee C
│     └── Employee D

Departments are composites.

Employees are leaf nodes.

Composite vs Decorator

Composite Decorator
Represents tree structures Adds functionality
Part-whole relationship Behavior enhancement
Groups objects Wraps objects

Composite vs Facade

Composite Facade
Represents hierarchy Simplifies subsystem access
Tree structure Single entry point
Recursive composition No recursive structure

Real Life Use Cases

  • File Systems.
  • Organization Structures.
  • Menu Systems.
  • HTML DOM Trees.
  • UI Component Trees.
  • Product Categories.
  • Folder Management Systems.

Composite Pattern in Spring

Bean Definition Hierarchy

Spring internally maintains hierarchical bean definitions and application contexts using composite-like structures.

Security Filter Chains


Security Filter Chain

Authentication Filter
Authorization Filter
JWT Filter
CORS Filter

The filter chain behaves similarly to a composite structure.

Advantages

  • Treats individual and group objects uniformly.
  • Simplifies client code.
  • Supports recursive structures.
  • Easy to add new components.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.

Disadvantages

  • Can make design overly generic.
  • Difficult to restrict component types.
  • May increase complexity in simple systems.

When to Use

  • Tree structures exist.
  • Part-whole relationships exist.
  • Client should treat groups and individuals uniformly.
  • Recursive structures are required.

When Not to Use

  • No hierarchical structure exists.
  • Objects do not form parent-child relationships.
  • Simple object structures are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Composite Pattern solve?

It allows clients to treat individual objects and groups of objects uniformly.

What are Leaf and Composite?

Leaf represents an individual object, while Composite represents a collection of objects.

What is the most common example of Composite Pattern?

File and Folder hierarchy.

Which principle does Composite Pattern support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Composite is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • Represents part-whole hierarchies.
  • Uses a common interface for individual and grouped objects.
  • Common example: File and Folder structure.
  • Supports recursive tree structures.
  • Simplifies client code.
  • Frequently asked in design pattern interviews.

subtopics.name: Decorator Pattern
subtopics.content:
Decorator Pattern

The Decorator Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that allows behavior to be added to an object dynamically at runtime without modifying its existing code.

It wraps an existing object inside another object that adds new functionality.

Why Do We Need Decorator Pattern?

As applications grow, we often need multiple combinations of features.

Using inheritance for every combination leads to class explosion.

Example

Suppose we are building a Coffee Ordering System.

Available Add-ons:

  • Milk
  • Sugar
  • Whipped Cream
  • Chocolate

Without Decorator Pattern: 


Coffee

MilkCoffee

SugarCoffee

MilkSugarCoffee

MilkSugarChocolateCoffee

MilkChocolateCoffee

SugarChocolateCoffee

...

The number of classes keeps increasing.

Problem Without Decorator Pattern

Inheritance creates too many subclasses. 


Coffee
   |
   +--> MilkCoffee
   |
   +--> SugarCoffee
   |
   +--> ChocolateCoffee
   |
   +--> MilkSugarCoffee

Maintaining such a hierarchy becomes difficult.

Decorator Pattern Solution

Instead of creating subclasses, we wrap objects with decorators. 


Coffee
   |
   +--> Milk Decorator
              |
              +--> Sugar Decorator
                          |
                          +--> Chocolate Decorator

Features can be added dynamically at runtime.

Real World Example

Coffee Shop


Base Coffee = ₹100

+ Milk = ₹20

+ Sugar = ₹10

+ Chocolate = ₹30

Final Cost: 


Coffee
 + Milk
 + Sugar

Total = ₹130

Each decorator adds additional behavior and cost.

Structure of Decorator Pattern


Component
    |
Concrete Component
    |
Decorator
    |
Concrete Decorators

Implementation

Step 1: Create Component Interface


public interface Coffee {

    String getDescription();

    double getCost();
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Component


public class SimpleCoffee
        implements Coffee {

    @Override
    public String getDescription() {

        return "Simple Coffee";
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {

        return 100;
    }
}

Step 3: Create Abstract Decorator


public abstract class CoffeeDecorator
        implements Coffee {

    protected Coffee coffee;

    public CoffeeDecorator(
            Coffee coffee) {

        this.coffee = coffee;
    }
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Decorators

Milk Decorator


public class MilkDecorator
        extends CoffeeDecorator {

    public MilkDecorator(
            Coffee coffee) {

        super(coffee);
    }

    @Override
    public String getDescription() {

        return coffee.getDescription()
                + ", Milk";
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {

        return coffee.getCost() + 20;
    }
}

Sugar Decorator


public class SugarDecorator
        extends CoffeeDecorator {

    public SugarDecorator(
            Coffee coffee) {

        super(coffee);
    }

    @Override
    public String getDescription() {

        return coffee.getDescription()
                + ", Sugar";
    }

    @Override
    public double getCost() {

        return coffee.getCost() + 10;
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


Coffee coffee =
        new SimpleCoffee();

coffee =
        new MilkDecorator(coffee);

coffee =
        new SugarDecorator(coffee);

System.out.println(
    coffee.getDescription()
);

System.out.println(
    coffee.getCost()
);

Output


Simple Coffee, Milk, Sugar

130

How It Works

  1. Create a base object.
  2. Wrap it inside a decorator.
  3. Add more decorators if required.
  4. Each decorator adds new behavior.

SimpleCoffee
      |
      v
MilkDecorator
      |
      v
SugarDecorator

Class Diagram


                Coffee
                   |
        -----------------------
        |                     |
SimpleCoffee       CoffeeDecorator
                          |
                -----------------
                |               |
        MilkDecorator   SugarDecorator

Decorator vs Inheritance

Inheritance Decorator
Compile-time behavior Runtime behavior
Class explosion possible No class explosion
Rigid Flexible
Static relationships Dynamic relationships

Decorator vs Adapter

Decorator Adapter
Adds behavior Changes interface
Enhances functionality Provides compatibility
Same interface Different interface

Decorator Pattern in Spring

Servlet Filters

Filters wrap incoming HTTP requests and responses. 


Request
   |
Authentication Filter
   |
Logging Filter
   |
CORS Filter
   |
Controller

Each filter adds behavior without changing the original request.

Spring Security Filter Chain


Request
   |
JWT Filter
   |
Authentication Filter
   |
Authorization Filter
   |
Controller

This is one of the most common real-world examples of Decorator Pattern.

Real Life Use Cases

  • Coffee Ordering Systems.
  • Java I/O Streams.
  • Spring Security Filters.
  • Servlet Filters.
  • Logging Wrappers.
  • Compression Wrappers.
  • Encryption Layers.

Decorator Pattern in Java IO


InputStream input =
    new FileInputStream("file.txt");

InputStream buffer =
    new BufferedInputStream(input);

BufferedInputStream decorates FileInputStream by adding buffering functionality.

Advantages

  • Adds functionality dynamically.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.
  • Avoids subclass explosion.
  • Flexible runtime behavior.
  • Supports composition over inheritance.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of small classes.
  • Debugging may become difficult.
  • Multiple decorators can increase complexity.

When to Use

  • Need runtime feature addition.
  • Inheritance hierarchy is growing.
  • Need flexible combinations of behavior.
  • Want composition over inheritance.

When Not to Use

  • Behavior is fixed.
  • Simple inheritance is sufficient.
  • No need for runtime flexibility.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Decorator Pattern solve?

It allows behavior to be added dynamically without modifying existing code.

What principle does Decorator Pattern promote?

Composition over inheritance.

What is the most common Java example?

Java IO Streams such as BufferedInputStream.

Where is Decorator Pattern used in Spring?

Servlet Filters and Spring Security Filter Chains.

Interview Quick Notes
