  • Decorator is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • Adds functionality dynamically at runtime.
  • Avoids subclass explosion.
  • Uses composition instead of inheritance.
  • Java IO Streams are classic examples.
  • Spring Security Filter Chain demonstrates Decorator Pattern.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Facade Pattern
subtopics.content:
Facade Pattern

The Facade Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem.

Instead of interacting with multiple classes directly, clients communicate with a single Facade object that coordinates all subsystem operations.

Why Do We Need Facade Pattern?

Large applications often contain multiple interconnected classes and subsystems.

Clients may need to understand and interact with several components to perform a simple task.

This increases complexity and coupling.

Facade Pattern hides this complexity by exposing a single entry point.

Real World Example

Home Theater System

To watch a movie, a user may need to interact with:

  • Projector
  • Sound System
  • DVD Player
  • Lights

Without a Facade: 


projector.turnOn();

soundSystem.turnOn();

dvdPlayer.turnOn();

lights.dim();

dvdPlayer.playMovie();

With a Facade: 


homeTheater.watchMovie();

The Facade handles all subsystem interactions.

Problem Without Facade Pattern


Client
  |
  +--> AuthenticationService
  |
  +--> UserService
  |
  +--> NotificationService
  |
  +--> AuditService

The client must know about all subsystems and their interactions.

Facade Pattern Solution

Introduce a Facade that encapsulates subsystem complexity. 


Client
   |
   v
Facade
   |
   +--> AuthenticationService
   |
   +--> UserService
   |
   +--> NotificationService
   |
   +--> AuditService

The client interacts only with the Facade.

Structure of Facade Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Facade
   |
Subsystem Classes

Implementation

Step 1: Create Subsystem Classes


public class AuthenticationService {

    public void authenticate() {

        System.out.println(
            "User Authenticated"
        );
    }
}


public class NotificationService {

    public void sendWelcomeEmail() {

        System.out.println(
            "Email Sent"
        );
    }
}


public class AuditService {

    public void logActivity() {

        System.out.println(
            "Audit Logged"
        );
    }
}

Step 2: Create Facade


public class UserFacade {

    private AuthenticationService
            authService =
            new AuthenticationService();

    private NotificationService
            notificationService =
            new NotificationService();

    private AuditService
            auditService =
            new AuditService();

    public void registerUser() {

        authService.authenticate();

        notificationService
                .sendWelcomeEmail();

        auditService.logActivity();
    }
}

Step 3: Client Code


UserFacade facade =
    new UserFacade();

facade.registerUser();

How It Works

  1. Client calls the Facade.
  2. Facade coordinates subsystem operations.
  3. Subsystems perform actual work.
  4. Results are returned to the client.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Facade
                    |
      ----------------------------
      |            |            |
 Authentication  Notification  Audit
    Service       Service     Service

Banking Example

A fund transfer operation may involve:

  • Account Validation
  • Balance Check
  • Money Transfer
  • Transaction Logging
  • Notification Sending

Without Facade: 


validateAccount();

checkBalance();

transferMoney();

logTransaction();

sendNotification();

With Facade: 


bankFacade.transferFunds();

Facade vs Adapter

Facade Adapter
Simplifies interface Converts interface
Hides complexity Provides compatibility
Works with subsystems Works with incompatible classes

Facade vs Decorator

Facade Decorator
Simplifies usage Adds functionality
Provides unified interface Wraps object behavior
Focuses on usability Focuses on enhancement

Facade vs Proxy

Facade Proxy
Simplifies access Controls access
Works with many objects Usually wraps one object
Hides subsystem complexity Adds access control

Facade Pattern in Spring

Service Layer

The Service Layer often acts as a Facade. 


Controller
    |
    v
OrderService
    |
    +--> PaymentService
    |
    +--> InventoryService
    |
    +--> NotificationService

Controllers interact only with OrderService.

RestTemplate

RestTemplate simplifies complex HTTP communication and acts as a Facade over lower-level networking APIs.

Real Life Use Cases

  • Service Layer in Spring Boot.
  • Banking Applications.
  • E-commerce Order Processing.
  • Home Theater Systems.
  • Payment Processing Systems.
  • Travel Booking Platforms.
  • Microservice Orchestration.

Advantages

  • Reduces system complexity.
  • Decreases coupling.
  • Provides a clean API.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Hides subsystem details.

Disadvantages

  • Facade can become a God Object.
  • May hide useful subsystem functionality.
  • Additional abstraction layer.

When to Use

  • Complex subsystems exist.
  • Need a simplified API.
  • Want to reduce coupling.
  • Need a single entry point.

When Not to Use

  • System is already simple.
  • Clients need direct access to all subsystem features.
  • Additional abstraction is unnecessary.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Facade Pattern solve?

It simplifies interaction with complex subsystems by providing a unified interface.

What is the difference between Facade and Adapter?

Facade simplifies interfaces, whereas Adapter converts incompatible interfaces.

What is the difference between Facade and Proxy?

Facade simplifies access to subsystems, while Proxy controls access to an object.

Where is Facade Pattern used in Spring?

Service Layer classes commonly act as Facades.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Facade is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • Provides a simplified interface to complex subsystems.
  • Reduces coupling between clients and subsystems.
  • Acts as a single entry point.
  • Commonly implemented through Service Layers.
  • Different from Adapter and Proxy.
  • Improves maintainability and readability.

subtopics.name: Flyweight Pattern
subtopics.content:
Flyweight Pattern

The Flyweight Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that minimizes memory usage by sharing common object data among multiple objects.

Instead of creating duplicate objects, Flyweight reuses existing objects whenever possible.

Why Do We Need Flyweight Pattern?

Applications sometimes create millions of similar objects.

Creating separate objects for each instance can consume huge amounts of memory.

Flyweight Pattern reduces memory consumption by sharing common state.

Real World Example

Text Editor

Consider a document containing: 


100,000 Characters

Each character may contain:

  • Font Family
  • Font Size
  • Font Style
  • Color

Storing these attributes for every character wastes memory.

Instead: 


Character
    |
    +--> Shared Font Object

Thousands of characters can share the same font object.

Problem Without Flyweight Pattern


Character A

Font = Arial
Size = 12
Color = Black

Character B

Font = Arial
Size = 12
Color = Black

Character C

Font = Arial
Size = 12
Color = Black

Identical information is stored repeatedly.

Flyweight Pattern Solution

Separate object state into:

  • Intrinsic State (shared)
  • Extrinsic State (unique)

Intrinsic State

Shared data stored once. 


Font Name
Font Size
Font Color

Extrinsic State

Unique data stored externally. 


Character Position

Line Number

Cursor Location

Structure of Flyweight Pattern


Client
   |
Flyweight Factory
   |
Flyweight Objects

Implementation

Step 1: Create Flyweight Interface


public interface Shape {

    void draw(
        int x,
        int y
    );
}

Step 2: Create Flyweight Class


public class Circle
        implements Shape {

    private String color;

    public Circle(
            String color) {

        this.color = color;
    }

    @Override
    public void draw(
            int x,
            int y) {

        System.out.println(
            "Circle: " +
            color +
            " Position: " +
            x + "," + y
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Flyweight Factory


import java.util.HashMap;
import java.util.Map;

public class ShapeFactory {

    private static final
    Map<String, Shape>
    circles =
    new HashMap<>();

    public static Shape
        getCircle(String color) {

        if(!circles.containsKey(color)) {

            circles.put(
                color,
                new Circle(color)
            );
        }

        return circles.get(color);
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Shape red1 =
    ShapeFactory.getCircle("RED");

Shape red2 =
    ShapeFactory.getCircle("RED");

System.out.println(
    red1 == red2
);

Output: 


true

Both references point to the same object.

How It Works

  1. Client requests an object.
  2. Factory checks cache.
  3. If object exists, return existing object.
  4. Otherwise create and cache new object.
  5. Shared object is reused.

Class Diagram


             Client
                |
                v
       Flyweight Factory
                |
        ----------------
        |              |
     Circle        Rectangle

Real Life Use Cases

  • Text Editors.
  • Game Development.
  • Map Applications.
  • Character Rendering.
  • Icon Libraries.
  • Caching Systems.

Flyweight in Java

String Pool


String s1 = "Java";

String s2 = "Java";

System.out.println(
    s1 == s2
);

Output: 


true

Java reuses the same String object from the String Pool.

Flyweight in Spring

Singleton Beans behave similarly to Flyweight objects because the same instance is reused across the application.

Advantages

  • Reduces memory usage.
  • Improves performance.
  • Supports object reuse.
  • Useful for large-scale systems.
  • Reduces object creation cost.

Disadvantages

  • Increased complexity.
  • Requires separation of state.
  • Can complicate debugging.

When to Use

  • Large numbers of similar objects exist.
  • Memory optimization is important.
  • Object creation is expensive.
  • Shared state can be extracted.

When Not to Use

  • Small number of objects.
  • Memory is not a concern.
  • Objects contain mostly unique state.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Flyweight solve?

It reduces memory usage by sharing common object state.

What are Intrinsic and Extrinsic States?

Intrinsic state is shared. Extrinsic state is unique and provided externally.

What is the most common Java example?

String Pool.

Which design principle is used?

Object sharing and caching.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Flyweight is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • Optimizes memory by sharing objects.
  • Uses Intrinsic and Extrinsic State.
  • String Pool is the most common Java example.
  • Useful when millions of similar objects exist.
  • Often implemented using caches and factories.

subtopics.name: Proxy Pattern
subtopics.content:
Proxy Pattern

The Proxy Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that provides a placeholder or surrogate object to control access to another object.

Instead of interacting directly with the real object, clients communicate through a proxy object.

Why Do We Need Proxy Pattern?

Sometimes direct access to an object is not desirable.

Reasons may include:

  • Security
  • Lazy Loading
  • Caching
  • Logging
  • Remote Access
  • Performance Optimization

The Proxy controls how and when the real object is accessed.

Real World Example

Bank ATM

Customers do not directly access the bank's database.

Instead: 


Customer
    |
    v
ATM Machine
    |
    v
Bank Server

The ATM acts as a Proxy between the customer and the banking system.

Problem Without Proxy Pattern

Suppose loading an image requires downloading a large file from a server. 


Image image =
    new RealImage(
        "large-image.jpg"
    );

The image is loaded immediately, even if the user never views it.

This wastes resources.

Proxy Pattern Solution

Create a Proxy object that delays loading until needed. 


Client
   |
   v
Image Proxy
   |
   v
Real Image

The real object is created only when required.

Structure of Proxy Pattern


Client
   |
Subject Interface
   |
-------------------
|                 |
Proxy        Real Subject

Where:

  • Subject = Common interface.
  • Real Subject = Actual object.
  • Proxy = Controls access.

Implementation

Step 1: Create Subject Interface


public interface Image {

    void display();
}

Step 2: Create Real Subject


public class RealImage
        implements Image {

    private String fileName;

    public RealImage(
            String fileName) {

        this.fileName = fileName;

        loadFromDisk();
    }

    private void loadFromDisk() {

        System.out.println(
            "Loading Image..."
        );
    }

    @Override
    public void display() {

        System.out.println(
            "Displaying Image"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Proxy


public class ProxyImage
        implements Image {

    private RealImage realImage;

    private String fileName;

    public ProxyImage(
            String fileName) {

        this.fileName = fileName;
    }

    @Override
    public void display() {

        if(realImage == null) {

            realImage =
                new RealImage(
                    fileName
                );
        }

        realImage.display();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Image image =
    new ProxyImage(
        "photo.jpg"
    );

System.out.println(
    "Image Not Loaded Yet"
);

image.display();

Output


Image Not Loaded Yet

Loading Image...

Displaying Image

The image loads only when required.

How It Works

  1. Client interacts with Proxy.
  2. Proxy decides whether to access Real Object.
  3. Proxy may perform validation, caching, logging, or lazy loading.
  4. Proxy delegates request to Real Object.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Subject
                    ^
                    |
           ----------------
           |              |
         Proxy      RealObject

Types of Proxy Pattern

1. Virtual Proxy

Creates expensive objects only when needed.

Example:

  • Lazy Loading Images
  • Large Documents
  • Video Streaming

2. Protection Proxy

Controls access based on permissions. 


Admin User
    |
    v
Protection Proxy
    |
    v
Sensitive Data

Only authorized users can access the real object.

3. Remote Proxy

Represents objects located on remote servers. 


Client
   |
Remote Proxy
   |
Remote Service

Used in distributed systems.

4. Caching Proxy

Stores previously fetched results. 


Request
   |
Cache Proxy
   |
Database

Reduces repeated database access.

5. Logging Proxy

Logs requests before forwarding them. 


Client
   |
Logging Proxy
   |
Service

Proxy vs Decorator

Proxy Decorator
Controls access Adds behavior
Focuses on object access Focuses on functionality
May prevent access Enhances access

Proxy vs Facade

Proxy Facade
Controls access Simplifies access
Usually wraps one object Works with many subsystems
Same interface New simplified interface

Proxy Pattern in Spring Framework

Spring AOP

Spring AOP creates proxy objects around beans. 


Client
   |
Spring Proxy
   |
Actual Service

The proxy adds:

  • Logging
  • Transactions
  • Security
  • Caching

@Transactional


@Transactional
public void saveUser() {
}

Spring creates a proxy around the bean and manages transactions automatically.

@Cacheable


@Cacheable("users")
public User getUser(Long id) {
}

Spring uses a caching proxy internally.

Real Life Use Cases

  • Spring AOP.
  • Transaction Management.
  • Caching Systems.
  • Security Layers.
  • Lazy Loading in Hibernate.
  • Remote Service Access.
  • API Gateways.

Proxy Pattern in Hibernate

Hibernate uses proxies for lazy loading. 


@OneToMany(
    fetch = FetchType.LAZY
)

The related data is loaded only when accessed.

Advantages

  • Controls object access.
  • Supports lazy loading.
  • Improves performance.
  • Adds security and caching.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.

Disadvantages

  • Additional layer of abstraction.
  • Increased complexity.
  • Extra objects created.
  • May affect debugging.

When to Use

  • Need lazy loading.
  • Need access control.
  • Need caching.
  • Need logging or auditing.
  • Need remote object access.

When Not to Use

  • Direct access is sufficient.
  • No performance or security concerns exist.
  • Additional abstraction is unnecessary.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Proxy Pattern solve?

It controls access to an object while keeping the same interface.

What is the difference between Proxy and Decorator?

Proxy controls access, while Decorator adds functionality.

Where is Proxy Pattern used in Spring?

Spring AOP, Transactions, Caching, and Security.

What is a Virtual Proxy?

A proxy that delays object creation until needed.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Proxy is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • Provides controlled access to an object.
  • Uses the same interface as the real object.
  • Supports lazy loading, caching, logging, and security.
  • Spring AOP is the most common Java example.
  • Hibernate Lazy Loading uses Proxy Pattern.
  • @Transactional works through Spring proxies.

subtopics.name: Chain of Responsibility Pattern
subtopics.content:
Chain of Responsibility Pattern

The Chain of Responsibility Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that allows a request to pass through a chain of handlers until one of them processes the request.

Instead of a sender directly calling a specific receiver, the request moves through multiple handlers, creating loose coupling between sender and receiver.

Why Do We Need Chain of Responsibility?

In many applications, a request may need to go through multiple processing steps before reaching its final destination.

Examples:

  • Authentication
  • Authorization
  • Validation
  • Logging
  • Rate Limiting
  • Error Handling

Without Chain of Responsibility, the client needs to explicitly call each handler.

Real World Example

Customer Support System

Suppose a customer raises a complaint. 


Customer Request
       |
       v
Level 1 Support
       |
       v
Level 2 Support
       |
       v
Manager

If Level 1 cannot resolve the issue, it forwards the request to Level 2.

The request moves through the chain until someone handles it.

Problem Without Chain of Responsibility


if(requestType.equals("BASIC")) {
    level1.handle();
}
else if(requestType.equals("ADVANCED")) {
    level2.handle();
}
else {
    manager.handle();
}

This tightly couples request handling logic with the client.

Adding new handlers requires modifying existing code.

Chain of Responsibility Solution

Each handler knows:

  • How to process a request.
  • Who the next handler is.

Request
   |
   v
Handler 1
   |
   v
Handler 2
   |
   v
Handler 3

The request automatically flows through the chain.

Structure of Chain of Responsibility


Client
   |
   v
Handler
   |
------------------------
|           |          |
Auth     Validation   Logging

Implementation

Step 1: Create Handler Abstract Class


public abstract class Handler {

    protected Handler nextHandler;

    public void setNextHandler(
            Handler nextHandler) {

        this.nextHandler =
                nextHandler;
    }

    public abstract void handle(
            String request);
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Handlers

Authentication Handler


public class AuthenticationHandler
        extends Handler {

    @Override
    public void handle(
            String request) {

        System.out.println(
            "Authentication Passed"
        );

        if(nextHandler != null) {
            nextHandler.handle(
                    request);
        }
    }
}

Validation Handler


public class ValidationHandler
        extends Handler {

    @Override
    public void handle(
            String request) {

        System.out.println(
            "Validation Passed"
        );

        if(nextHandler != null) {
            nextHandler.handle(
                    request);
        }
    }
}

Business Handler


public class BusinessHandler
        extends Handler {

    @Override
    public void handle(
            String request) {

        System.out.println(
            "Business Logic Executed"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Configure Chain


AuthenticationHandler auth =
        new AuthenticationHandler();

ValidationHandler validation =
        new ValidationHandler();

BusinessHandler business =
        new BusinessHandler();

auth.setNextHandler(
        validation);

validation.setNextHandler(
        business);

Step 4: Process Request


auth.handle("Create User");

Output


Authentication Passed

Validation Passed

Business Logic Executed

How It Works

  1. Client sends request to first handler.
  2. Handler processes request.
  3. Handler forwards request to next handler.
  4. Chain continues until request is handled.

Class Diagram


                Client
                   |
                   v
                Handler
                   |
      -------------------------
      |           |           |
 Authentication Validation Business

API Request Processing Example

A REST API request typically goes through multiple handlers. 


Incoming Request
        |
        v
Authentication Filter
        |
        v
Authorization Filter
        |
        v
Rate Limiter
        |
        v
Controller

Each component handles part of the request and forwards it.

Spring Security Example

Spring Security is one of the most common examples of Chain of Responsibility. 


Request
   |
SecurityContextFilter
   |
JWTAuthenticationFilter
   |
AuthorizationFilter
   |
ExceptionFilter
   |
Controller

Each filter processes the request and passes it to the next filter.

Servlet Filter Example


Request
   |
Logging Filter
   |
Authentication Filter
   |
Compression Filter
   |
Controller

Servlet Filters internally use Chain of Responsibility.

Chain of Responsibility vs Decorator

Chain of Responsibility Decorator
Passes request through handlers Adds behavior to object
Multiple handlers process request Wraps a single object
Focuses on request processing Focuses on functionality enhancement

Chain of Responsibility vs Command

Chain of Responsibility Command
Request moves through handlers Encapsulates an action
Multiple handlers may participate Single command executes action

Real Life Use Cases

  • Spring Security Filter Chain.
  • Servlet Filters.
  • API Gateway Request Processing.
  • Approval Workflows.
  • Customer Support Escalation.
  • Middleware Pipelines.
  • Exception Handling Chains.

Advantages

  • Reduces coupling between sender and receiver.
  • Easy to add new handlers.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Flexible request processing.
  • Handlers can be reordered dynamically.

Disadvantages

  • Request path can become difficult to debug.
  • Performance overhead for long chains.
  • No guarantee that a request will be handled.

When to Use

  • Multiple objects can process a request.
  • Request processing order matters.
  • Need dynamic processing pipelines.
  • Want loose coupling between sender and receiver.

When Not to Use

  • Only one handler exists.
  • Processing flow is fixed and simple.
  • Performance is extremely critical.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Chain of Responsibility solve?

It allows requests to pass through multiple handlers without coupling the sender to a specific receiver.

What is the most common Spring example?

Spring Security Filter Chain.

Can multiple handlers process the same request?

Yes. Each handler may process and forward the request.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Chain of Responsibility is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Requests pass through a chain of handlers.
  • Each handler decides whether to process or forward the request.
  • Spring Security Filter Chain is the most common Java example.
  • Reduces coupling between sender and receiver.
  • Supports dynamic request processing pipelines.
  • Frequently used in middleware and API processing.

subtopics.name: Command Pattern
subtopics.content:
Command Pattern

The Command Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that encapsulates a request as an object, allowing requests to be parameterized, queued, logged, and undone.

Instead of directly invoking a method, the request is wrapped inside a Command object and executed later.

Why Do We Need Command Pattern?

In traditional programming, a client directly invokes methods on objects. 


light.turnOn();

light.turnOff();

This tightly couples the client with the receiver object.

Command Pattern decouples the sender from the receiver by introducing a Command object.

Real World Example

TV Remote Control

When you press a button on a remote:

  • The button does not know how the TV works.
  • It simply executes a command.

Remote Button
      |
      v
Command
      |
      v
Television

The button acts as the Invoker and the TV acts as the Receiver.

Problem Without Command Pattern


public class Remote {

    private TV tv;

    public void pressButton() {

        tv.turnOn();
    }
}

The Remote is tightly coupled to the TV implementation.

Adding support for other devices requires modifying existing code.

Command Pattern Solution

Encapsulate every action inside a Command object. 


Client
   |
   v
Command
   |
   v
Receiver

The Invoker executes commands without knowing implementation details.

Structure of Command Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Invoker
   |
   v
Command
   |
   v
Receiver

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Command Defines execute method
Concrete Command Implements action
Receiver Performs actual work
Invoker Triggers command execution
Client Creates and wires objects

Implementation

Step 1: Create Command Interface


public interface Command {

    void execute();
}

Step 2: Create Receiver


public class TV {

    public void turnOn() {

        System.out.println(
            "TV Turned ON"
        );
    }

    public void turnOff() {

        System.out.println(
            "TV Turned OFF"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Concrete Commands

Turn On Command


public class TurnOnCommand
        implements Command {

    private TV tv;

    public TurnOnCommand(
            TV tv) {

        this.tv = tv;
    }

    @Override
    public void execute() {

        tv.turnOn();
    }
}

Turn Off Command


public class TurnOffCommand
        implements Command {

    private TV tv;

    public TurnOffCommand(
            TV tv) {

        this.tv = tv;
    }

    @Override
    public void execute() {

        tv.turnOff();
    }
}

Step 4: Create Invoker


public class RemoteControl {

    private Command command;

    public void setCommand(
            Command command) {

        this.command = command;
    }

    public void pressButton() {

        command.execute();
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


TV tv = new TV();

Command onCommand =
        new TurnOnCommand(tv);

RemoteControl remote =
        new RemoteControl();

remote.setCommand(
        onCommand);

remote.pressButton();

Output


TV Turned ON

How It Works

  1. Client creates Receiver.
  2. Client creates Command object.
  3. Command stores Receiver reference.
  4. Invoker executes Command.
  5. Command delegates work to Receiver.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Command
                    ^
                    |
          -------------------
          |                 |
    TurnOnCommand   TurnOffCommand
          |
          v
          TV
          ^
          |
     RemoteControl

Undo and Redo Support

One major advantage of Command Pattern is support for Undo and Redo operations. 


Execute Command

Store Command

Undo Command

Redo Command

This is widely used in editors and IDEs.

Real World Use Cases

Text Editors


Copy Command

Paste Command

Delete Command

Undo Command

ATM Systems


Withdraw Command

Deposit Command

Transfer Command

Task Scheduling Systems

Commands can be queued and executed later. 


Queue

Command 1

Command 2

Command 3

Command Pattern in Spring

Runnable


Runnable task =
    () -> System.out.println(
        "Execute Task"
    );

executor.execute(task);

Runnable acts as a Command object.

TaskExecutor


TaskExecutor
       |
       v
Runnable Command

Spring executes commands asynchronously.

Kafka Consumers

Messages received from Kafka often trigger command-like operations. 


Message
   |
   v
Process Order Command

Command vs Strategy

Command Strategy
Represents an action Represents an algorithm
Can be queued Cannot be queued directly
Supports Undo No Undo concept

Command vs Chain of Responsibility

Command Chain of Responsibility
Encapsulates action Passes request through handlers
Single command execution Multiple handlers may execute

Advantages

  • Decouples sender and receiver.
  • Supports Undo and Redo.
  • Supports request queuing.
  • Supports logging and auditing.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of command classes.
  • Additional abstraction layer.
  • Can increase complexity for simple actions.

When to Use

  • Need Undo/Redo functionality.
  • Need task scheduling.
  • Need request logging.
  • Need asynchronous execution.
  • Need loose coupling between sender and receiver.

When Not to Use

  • Actions are extremely simple.
  • No need for queuing or undo.
  • Additional abstraction adds unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Command Pattern solve?

It encapsulates requests as objects and decouples senders from receivers.

What is the most common real-world example?

Remote Control and Undo/Redo functionality.

Which Java interface resembles Command Pattern?

Runnable.

What is the biggest advantage of Command Pattern?

Support for queuing, logging, and undo operations.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Command is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Encapsulates requests as objects.
  • Decouples sender from receiver.
  • Supports Undo/Redo operations.
  • Supports task scheduling and queuing.
  • Runnable is a common Java example.
  • Frequently used in editors, schedulers, and messaging systems.

subtopics.name: Interpreter Pattern
subtopics.content:
Interpreter Pattern

The Interpreter Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern used to define the grammar of a language and provide an interpreter to evaluate sentences written in that language.

It is commonly used when applications need to process, parse, and evaluate expressions.

Why Do We Need Interpreter Pattern?

Many applications need to understand and execute custom languages or expressions.

Examples include:

  • SQL Queries
  • Regular Expressions
  • Mathematical Expressions
  • Business Rules Engines
  • Search Query Languages
  • Configuration Languages

Instead of writing complex conditional logic, Interpreter Pattern models grammar rules as classes.

Real World Example

Calculator

Suppose we want to evaluate: 


10 + 20

Instead of hardcoding logic: 


if(operator.equals("+")) {
   return a + b;
}

We can represent operators as interpreter classes.

Problem Without Interpreter Pattern


String expression =
    "10 + 20";

if(expression.contains("+")) {
    // Addition Logic
}

if(expression.contains("-")) {
    // Subtraction Logic
}

if(expression.contains("*")) {
    // Multiplication Logic
}

As grammar grows, code becomes difficult to maintain.

Interpreter Pattern Solution

Represent each grammar rule as a separate class. 


Expression
      |
      +--> NumberExpression
      |
      +--> AddExpression
      |
      +--> SubtractExpression

Complex expressions are built by combining simple expressions.

Structure of Interpreter Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Abstract Expression
   |
-------------------------
|                       |
Terminal          Non-Terminal
Expression         Expression

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Abstract Expression Defines interpret() method
Terminal Expression Represents basic values
Non-Terminal Expression Represents grammar rules
Context Stores information required for interpretation
Client Builds expression tree

Implementation

Step 1: Create Expression Interface


public interface Expression {

    int interpret();
}

Step 2: Create Terminal Expression


public class NumberExpression
        implements Expression {

    private int number;

    public NumberExpression(
            int number) {

        this.number = number;
    }

    @Override
    public int interpret() {

        return number;
    }
}

Step 3: Create Non-Terminal Expression

Addition Expression


public class AddExpression
        implements Expression {

    private Expression left;

    private Expression right;

    public AddExpression(
            Expression left,
            Expression right) {

        this.left = left;
        this.right = right;
    }

    @Override
    public int interpret() {

        return left.interpret()
                + right.interpret();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Expression ten =
    new NumberExpression(10);

Expression twenty =
    new NumberExpression(20);

Expression expression =
    new AddExpression(
        ten,
        twenty
    );

System.out.println(
    expression.interpret()
);

Output


30

How It Works

  1. Create terminal expressions.
  2. Create non-terminal expressions.
  3. Build an expression tree.
  4. Call interpret() on root node.
  5. Evaluation occurs recursively.

Expression Tree Example

Expression: 


10 + 20 - 5

Expression Tree: 


        (-)
       /   \
     (+)    5
    /   \
  10    20

Each node interprets itself recursively.

Class Diagram


                Expression
                     ^
                     |
        --------------------------
        |                        |
NumberExpression       AddExpression
                        |
                 Expression Tree

SQL Parser Example

Consider: 


SELECT * FROM USERS
WHERE AGE > 18

A database engine interprets SQL grammar and converts it into executable operations.

This is a real-world application of Interpreter Pattern.

Regular Expression Example

Regex engines interpret patterns such as: 


[a-z]+@[a-z]+\.com

Each grammar rule is represented internally using expression trees.

Interpreter Pattern in Spring

Interpreter Pattern is not heavily used directly in Spring applications.

However, it appears in:

  • Spring Expression Language (SpEL)
  • Spring Security Expressions
  • Query DSLs

Spring Expression Language (SpEL)


@Value("#{2 + 3}")
private int result;

Spring interprets the expression and evaluates it at runtime.

Spring Security Expressions


@PreAuthorize(
   "hasRole('ADMIN')"
)

The expression is parsed and interpreted dynamically.

Real Life Use Cases

  • SQL Parsers.
  • Regex Engines.
  • Expression Evaluators.
  • Rule Engines.
  • Compilers.
  • Configuration Parsers.
  • Spring Expression Language.

Advantages

  • Easy to extend grammar.
  • Supports complex expressions.
  • Follows Open/Closed Principle.
  • Grammar rules are reusable.
  • Easy to represent recursive structures.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of classes.
  • Complex grammar leads to complex design.
  • Performance may degrade for large expressions.
  • Difficult to maintain huge languages.

When to Use

  • Need to interpret expressions.
  • Grammar is relatively simple.
  • Language rules change frequently.
  • Expression trees are useful.

When Not to Use

  • Grammar is extremely complex.
  • Performance is critical.
  • Parser generators are more suitable.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Interpreter Pattern solve?

It provides a way to represent and evaluate grammar rules using objects.

What are Terminal and Non-Terminal Expressions?

Terminal expressions represent basic values, while Non-Terminal expressions represent grammar rules.

What is the most common real-world example?

SQL Parsers and Regular Expression Engines.

Where is Interpreter Pattern used in Spring?

Spring Expression Language (SpEL).

Interview Quick Notes

  • Interpreter is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Used for parsing and evaluating expressions.
  • Represents grammar rules as classes.
  • Uses Terminal and Non-Terminal Expressions.
  • SQL Parsers and Regex Engines are common examples.
  • SpEL is a Spring example.
  • Works well for small and medium-sized grammars.

subtopics.name: Iterator Pattern
subtopics.content:
Iterator Pattern

The Iterator Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its internal structure.

It allows clients to traverse collections such as Lists, Arrays, Sets, Trees, and Maps without knowing how the collection is implemented internally.

Why Do We Need Iterator Pattern?

Collections can have different internal implementations.

Examples:

  • ArrayList
  • LinkedList
  • HashSet
  • TreeSet
  • HashMap

Without Iterator Pattern, client code would need to understand the internal structure of every collection.

Real World Example

TV Remote

When browsing

subtopics.name: Observer Pattern
subtopics.content:
Observer Pattern

The Observer Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that defines a one-to-many dependency between objects so that when one object changes state, all its dependent objects are automatically notified and updated.

It is commonly used in event-driven systems, messaging systems, UI frameworks, and distributed applications.

Why Do We Need Observer Pattern?

Many applications require multiple components to react whenever a particular event occurs.

Examples:

  • Stock Price Updates
  • Email Notifications
  • Order Status Updates
  • Social Media Followers
  • Event Publishing Systems
  • Microservice Events

Without Observer Pattern, the object generating the event must manually notify every dependent object.

Real World Example

YouTube Channel Subscription

Suppose you subscribe to a YouTube channel.

When the creator uploads a new video: 


Creator Uploads Video
         |
         v
    Subscribers
    /    |     \
User1 User2 User3

All subscribers automatically receive notifications.

The YouTube Channel is the Subject and Subscribers are Observers.

Problem Without Observer Pattern


public class StockMarket {

    private EmailService email;

    private SMSService sms;

    private MobileApp app;

    public void priceChanged() {

        email.notifyUser();

        sms.notifyUser();

        app.notifyUser();
    }
}

The StockMarket class is tightly coupled with all notification services.

Adding a new notification channel requires modifying existing code.

Observer Pattern Solution

Create a Subject that maintains a list of Observers.

Whenever state changes:

  1. Subject updates itself.
  2. Subject notifies all observers.
  3. Observers react independently.

Subject
   |
   +--> Observer 1
   |
   +--> Observer 2
   |
   +--> Observer 3

Structure of Observer Pattern


          Subject
             |
    -------------------
    |        |        |
Observer Observer Observer

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Subject Maintains observers
Observer Receives updates
Concrete Subject Stores actual state
Concrete Observer Handles notifications

Implementation

Step 1: Create Observer Interface


public interface Observer {

    void update(
        String message
    );
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Observer


public class EmailSubscriber
        implements Observer {

    @Override
    public void update(
            String message) {

        System.out.println(
            "Email Received: "
            + message
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Subject Interface


public interface Subject {

    void addObserver(
        Observer observer
    );

    void removeObserver(
        Observer observer
    );

    void notifyObservers();
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Subject


import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public class YouTubeChannel
        implements Subject {

    private List<Observer>
            observers =
            new ArrayList<>();

    private String videoTitle;

    @Override
    public void addObserver(
            Observer observer) {

        observers.add(observer);
    }

    @Override
    public void removeObserver(
            Observer observer) {

        observers.remove(observer);
    }

    @Override
    public void notifyObservers() {

        for(Observer observer
                : observers) {

            observer.update(
                videoTitle
            );
        }
    }

    public void uploadVideo(
            String title) {

        this.videoTitle = title;

        notifyObservers();
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


YouTubeChannel channel =
        new YouTubeChannel();

Observer user1 =
        new EmailSubscriber();

Observer user2 =
        new EmailSubscriber();

channel.addObserver(user1);

channel.addObserver(user2);

channel.uploadVideo(
        "Observer Pattern"
);

Output


Email Received:
Observer Pattern

Email Received:
Observer Pattern

How It Works

  1. Observers register with Subject.
  2. Subject maintains observer list.
  3. State changes occur.
  4. Subject notifies all observers.
  5. Observers react independently.

Class Diagram


                Subject
                   |
           ----------------
           |              |
      Observer      Observer
           |
    EmailSubscriber

Push vs Pull Model

Push Model

Subject sends complete data to observers. 


subject.notify(
    "Stock Price = 100"
);

Observer immediately receives required information.

Pull Model

Subject sends minimal information. 


observer.update();

Observer pulls required data from Subject.

Stock Market Example


Stock Price Updated
         |
         v
   Stock Market
         |
 -------------------
 |        |        |
Email    SMS    Mobile App

Whenever stock prices change, all subscribers receive updates automatically.

E-Commerce Example

Product Back In Stock Notification: 


Product Available
        |
        v
Customer 1

Customer 2

Customer 3

All interested customers receive notifications.

Microservices Example

Order Created Event: 


Order Service
      |
      v
Event Bus
      |
 -------------------
 |        |        |
Email  Payment  Inventory

Each service observes and reacts independently.

Observer Pattern in Spring

Spring Events


ApplicationEventPublisher

Publishes events to registered listeners.

Publisher


publisher.publishEvent(
    new OrderCreatedEvent()
);

Observer


@EventListener
public void handleEvent(
        OrderCreatedEvent event) {

    System.out.println(
        "Event Received"
    );
}

Spring's event mechanism is a direct implementation of Observer Pattern.

Observer vs Publish Subscribe

Observer Pub/Sub
Direct relationship Uses message broker
Usually in-process Distributed systems
Tighter coupling Looser coupling

Advantages

  • Loose coupling.
  • Easy to add new observers.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Automatic notifications.
  • Promotes event-driven architecture.

Disadvantages

  • Unexpected update chains.
  • Debugging can be difficult.
  • Performance issues with many observers.
  • Potential memory leaks if observers are not removed.

When to Use

  • Multiple objects need updates.
  • Event-driven systems.
  • Notification systems.
  • Real-time updates.
  • Publish-subscribe style communication.

When Not to Use

  • Only one dependent object exists.
  • Updates are infrequent.
  • Simple direct method calls are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Observer Pattern solve?

It allows automatic notification of multiple dependent objects when state changes.

What is the difference between Observer and Pub/Sub?

Observer usually works in-process, whereas Pub/Sub uses message brokers and distributed communication.

What is the most common Spring example?

ApplicationEventPublisher and @EventListener.

Which principle does Observer Pattern support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Observer is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Implements one-to-many dependency.
  • Subject notifies all observers automatically.
  • Widely used in event-driven architectures.
  • Spring Events use Observer Pattern.
  • Supports loose coupling.
  • Frequently asked in Java and Spring interviews.

subtopics.name: State Pattern
subtopics.content:
State Pattern

The State Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that allows an object to change its behavior when its internal state changes.

The object appears to change its class dynamically by delegating behavior to different state objects.

Why Do We Need State Pattern?

Many applications behave differently depending on their current state.

Without State Pattern, developers often use large if-else or switch statements. 


if(state.equals("NEW")) {

    // Logic A

}
else if(state.equals("PROCESSING")) {

    // Logic B

}
else if(state.equals("COMPLETED")) {

    // Logic C

}

As states grow, the code becomes difficult to maintain and extend.

Real World Example

Traffic Signal

A traffic signal behaves differently based on its state. 


RED
 |
 v
GREEN
 |
 v
YELLOW
 |
 v
RED

Each state has different behavior and transition rules.

Problem Without State Pattern

Consider an Order Management System. 


NEW

PROCESSING

SHIPPED

DELIVERED

CANCELLED

Without State Pattern: 


if(orderStatus.equals("NEW")) {

    processOrder();

}
else if(orderStatus.equals("SHIPPED")) {

    trackOrder();

}
else if(orderStatus.equals("DELIVERED")) {

    generateInvoice();

}

Adding new states requires modifying existing code.

State Pattern Solution

Move state-specific behavior into separate state classes. 


Context
   |
   +--> New State
   |
   +--> Processing State
   |
   +--> Shipped State

The Context delegates work to the current State object.

Structure of State Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Context
   |
   v
State Interface
   |
-----------------------
|          |          |
State1   State2    State3

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Context Maintains current state
State Defines behavior contract
Concrete State Implements state-specific behavior

Implementation

Step 1: Create State Interface


public interface OrderState {

    void handleOrder(
        OrderContext context
    );
}

Step 2: Create Context


public class OrderContext {

    private OrderState state;

    public void setState(
            OrderState state) {

        this.state = state;
    }

    public void process() {

        state.handleOrder(this);
    }
}

Step 3: Create Concrete States

New State


public class NewState
        implements OrderState {

    @Override
    public void handleOrder(
            OrderContext context) {

        System.out.println(
            "Order Processing"
        );

        context.setState(
            new ProcessingState()
        );
    }
}

Processing State


public class ProcessingState
        implements OrderState {

    @Override
    public void handleOrder(
            OrderContext context) {

        System.out.println(
            "Order Shipped"
        );

        context.setState(
            new ShippedState()
        );
    }
}

Shipped State


public class ShippedState
        implements OrderState {

    @Override
    public void handleOrder(
            OrderContext context) {

        System.out.println(
            "Order Delivered"
        );
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


OrderContext order =
        new OrderContext();

order.setState(
        new NewState()
);

order.process();

order.process();

order.process();

Output


Order Processing

Order Shipped

Order Delivered

How It Works

  1. Context maintains the current state.
  2. Client invokes operations on Context.
  3. Context delegates execution to current State.
  4. State performs behavior.
  5. State may transition Context to another State.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Context
                    |
                    v
                 State
                    ^
                    |
     --------------------------------
     |              |              |
 NewState   ProcessingState   ShippedState

Order Management Example


NEW
 |
 v
PROCESSING
 |
 v
SHIPPED
 |
 v
DELIVERED

Each state determines what actions are allowed.

ATM Machine Example

ATM behavior changes based on card insertion. 


No Card
   |
Insert Card
   |
Has Card
   |
Enter PIN
   |
Authenticated

Different operations become available in different states.

Vending Machine Example


No Coin
   |
Insert Coin
   |
Coin Inserted
   |
Select Product
   |
Dispense Product

The machine behaves differently depending on its current state.

Workflow Engine Example


Draft
  |
  v
Review
  |
  v
Approved
  |
  v
Published

State Pattern is widely used in workflow management systems.

State Pattern in Spring Boot

Order Processing System


NEW

PAID

PACKED

SHIPPED

DELIVERED

Each state can be represented by a separate service implementation.

Payment Processing


PENDING

SUCCESS

FAILED

REFUNDED

State Pattern helps manage complex payment lifecycles.

State vs Strategy

State Strategy
Represents state of object Represents algorithm
State changes automatically Client selects strategy
Behavior depends on current state Behavior depends on chosen algorithm

State vs Chain of Responsibility

State Chain of Responsibility
Single active state Multiple handlers
Behavior changes with state Request flows through chain

Advantages

  • Eliminates large if-else blocks.
  • Encapsulates state-specific behavior.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Makes state transitions explicit.

Disadvantages

  • Large number of state classes.
  • Increased design complexity.
  • Overkill for simple state machines.

When to Use

  • Object behavior depends on state.
  • Many state transitions exist.
  • Large if-else blocks are present.
  • Workflow-driven systems.
  • Finite State Machines (FSM).

When Not to Use

  • Only a few states exist.
  • State transitions are very simple.
  • Simple conditions are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does State Pattern solve?

It allows objects to change behavior when their internal state changes.

What is the difference between State and Strategy?

State changes automatically during execution, whereas Strategy is chosen by the client.

What is the most common real-world example?

Order Processing Workflow and Traffic Signals.

What principle does State Pattern support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • State is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Encapsulates state-specific behavior.
  • Eliminates complex if-else chains.
  • Context delegates work to current State.
  • State transitions can happen dynamically.
  • Widely used in workflows and state machines.
  • Common examples: Orders, ATMs, Traffic Signals, Payment Systems.

subtopics.name: Strategy Pattern
subtopics.content:
Strategy Pattern

The Strategy Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each algorithm in a separate class, and makes them interchangeable at runtime.

It allows the behavior of an object to be selected dynamically without modifying existing code.

Why Do We Need Strategy Pattern?

Applications often have multiple ways of performing the same task.

Examples:

  • Payment Methods
  • Sorting Algorithms
  • Compression Algorithms
  • Shipping Methods
  • Authentication Mechanisms

Without Strategy Pattern, developers typically use large if-else or switch statements. 


if(paymentType.equals("UPI")) {

    processUPI();

}
else if(paymentType.equals("CARD")) {

    processCard();

}
else if(paymentType.equals("NETBANKING")) {

    processNetBanking();

}

As new algorithms are added, the code becomes difficult to maintain.

Real World Example

Google Maps Navigation

Google Maps provides multiple route strategies.

  • Fastest Route
  • Shortest Route
  • Toll-Free Route
  • Bike Route

Google Maps
      |
      +--> Fastest Route
      |
      +--> Shortest Route
      |
      +--> Toll-Free Route

The user chooses a strategy and the application executes it.

Problem Without Strategy Pattern


public void pay(
        String paymentType) {

    if(paymentType.equals("UPI")) {

        processUPI();

    }
    else if(paymentType.equals("CARD")) {

        processCard();

    }
    else if(paymentType.equals("NETBANKING")) {

        processNetBanking();

    }
}

Adding a new payment method requires modifying existing code.

This violates the Open/Closed Principle.

Strategy Pattern Solution

Move each algorithm into a separate strategy class. 


Payment Strategy
      |
--------------------------
|           |            |
UPI       Card      NetBanking

The Context delegates execution to the selected strategy.

Structure of Strategy Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Context
   |
   v
Strategy
   |
-------------------------
|           |           |
S1         S2          S3

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Strategy Defines algorithm contract
Concrete Strategy Implements algorithm
Context Uses selected strategy
Client Chooses strategy

Implementation

Step 1: Create Strategy Interface


public interface PaymentStrategy {

    void pay(
        double amount
    );
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Strategies

UPI Payment


public class UpiPayment
        implements PaymentStrategy {

    @Override
    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        System.out.println(
            "Paid using UPI : "
            + amount
        );
    }
}

Card Payment


public class CardPayment
        implements PaymentStrategy {

    @Override
    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        System.out.println(
            "Paid using Card : "
            + amount
        );
    }
}

Net Banking Payment


public class NetBankingPayment
        implements PaymentStrategy {

    @Override
    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        System.out.println(
            "Paid using Net Banking : "
            + amount
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Context


public class PaymentService {

    private PaymentStrategy
            paymentStrategy;

    public void setStrategy(
            PaymentStrategy strategy) {

        this.paymentStrategy =
                strategy;
    }

    public void pay(
            double amount) {

        paymentStrategy.pay(
                amount);
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


PaymentService service =
        new PaymentService();

service.setStrategy(
        new UpiPayment()
);

service.pay(1000);

Output


Paid using UPI : 1000

How It Works

  1. Client selects a strategy.
  2. Context stores the strategy.
  3. Context delegates execution to strategy.
  4. Strategy performs the algorithm.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                 Context
                    |
                    v
                 Strategy
                    ^
                    |
    ---------------------------------
    |              |               |
  UPI         CardPayment    NetBanking

Payment Gateway Example


Checkout
   |
   +--> UPI
   |
   +--> Credit Card
   |
   +--> Wallet
   |
   +--> Net Banking

Different payment algorithms can be selected at runtime.

Sorting Algorithm Example


Sort Context
      |
---------------------
|         |         |
Quick    Merge    Bubble
Sort      Sort     Sort

The client chooses the most suitable sorting strategy.

Compression Example


File Compressor
      |
---------------------
|         |         |
ZIP      RAR       GZIP

Each compression technique becomes a separate strategy.

Strategy Pattern in Spring Boot

Multiple Service Implementations


public interface NotificationService {

    void send();
}


@Service
public class EmailNotificationService
        implements NotificationService {
}


@Service
public class SMSNotificationService
        implements NotificationService {
}

Different implementations represent different strategies.

Payment Gateway Integration


PaymentStrategy
      |
-------------------------
|           |           |
Razorpay   Stripe    PayPal

Spring applications frequently use Strategy Pattern for payment processing.

Strategy vs State

Strategy State
Represents an algorithm Represents a state
Selected by client Changes automatically
Focuses on behavior selection Focuses on state transitions

Strategy vs Command

Strategy Command
Encapsulates algorithm Encapsulates request
Chooses how work is done Represents work to be done

Advantages

  • Eliminates large if-else blocks.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Algorithms become reusable.
  • Easy to add new strategies.
  • Runtime algorithm selection.

Disadvantages

  • Increases number of classes.
  • Client must understand available strategies.
  • Can be overkill for simple logic.

When to Use

  • Multiple algorithms exist.
  • Runtime algorithm selection is needed.
  • Large if-else blocks exist.
  • Need extensible business logic.

When Not to Use

  • Only one algorithm exists.
  • Logic is very simple.
  • Additional abstraction adds complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Strategy Pattern solve?

It allows algorithms to be encapsulated and selected dynamically at runtime.

What is the difference between Strategy and State?

Strategy focuses on algorithm selection, while State focuses on behavior changes due to state transitions.

What is the most common real-world example?

Payment Gateway Integration.

Which SOLID principle does Strategy support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Strategy is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Encapsulates algorithms into separate classes.
  • Allows runtime algorithm selection.
  • Eliminates large if-else chains.
  • Widely used in payment gateways.
  • Frequently used in Spring Boot service implementations.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Template Method Pattern
subtopics.content:
Template Method Pattern

The Template Method Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class while allowing subclasses to override specific steps without changing the overall algorithm structure.

It promotes code reuse by placing common workflow logic in a parent class and customizable behavior in child classes.

Why Do We Need Template Method Pattern?

Many applications follow a fixed workflow where some steps are common and others vary.

Examples:

  • Payment Processing
  • Report Generation
  • Data Parsing
  • File Processing
  • Spring Framework Lifecycle

Without Template Method Pattern, duplicate workflow logic appears across multiple classes.

Real World Example

Making Tea and Coffee

Both tea and coffee preparation follow a similar process: 


Boil Water
      |
      v
Prepare Drink
      |
      v
Pour Into Cup
      |
      v
Add Ingredients

The overall algorithm remains the same.

Only the drink preparation and ingredients differ.

Problem Without Template Method Pattern


TeaMaker

boilWater()

addTea()

pourIntoCup()

addSugar()


CoffeeMaker

boilWater()

addCoffee()

pourIntoCup()

addMilk()

Common logic such as boiling water and pouring into a cup is duplicated.

Template Method Solution

Move common workflow into a base class and allow subclasses to customize specific steps. 


Beverage
    |
---------------------
|                   |
Tea              Coffee

Structure of Template Method Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Abstract Class
   |
---------------------
|                   |
Tea            Coffee

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Abstract Class Defines template method
Template Method Defines algorithm skeleton
Concrete Class Implements variable steps

Implementation

Step 1: Create Abstract Class


public abstract class Beverage {

    public final void prepareDrink() {

        boilWater();

        brew();

        pourIntoCup();

        addIngredients();
    }

    private void boilWater() {

        System.out.println(
            "Boiling Water"
        );
    }

    private void pourIntoCup() {

        System.out.println(
            "Pouring Into Cup"
        );
    }

    protected abstract void brew();

    protected abstract void addIngredients();
}

Step 2: Create Tea Class


public class Tea
        extends Beverage {

    @Override
    protected void brew() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Tea Leaves"
        );
    }

    @Override
    protected void addIngredients() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Sugar"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Coffee Class


public class Coffee
        extends Beverage {

    @Override
    protected void brew() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Coffee Powder"
        );
    }

    @Override
    protected void addIngredients() {

        System.out.println(
            "Adding Milk"
        );
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Beverage beverage =
        new Tea();

beverage.prepareDrink();

Output


Boiling Water

Adding Tea Leaves

Pouring Into Cup

Adding Sugar

How It Works

  1. Client invokes template method.
  2. Template method executes fixed workflow.
  3. Variable steps are delegated to subclasses.
  4. Subclasses provide custom implementations.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
                Beverage
                    |
      -------------------------
      |                       |
     Tea                   Coffee

Hook Methods

A Hook is an optional method that subclasses may override. 


protected boolean
customerWantsSugar() {

    return true;
}

Template methods can use hooks to customize workflow behavior.

Report Generation Example


Generate Report
       |
       v
Read Data
       |
       v
Process Data
       |
       v
Generate Output

Different reports can implement their own processing logic while following the same workflow.

File Parsing Example


Read File
     |
     v
Parse Data
     |
     v
Validate Data
     |
     v
Store Data

CSV, XML, and JSON parsers may override parsing logic while keeping the same workflow.

Template Method in Spring Framework

Spring heavily uses Template Method Pattern.

JdbcTemplate


jdbcTemplate.query(
    sql,
    rowMapper
);

JdbcTemplate handles:

  • Connection Creation
  • Statement Creation
  • Exception Handling
  • Resource Cleanup

Developers provide only query-specific logic.

RestTemplate


restTemplate.getForObject(
    url,
    User.class
);

RestTemplate manages:

  • HTTP Connections
  • Serialization
  • Error Handling
  • Response Processing

AbstractController

Spring MVC internally uses Template Method concepts in controller execution flow.

Template Method vs Strategy

Template Method Strategy
Uses inheritance Uses composition
Workflow fixed in parent Algorithm selected dynamically
Compile-time customization Runtime customization

Template Method vs Command

Template Method Command
Defines workflow Encapsulates request
Inheritance based Composition based

Advantages

  • Promotes code reuse.
  • Eliminates duplicate workflow code.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Provides consistent algorithm structure.
  • Easy to extend behavior.

Disadvantages

  • Relies heavily on inheritance.
  • Changes to base class affect all subclasses.
  • Can become difficult to maintain with deep hierarchies.

When to Use

  • Workflow is fixed.
  • Only a few steps vary.
  • Code duplication exists across classes.
  • Need reusable process templates.

When Not to Use

  • Workflow changes frequently.
  • Runtime behavior selection is required.
  • Composition-based solutions are preferred.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Template Method solve?

It defines a fixed algorithm structure while allowing subclasses to customize individual steps.

What is the most common Spring example?

JdbcTemplate and RestTemplate.

What is a Hook Method?

An optional method that subclasses may override to customize behavior.

What is the difference between Template Method and Strategy?

Template Method uses inheritance and fixed workflow, while Strategy uses composition and runtime algorithm selection.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Template Method is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Defines algorithm skeleton in a parent class.
  • Allows subclasses to customize specific steps.
  • Promotes code reuse.
  • Uses inheritance.
  • JdbcTemplate and RestTemplate are classic Spring examples.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Visitor Pattern
subtopics.content:
Visitor Pattern

The Visitor Pattern is a Behavioral Design Pattern that allows adding new operations to existing object structures without modifying those objects.

It separates algorithms from the objects on which they operate.

Why Do We Need Visitor Pattern?

Consider a system containing multiple object types. 


Circle

Rectangle

Triangle

Now suppose we need to perform different operations:

  • Calculate Area
  • Calculate Perimeter
  • Export to JSON
  • Export to XML
  • Generate Reports

Without Visitor Pattern, we would continuously modify these classes whenever a new operation is added.

This violates the Open/Closed Principle.

Real World Example

Tax Calculation System

Different item types exist:

  • Book
  • Electronics
  • Food

Different operations may be required:

  • Tax Calculation
  • Discount Calculation
  • Invoice Generation

Instead of modifying product classes, visitors perform these operations.

Problem Without Visitor Pattern


class Book {

    calculateTax();

    calculateDiscount();

    exportJson();

    exportXml();
}

As operations increase, classes become bloated.

Every new operation requires modifying all existing classes.

Visitor Pattern Solution

Move operations into separate Visitor classes. 


Elements
   |
   +--> Book
   |
   +--> Electronics
   |
   +--> Food

Visitors
   |
   +--> TaxVisitor
   |
   +--> DiscountVisitor
   |
   +--> InvoiceVisitor

New operations can be added without modifying existing element classes.

Structure of Visitor Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Visitor
   |
-------------------------
|           |           |
Book     Product    Food

Key Components

Component Responsibility
Visitor Defines operations
Concrete Visitor Implements operations
Element Accepts visitor
Concrete Element Specific object type

Implementation

Step 1: Create Visitor Interface


public interface Visitor {

    void visit(Book book);

    void visit(Electronics electronics);
}

Step 2: Create Element Interface


public interface Item {

    void accept(
        Visitor visitor
    );
}

Step 3: Create Concrete Elements

Book


public class Book
        implements Item {

    private double price;

    public Book(
            double price) {

        this.price = price;
    }

    public double getPrice() {

        return price;
    }

    @Override
    public void accept(
            Visitor visitor) {

        visitor.visit(this);
    }
}

Electronics


public class Electronics
        implements Item {

    private double price;

    public Electronics(
            double price) {

        this.price = price;
    }

    public double getPrice() {

        return price;
    }

    @Override
    public void accept(
            Visitor visitor) {

        visitor.visit(this);
    }
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Visitor


public class TaxVisitor
        implements Visitor {

    @Override
    public void visit(
            Book book) {

        System.out.println(
            "Book Tax : "
            + book.getPrice() * 0.05
        );
    }

    @Override
    public void visit(
            Electronics electronics) {

        System.out.println(
            "Electronics Tax : "
            + electronics.getPrice() * 0.18
        );
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


Item book =
    new Book(1000);

Item laptop =
    new Electronics(50000);

Visitor taxVisitor =
    new TaxVisitor();

book.accept(taxVisitor);

laptop.accept(taxVisitor);

Output


Book Tax : 50.0

Electronics Tax : 9000.0

How It Works

  1. Client creates Visitor.
  2. Client passes Visitor to Element.
  3. Element accepts Visitor.
  4. Visitor executes type-specific logic.
  5. Operation remains outside Element class.

Double Dispatch

Visitor Pattern uses Double Dispatch.

Method selection depends on:

  • Visitor Type
  • Element Type

book.accept(visitor);

visitor.visit(book);

Java resolves both types during execution.

Class Diagram


                 Visitor
                    ^
                    |
              TaxVisitor

                    ^
                    |
                  Item
                 /    \
              Book  Electronics

Compiler Example

Compilers frequently use Visitor Pattern. 


Syntax Tree
      |
      +--> Variable Node
      |
      +--> Function Node
      |
      +--> Expression Node

Visitors perform:

  • Code Generation
  • Optimization
  • Validation
  • Static Analysis

Report Generation Example


Employee

Manager

Department

Visitors can generate:

  • PDF Reports
  • Excel Reports
  • HTML Reports

Without changing the domain classes.

Visitor Pattern in Spring

Visitor Pattern is not directly visible in Spring APIs but appears internally in:

  • Bean Processing
  • AST Processing
  • Expression Parsing
  • Annotation Processing

Spring Expression Language (SpEL)

SpEL builds expression trees and visitors process nodes during evaluation. 


#{user.age > 18}

Hibernate Query Parsing

Hibernate traverses query trees using visitor-like implementations.

Visitor vs Strategy

Visitor Strategy
Adds operations Changes algorithms
Works on object structures Works on a single context
Uses double dispatch Uses composition

Visitor vs Command

Visitor Command
Adds operations to objects Encapsulates requests
Traverses object structures Executes actions

Advantages

  • Add new operations without modifying existing classes.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Separates business logic from object structure.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Useful for tree-like structures.

Disadvantages

  • Difficult to add new element types.
  • Large number of visitor methods.
  • Can increase complexity.
  • Requires understanding of double dispatch.

When to Use

  • Object structure is stable.
  • Operations change frequently.
  • Need multiple unrelated operations.
  • Tree structures exist.
  • Compiler-like systems.

When Not to Use

  • New element types are added frequently.
  • Object structure changes regularly.
  • Simple method calls are sufficient.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Visitor Pattern solve?

It allows adding new operations without modifying existing object classes.

What is Double Dispatch?

Method selection depends on both the Visitor type and Element type.

What is the most common use case?

Compilers, AST Processing, and Report Generation.

Which principle does Visitor support?

Open/Closed Principle.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Visitor is a Behavioral Design Pattern.
  • Adds operations without modifying existing classes.
  • Uses Visitor and Element hierarchy.
  • Relies on Double Dispatch.
  • Common in Compilers and AST Processing.
  • Works best when object structure is stable.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: DNS Overview
subtopics.content: The Domain Name System (DNS) is a hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses, enabling browsers to locate and communicate with web servers.

subtopics.name: DNS Resolver
subtopics.content: A DNS Resolver, also known as a Recursive Resolver, receives DNS queries from clients and performs the lookup process on their behalf. It communicates with Root Servers, TLD Servers, and Authoritative Name Servers to resolve a domain name into an IP address.

subtopics.name: Root DNS Server
subtopics.content: Root DNS Servers are the first step in DNS resolution. They do not store the IP address of a domain but direct the DNS Resolver to the appropriate Top-Level Domain (TLD) server based on the domain extension such as .com, .org, or .net.

subtopics.name: Top-Level Domain (TLD) Server
subtopics.content: TLD Servers manage information about domains within a specific top-level domain, such as .com or .org. They provide the DNS Resolver with the address of the Authoritative Name Server responsible for the requested domain.

subtopics.name: Authoritative Name Server
subtopics.content: An Authoritative Name Server stores the actual DNS records for a domain. When queried, it returns the requested information, such as the IP address associated with a domain name.

subtopics.name: DNS Resolution Process
subtopics.content: When a user enters a domain name such as telusko.com, the DNS Resolver first queries a Root DNS Server. The Root Server responds with the location of the appropriate TLD Server (for example, .com). The Resolver then queries the TLD Server, which returns the address of the Authoritative Name Server. Finally, the Resolver queries the Authoritative Name Server and receives the domain's IP address, which is returned to the user's browser.

subtopics.name: DNS Caching
subtopics.content: DNS Caching stores previously resolved domain names and their IP addresses for a specified Time To Live (TTL). This reduces lookup time and minimizes the number of requests sent to DNS servers.

subtopics.name: DNS Records
subtopics.content: DNS Records are entries stored in DNS servers that provide information about a domain. Common record types include A Records (IPv4 addresses), AAAA Records (IPv6 addresses), CNAME Records (aliases), MX Records (mail servers), and TXT Records (text-based information).

subtopics.name: What is Caching?
subtopics.content: Caching is a technique used to store frequently accessed data temporarily so that future requests can be served faster. Think of it like keeping your favorite snacks close by instead of going to the store every time.

subtopics.name: Why Caching is Important
subtopics.content: Caching improves performance and reduces load on databases or servers. By reusing stored data, applications respond faster and handle more users efficiently.

subtopics.name: Types of Caching
subtopics.content:
There are several types of caching:

  • Memory Cache: Data stored in RAM for quick access.
  • Disk Cache: Data stored on disk to persist between sessions.
  • Browser Cache: Stores web content locally to speed up page loading.
  • Application Cache: Used by software applications to store computation results.

subtopics.name: Cache Expiration and TTL
subtopics.content: Cached data is not permanent. Each item has a Time To Live (TTL) or expiration time. Once expired, the cache must fetch fresh data from the source.

subtopics.name: Cache Invalidation
subtopics.content: Cache invalidation is the process of removing outdated or incorrect data from the cache. It ensures users always get the most accurate and up-to-date information.

subtopics.name: Cache Best Practices
subtopics.content:
Some best practices include:

  • Only cache frequently accessed data.
  • Set appropriate TTL values to balance freshness and performance.
  • Use distributed caches for large applications.
  • Monitor cache hit/miss rates to optimize performance.

subtopics.name: Real-World Examples
subtopics.content: Examples of caching include web browser cache for images, CDNs caching static website content, Redis or Memcached storing session data, and CPU cache storing instructions for faster processing.

subtopics.name: Read Through Cache (RTC)
subtopics.content: Read Through Cache is a caching strategy where the cache sits between the application and the database. The application always queries the cache first. If the data is not present, the cache itself fetches it from the database, stores it, and returns it to the application.

subtopics.name: Cache Hit in Read Through Cache
subtopics.content:
In a cache hit scenario, the requested data is already present in the cache. The cache directly returns the data to the application without contacting the database, resulting in very fast response times.

  • Application → Cache → Response

subtopics.name: Cache Miss in Read Through Cache
subtopics.content:
In a cache miss scenario, the cache does not contain the requested data. The cache fetches the data from the database, stores it internally, and then returns it to the application.

  • Application → Cache → Database → Cache → Application

subtopics.name: Write Through Cache (WTC)
subtopics.content: Write Through Cache is a strategy where every write operation is first written to the cache and then immediately written to the database. This ensures strong consistency between cache and database.

subtopics.name: Write Through Cache Flow
subtopics.content:
In Write Through Cache, when data is updated, it is written simultaneously to both cache and database. This ensures the cache always stays in sync with the database but may introduce higher write latency.

  • Client → Backend → Cache → Database

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache (Cache Aside Pattern)
subtopics.content: Write Aside Cache is a strategy where the application writes directly to the database and then updates or invalidates the cache separately. It is the most commonly used caching pattern in real-world systems.

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache Read Flow
subtopics.content:
On a cache hit, data is returned directly from cache. On a cache miss, the application fetches data from the database, stores it in cache, and returns it to the user.

  • Cache Hit: Client → Backend → Cache → Response
  • Cache Miss: Client → Backend → Cache → DB → Cache → Response

subtopics.name: Write Aside Cache Write Flow
subtopics.content:
In Write Aside Cache, write operations update the database first. After a successful update, the cache is either invalidated or refreshed to avoid stale data.

  • Client → Backend → Database → Cache Invalidate

subtopics.name: Comparison of RTC, WTC, and WAC
subtopics.content:
• Read Through Cache: Cache handles DB fetch on miss.
  • Write Through Cache: Writes go to cache and DB simultaneously.
  • Write Aside Cache: App writes to DB first, cache updated later.

Write Aside Cache is the most widely used in modern backend systems due to its simplicity and scalability.

subtopics.name: When to Use Each Cache Strategy
subtopics.content:
• RTC: When you want cache-managed data loading.
  • WTC: When strong consistency is required.
  • WAC: When read-heavy systems need scalability and flexibility.

subtopics.name: Common  Questions
subtopics.content:
1. What is Read Through Cache?
  2. What is Write Through Cache?
  3. What is Write Aside Cache?
  4. Difference between RTC and WAC?
  5. Difference between WTC and WAC?
  6. Which caching strategy is most used in production?
  7. How does cache invalidation work in WAC?
  8. What are the risks of cache stampede?
  9. When would you avoid Write Through Cache?
  10. How do these caching strategies affect system performance?

subtopics.name: Least Recently Used (LRU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the item that has not been used for the longest time. Good for workloads where recently used items are likely to be reused.

subtopics.name: Least Frequently Used (LFU)
subtopics.content: Evicts the item that has been used the fewest times. Useful when some items are accessed more frequently than others.

subtopics.name: First In, First Out (FIFO)
